topics.title: Basic Structure of Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Structure of Computers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer Organization and Architecture (COA)** is the study of the internal structure, functional behaviour, and design principles of computer systems. According to the standard KTU 2024 syllabus definition:

> **Computer Architecture** refers to the attributes of a system as seen by the programmer — i.e., the conceptual structure and functional behaviour (Instruction Set Architecture, addressing modes, data types, I/O mechanisms).

> **Computer Organization** refers to the operational units and their interconnections that realize the architectural specifications (control signals, interfaces, memory technology, bus structures).

In simple KTU board-exam language:
**Architecture = What the system does (programmer-visible attributes)**
**Organization = How it is implemented (hardware-visible attributes)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Line (Dec 2019, Jun 2022):**
> *"Architecture is the set of attributes that are visible to the programmer; Organization is the transparent implementation of these attributes."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Blueprint vs. The Construction

Imagine you want to construct a hospital:
- **Architecture (Blueprint):** Defines how many floors, room layout, number of operation theatres, entrance design, fire exits. These are *user-observable* specifications.
- **Organization (Construction):** Decides whether the walls are made of brick or concrete, whether wiring is copper or aluminium, whether lifts are hydraulic or electric. These are *internal, transparent* decisions.

Similarly, in a computer:
- The **architecture** tells us that there is a `MUL` instruction, registers are 32-bit, memory is byte-addressable.
- The **organization** decides whether multiplication is done by a dedicated hardware unit or by repeated addition using the ALU, whether cache is 256 KB or 512 KB.

> [!NOTE]
> The **same architecture** can have **multiple organizations**. For example, the Intel x86 architecture has been implemented with different organizations (pipelined, superscalar, multi-core) over decades.

### 1.3 The Three Fundamental Functional Units of a Computer

Every digital computer — from a smartwatch chip to a supercomputer — is built around **three basic functional units**, originally proposed by **John von Neumann (1945)**:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — The "brain" that executes instructions. It contains:
   - **Control Unit (CU):** Directs all operations.
   - **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs computations.
   - **Register File:** Small, ultra-fast on-chip memory.

2. **Memory Unit (Primary Storage)** — Stores programs and data currently in use. Organized as a linear array of words/bytes, each with a unique address.

3. **Input/Output (I/O) Subsystem** — Interfaces the computer with the outside world (keyboard, display, disk, network).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A computer is a programmable, electronic device that can store, retrieve, and process data under the control of a stored program, consisting of three functional units: CPU, Memory, and I/O, interconnected via system buses."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Central Processing Unit (CPU)

The CPU is the core of every computer. It executes machine instructions fetched from memory. Internally, it is divided into:

- **Control Unit (CU):**
  - Generates **timing and control signals** for all other units.
  - Uses a **Program Counter (PC)** to track the next instruction.
  - Uses an **Instruction Register (IR)** to hold the currently executing instruction.
  - Contains the **decoder** that interprets opcode bits.

- **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):**
  - Performs all arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`) and logical (`AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, shift) operations.
  - Operands are taken from registers; results are stored back in registers.
  - Sets **flag bits** (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow, Parity) in a status register.

- **Register File:**
  - Set of high-speed storage cells inside the CPU.
  - Typical registers: **PC (Program Counter), IR (Instruction Register), MAR (Memory Address Register), MDR (Memory Data Register), AC/ACC (Accumulator), SP (Stack Pointer), GPR (General Purpose Registers)**.

### 2.2 Memory Unit — Hierarchical Organization

Modern computers use a **memory hierarchy** to balance speed, cost, and capacity:

```
        Registers  (1 cycle, < 1 KB, $$$)
            |
          Cache  (L1/L2/L3, ~10 cycles, MB, $$)
            |
        Main RAM  (~100 cycles, GB, $)
            |
        Secondary Storage  (Disk/SSD, ms, TB, ¢)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question (Jul 2021):** *Explain the memory hierarchy. Why is it necessary?*
> **Model Answer:** Memory hierarchy is necessary because faster memory is expensive and smaller in capacity, while cheaper memory is slower. By organizing memory in tiers, we achieve an optimal balance — the user perceives near-cache speed at near-disk cost.

### 2.3 System Buses — The Communication Backbone

All three functional units communicate through a **system bus**, which is a set of conducting wires carrying information. There are **three types of buses**:

1. **Data Bus:** Carries the actual data. **Bidirectional** in nature. Width typically 8, 16, 32, 64 bits.
2. **Address Bus:** Carries the memory/I/O address. **Unidirectional** (CPU → Memory/I/O). Width determines maximum addressable memory: $2^n$ locations for an $n$-bit bus.
3. **Control Bus:** Carries timing and control signals — `RD`, `WR`, `MREQ`, `IORQ`, `CLK`, `RESET`.

> [!NOTE]
> A system with **16-bit address bus** can address $2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64\text{ KB}$ memory locations.
> A **32-bit address bus** → $2^{32} = 4\text{ GB}$ addressable space.

### 2.4 The Stored Program Concept (von Neumann Architecture)

The revolutionary idea introduced by **John von Neumann** in 1945:
> *"Both instructions and data are stored in the same memory, and the CPU fetches instructions sequentially from memory using the Program Counter."*

**Advantages:**
- Programs can be modified just like data (self-modifying code, dynamic loading).
- Simplifies hardware — same bus serves both.

**Disadvantage — The "von Neumann Bottleneck":**
The single bus connecting CPU and memory limits throughput, since at any instant the bus can carry either an instruction OR a data word.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description / Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Addressable Memory = $2^n$ bytes | $n$ = number of address lines | Direct memory sizing problems |
| 2 | Memory Capacity = $2^n \times \text{word\_size}$ | bytes, KB, MB, GB | Capacity calculations |
| 3 | Data Bus Width = word size in bits | 8/16/32/64 bits | Bus design problems |
| 4 | CPU Performance Equation: $T = \dfrac{N \times C}{f}$ | $T$ = execution time, $N$ = instruction count, $C$ = CPI, $f$ = clock frequency | Performance estimation |
| 5 | MIPS = $\dfrac{f}{C \times 10^6}$ | Millions of Instructions Per Second | Speed comparison |
| 6 | MFLOPS = $\dfrac{\text{Floating-point ops}}{T \times 10^6}$ | Floating-point throughput | HPC benchmarks |
| 7 | Amdahl's Law: $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | $S$ = speedup, $f$ = parallel fraction, $k$ = parallel processors | Parallel performance |
| 8 | Clock Period $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | seconds | Timing analysis |
| 9 | CPU Time = Instruction Count $\times$ CPI $\times$ Clock Cycle Time | seconds | Performance optimization |
| 10 | Throughput = $\dfrac{1}{\text{Latency per task}}$ | tasks / second | Pipeline throughput |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes (`|`) are used in the table above** — all absolute values are expressed using $\vert$ notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Processor Design (Intel, AMD, ARM):** Performance engineers use these formulas daily to balance clock speed, IPC (Instructions Per Cycle), and core counts.
- **Embedded Systems (Automotive ECUs, IoT):** Memory hierarchy and bus width determine chip cost and power consumption.
- **Data Centers (Google, AWS):** Amdahl's Law guides the choice between scaling cores vs. increasing single-thread frequency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width

**Problem:** A processor has a **20-bit address bus** and an **8-bit data bus**. Calculate the maximum addressable memory.

**Step 1 — Identify what each bus does:**
The address bus width determines how many unique memory locations can be referenced. The data bus width determines how many bits are transferred in one memory access.

**Step 2 — Number of unique locations:**
For an $n$-bit address bus, the number of unique binary addresses possible is:

$$
N_{\text{locations}} = 2^n
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $n = 20$:**

$$
N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{20}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power of 2:**

$$
2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by bytes per location (data bus width = 8 bits = 1 byte):**

$$
\text{Capacity} = N_{\text{locations}} \times 1\ \text{byte} = 1{,}048{,}576\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to KB:**

$$
\text{Capacity} = \dfrac{1{,}048{,}576}{1024} = 1024\ \text{KB} = 1\ \text{MB}
$$

**Final Answer:** The processor can address a maximum of **1 MB** of memory.

> [!NOTE]
> Common KTU values to memorize:
> - 16-bit → 64 KB
> - 20-bit → 1 MB
> - 24-bit → 16 MB
> - 32-bit → 4 GB
> - 64-bit → 16 EB (Exabytes)

### 3.2 Derivation: CPU Execution Time using the Performance Equation

**Problem:** A program contains 100,000 instructions. The CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) is 2.5 on average. The processor clock frequency is 400 MHz. Calculate the total CPU execution time.

**Step 1 — Recall the CPU Performance Equation:**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{N \times C}{f}
$$

where:
- $N$ = number of instructions executed
- $C$ = average CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)
- $f$ = clock frequency

**Step 2 — Identify given values:**

$$
N = 100{,}000,\quad C = 2.5,\quad f = 400 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{100{,}000 \times 2.5}{400 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
100{,}000 \times 2.5 = 250{,}000\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by frequency:**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{250{,}000}{400 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{5}}{4 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 0.625 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to microseconds:**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 625 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 625\ \mu s
$$

**Final Answer:** The CPU takes **625 microseconds** to execute the program.

### 3.3 Derivation: MIPS Rating Calculation

**Problem:** For the same processor in Section 3.2, compute the MIPS rating.

**Step 1 — Recall the MIPS formula:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{C \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $f = 400\ \text{MHz}$, $C = 2.5$:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{400 \times 10^{6}}{2.5 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $10^6$:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{400}{2.5}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = 160
$$

**Final Answer:** The processor has a **MIPS rating of 160**.

### 3.4 Derivation: Amdahl's Law — Speedup from Parallelism

**Problem:** A program spends **80%** of its time in parallelizable code. We add **4 processors** to speed up this portion. Calculate the overall speedup.

**Step 1 — Recall Amdahl's Law:**

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}
$$

where:
- $S$ = total speedup
- $f$ = parallel fraction = 0.80
- $k$ = number of processors = 4
- $(1 - f)$ = serial fraction = 0.20

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{4}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term in the denominator:**

$$
1 - 0.80 = 0.20
$$

$$
\dfrac{0.80}{4} = 0.20
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the denominator:**

$$
0.20 + 0.20 = 0.40
$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.40} = 2.5
$$

**Final Answer:** The overall speedup is **2.5×**.

> [!NOTE]
> Even with 4 processors, the speedup is only 2.5× because of the 20% serial portion. Adding **infinite** processors would cap speedup at $1 / 0.20 = 5\times$. This is the **fundamental limit** of Amdahl's Law.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Performance Optimization Strategies

| Optimization Target | Affects Which Parameter | Typical Improvement |
|---|---|---|
| Reduce instruction count (better compiler) | $N$ | 20–40% |
| Reduce CPI (pipeline, faster ALU) | $C$ | 2–5× |
| Increase clock frequency | $f$ | Proportional |
| Parallelism (multi-core) | $f$ portion only | Limited by Amdahl |
| Cache optimization | $C$ (memory access penalty) | 2–10× effective |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Basic Computer System (von Neumann Model)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a basic stored-program computer, showing all major units, registers, and the system bus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[Central Processing Unit]
    CU[Control Unit]
    ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    REG[Register File]
    PC[Program Counter PC]
    IR[Instruction Register IR]
    MAR[Memory Address Register MAR]
    MDR[Memory Data Register MDR]
    MEM[Primary Memory RAM/ROM]
    IO[Input Output Devices]
    KBD[Keyboard]
    MON[Monitor]
    DISK[Secondary Storage Disk]
    DBUS[(Data Bus)]
    ABUS[(Address Bus)]
    CBUS[(Control Bus)]

    CPU --> CU
    CPU --> ALU
    CPU --> REG
    REG --> PC
    REG --> IR
    REG --> MAR
    REG --> MDR
    MAR --> ABUS
    MDR --> DBUS
    CU --> CBUS
    DBUS <--> MEM
    ABUS <--> MEM
    CBUS <--> MEM
    DBUS <--> IO
    ABUS <--> IO
    CBUS <--> IO
    IO --> KBD
    IO --> MON
    IO --> DISK

    classDef bus fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef unit fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#abebc6,stroke:#0e6655,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class DBUS,ABUS,CBUS bus
    class CPU,MEM,IO,CU,ALU unit
    class REG,PC,IR,MAR,MDR reg
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read the Diagram:**
> - The **double-arrow connections** to Memory and I/O indicate **bidirectional** data flow.
> - The **MAR** drives the **Address Bus** (unidirectional).
> - The **CU** drives the **Control Bus** (unidirectional).
> - All three buses are *shared* by CPU, Memory, and I/O.

### 4.2 Internal CPU Architecture — Bus Organization within CPU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU["CPU CHIP"]
        ABUS_INT((Internal Address Bus))
        DBUS_INT((Internal Data Bus))
        CBUS_INT((Control Lines))

        REGFILE[Register File]
        ALU_INT[ALU]
        CU_INT[Control Unit]

        REGFILE <--> ABUS_INT
        REGFILE <--> DBUS_INT
        ALU_INT <--> DBUS_INT
        CU_INT --> CBUS_INT
        CU_INT --> REGFILE
        CU_INT --> ALU_INT

        ABUS_INT --> ABUS_EXT((Address Bus))
        DBUS_INT --> DBUS_EXT((Data Bus))
        CBUS_INT --> CBUS_EXT((Control Bus))
    end

    classDef internal fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#212f3d,stroke-width:1px
    classDef external fill:#f5b041,stroke:#7e5109,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cpu fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#1a5276,stroke-width:2px

    class ABUS_INT,DBUS_INT,CBUS_INT internal
    class ABUS_EXT,DBUS_EXT,CBUS_EXT external
    class CPU,REGFILE,ALU_INT,CU_INT cpu
```

### 4.3 Memory Hierarchy — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REG_H[Registers Level 0 - 1 cycle - few KB]
    L1[L1 Cache - few cycles - tens of KB]
    L2[L2 Cache - tens of cycles - hundreds of KB]
    L3[L3 Cache - shared - tens of MB]
    RAM[Main Memory RAM - hundreds of cycles - GB]
    SSD[SSD - microseconds - TB]
    HDD[HDD Disk - milliseconds - TB]

    REG_H -->|miss| L1
    L1 -->|miss| L2
    L2 -->|miss| L3
    L3 -->|miss| RAM
    RAM -->|page fault| SSD
    SSD -->|spillover| HDD

    classDef fast fill:#abebc6,stroke:#0e6655,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    classDef slow fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px

    class REG_H fast
    class L1,L2,L3 mid
    class RAM,SSD,HDD slow
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always draw the hierarchy as a *pyramid* (or in Mermaid, a top-down flowchart with fast at top, slow at bottom). Mention **locality of reference** (temporal + spatial) when explaining why the hierarchy works.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Computer Architecture and Computer Organization with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Computer Architecture | Computer Organization |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Programmer-visible attributes | Hardware-transparent implementation |
| Concern | What the system does | How it is implemented |
| Examples | Instruction set, addressing modes, data types, I/O mechanisms | Control signals, memory technology, bus width, pipelining |
| Change | Requires software recompilation | Does not affect software |
| Example | "There is a MUL instruction" | "MUL is implemented by a dedicated hardware multiplier" |

The same architecture can have multiple organizations. *(1 mark for the difference statement, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for the relationship.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2022]**
*Explain the stored program concept proposed by John von Neumann. What is the von Neumann bottleneck?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **stored program concept**, proposed by **John von Neumann in 1945**, states that:
- Both **instructions and data** are stored in the **same memory** (linear array of addresses).
- The CPU fetches instructions **sequentially** using a **Program Counter**.
- Instructions can be manipulated as data, enabling self-modifying code and dynamic loading.

**Von Neumann Bottleneck:**
- Since a **single bus** connects CPU and memory, only **one entity** (either an instruction or a data word) can be transferred at a time.
- This limits the throughput of the system — the CPU often **waits** for memory.
- Modern solutions: **Harvard architecture** (separate instruction/data memories), **caches**, and **pipelining**.

*(1 mark stored-program idea, 1 mark execution flow, 1 mark bottleneck explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

(a) Draw the block diagram of a basic computer system and explain the functions of **CPU, Memory, and I/O units**. (7 marks)

(b) With a neat diagram, explain the **three types of system buses** and their roles. A processor has a **24-bit address bus** and a **16-bit data bus**. Compute the maximum addressable memory. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Functional Units:**

```
                   +--------------------------------+
                   |          C P U                 |
                   |  +-------+    +-------+        |
                   |  |  CU   |    |  ALU  |        |
                   |  +-------+    +-------+        |
                   |        +---------+              |
                   |        | Registers|             |
                   |        +---------+              |
                   +----------------+----------------+
                                    |
        +---------------------------+---------------------------+
        |                           |                           |
   +----+-----+                +-----+----+               +------+-----+
   |  Memory  |                |    I/O   |               |   I/O    |
   |   Unit   |                | Devices  |               | Devices  |
   +----------+                +----------+               +----------+
        ^                           ^                           ^
   (Keyboard,                  (Monitor,                   (Disk,
    RAM, ROM)                   Printer)                    Network)
```

**Functions:**
- **CPU:** Executes instructions via CU (control) and ALU (computation). Registers provide high-speed scratchpad storage.
- **Memory:** Stores active programs and data. Provides fast read/write access.
- **I/O:** Provides the interface between the computer and external devices.

*(2 marks diagram, 3 marks explanation, 2 marks register-level detail.)*

**Part (b) — System Buses and Memory Calculation:**

**Three Types of Buses:**

1. **Data Bus:** Carries actual data being transferred. **Bidirectional**. Width = word size. Here = 16 bits.
2. **Address Bus:** Carries memory/I/O addresses. **Unidirectional** (CPU → Memory/I/O). Width = 24 bits here.
3. **Control Bus:** Carries timing/control signals (`RD`, `WR`, `CLK`, `RESET`).

**Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Number of unique addresses:**

$$
N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216\ \text{locations}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by bytes per location (16 bits = 2 bytes):**

$$
\text{Capacity} = 2^{24} \times 2\ \text{bytes} = 2^{25}\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MB:**

$$
2^{25} = 2^{5} \times 2^{20} = 32 \times 1\text{MB} = 32\ \text{MB}
$$

**Final Answer:** Maximum addressable memory = **32 MB**.

*(2 marks bus diagram, 3 marks role explanation, 2 marks numerical calculation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

(a) Explain the **CPU performance equation** in detail. A processor runs at **2 GHz**, with an average CPI of **1.5**, executing **10 million instructions**. Calculate (i) CPU execution time, (ii) MIPS rating. (7 marks)

(b) Explain **Amdahl's Law** with its significance. A program spends **75%** of its execution time on a parallelizable section. We run it on **8 processors**. Calculate the speedup. What is the maximum theoretical speedup if we add infinitely many processors? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CPU Performance Equation:**

**Equation Derivation:**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{N \times C}{f}
$$

where $N$ = instruction count, $C$ = CPI, $f$ = clock frequency.

This represents total clock cycles ($N \times C$) divided by cycles per second.

**Given:** $f = 2\text{ GHz} = 2 \times 10^9\ \text{Hz}$, $C = 1.5$, $N = 10^7$.

**Step 1 — Compute CPU execution time:**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{10^7 \times 1.5}{2 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.75 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 7.5\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MIPS rating:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{C \times 10^6} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^9}{1.5 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{2000}{1.5} = 1333.33\ \text{MIPS}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{\text{CPU}} = 7.5\ \text{ms}$, MIPS = **1333.33**.

*(2 marks derivation, 3 marks substitution, 2 marks final numerical answer.)*

**Part (b) — Amdahl's Law:**

**Statement:** Amdahl's Law states that the maximum speedup achievable by improving a portion $f$ of a system is limited by the un-improved portion $(1-f)$.

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}
$$

**Given:** $f = 0.75$, $k = 8$.

**Step 1 — Substitute:**

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.75) + \dfrac{0.75}{8}} = \dfrac{1}{0.25 + 0.09375}
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.34375} \approx 2.909
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum speedup with infinite processors ($k \to \infty$):**

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{k \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f} = \dfrac{1}{0.25} = 4
$$

**Final Answer:** Speedup with 8 processors ≈ **2.91×**, Maximum theoretical speedup = **4×**.

**Significance:** Amdahl's Law demonstrates that **parallelism alone cannot achieve unlimited speedup**. The serial portion sets a hard ceiling. Hence, modern architectures focus on both **parallelism and serial performance**.

*(2 marks law statement, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks significance and maximum speedup.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing data bus with address bus** while calculating memory. Data bus = word size in bits; Address bus = number of locations. *1 mark deduction each.*
> 2. **Forgetting to convert** MHz/GHz to Hz, or microseconds to seconds. Always write units.
> 3. **Confusing MIPS with MFLOPS** — MIPS counts integer instructions only; MFLOPS is for floating-point.
> 4. **Amdahl's Law numerical mistake** — students forget to include the serial fraction $(1-f)$ in the denominator.
> 5. **Not drawing bus arrows** as bidirectional for data bus, unidirectional for address bus — examiners expect this clarity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basic Structure of Computers**

- ✅ **Three functional units**: CPU, Memory, I/O — connected via **system bus**.
- ✅ **CPU = CU + ALU + Register File**.
- ✅ **Registers inside CPU**: PC, IR, MAR, MDR, AC, SP, GPR.
- ✅ **Architecture** = programmer-visible; **Organization** = hardware-transparent.
- ✅ **Stored Program Concept (von Neumann, 1945)** — instructions and data in same memory.
- ✅ **von Neumann Bottleneck** — single shared bus limits throughput.
- ✅ **Three Buses**: Data (bidirectional), Address (unidirectional, CPU→Memory), Control (timing/control signals).
- ✅ **Addressable Memory** = $2^n \times (\text{data bus width in bytes})$.
- ✅ **Memory Hierarchy**: Registers → Cache → RAM → SSD → HDD (speed decreases, capacity increases, cost decreases).
- ✅ **CPU Performance Equation**: $T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{N \times C}{f}$.
- ✅ **MIPS** = $\dfrac{f}{C \times 10^6}$ — measures integer throughput.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/k}$ — serial portion limits maximum speedup.
- ✅ **Clock Period** $T_{clk} = 1/f$.
- ✅ **Harvard Architecture** (separate instruction/data memories) avoids von Neumann bottleneck.
- ✅ **Locality of Reference** (temporal + spatial) is the *reason* memory hierarchy works.
- ✅ Common address bus widths to memorize: **16-bit → 64 KB**, **20-bit → 1 MB**, **24-bit → 16 MB**, **32-bit → 4 GB**, **64-bit → 16 EB**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Functional Units ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Functional Units** are the fundamental, **logically distinct hardware subsystems** that together implement the stored-program concept of a digital computer. Each unit performs a well-defined role in the **Instruction Execution Cycle (IEC)** and communicates with the others via **shared buses** and **control signals**. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1), every general-purpose computer is constructed from **five canonical functional units**: the **Input Unit**, **Memory Unit (Primary Storage)**, the **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)**, the **Output Unit**, and the **Control Unit (CU)**. The processor is formed by tightly coupling the ALU, the Control Unit, and a small set of **high-speed on-chip registers** collectively called the **Register File**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Computer as a Restaurant Kitchen To understand why a computer is *not* one monolithic block, imagine a busy restaurant kitchen: - The **waiter taking your order** = **Input Unit** (Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner). It translates human intent into machine-readable signals. - The **refrigerator and pantry shelves** = **Memory Unit (RAM / Registers)**. Ingredients (data and instructions) are stored here, readily retrievable. - The **chef at the stove** = **ALU**. This is where the actual "cooking" — arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷), logic (AND, OR, NOT), and shift/compare operations — is performed. - The **plated dish served to the customer** = **Output Unit** (Monitor, Printer, Speaker). The result is presented in a form humans can perceive. - The **head chef shouting "chop! boil! plate!"** = **Control Unit**. It does not cook. It only orchestrates *when* each sub-unit acts, fetching the recipe (instruction) and dispatching control signals. Just as you cannot run a restaurant without a head chef coordinating the line, the Control Unit is the *conductor* of the entire system. ### 1.3 The Five Functional Units — Quick Map > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 expects students to draw the block diagram, label all **three bus structures** (Data, Address, Control), and briefly state the function of each block. Memorize the acronym **"I-M-A-O-C"** (Input, Memory, ALU, Output, Control) for rapid recall. | S.No | Functional Unit | Primary Role | Real-World Equivalent | |------|-----------------|--------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Input Unit** | Accepts raw data/program from the external environment and converts it to binary | Keyboard / Mouse / Microphone | | 2 | **Memory Unit** | Stores both **data** and **instructions**; provides fast random access | RAM chips, Cache, Registers | | 3 | **ALU** | Performs all computational and logical operations | The "calculator brain" of the CPU | | 4 | **Output Unit** | Returns processed results to the outside world in human-perceivable form | Monitor / Printer / Speaker | | 5 | **Control Unit** | Fetches, decodes instructions and generates timing/control signals | The "traffic policeman" inside the CPU | ### 1.4 Stored Program Concept (von Neumann Bottleneck) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **stored-program concept**, attributed to **John von Neumann (1945)**, states that **instructions and data reside in the *same* memory** and are indistinguishable at the hardware level. This is why the **Program Counter (PC)** can increment through data bytes — a famous security flaw (the *von Neumann bottleneck*). Modern CPUs mitigate this using **Harvard architecture** (separate instruction and data caches) at the on-chip level while retaining von Neumann semantics at the programmer's model level. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Operational Breakdown of Each Unit #### 2.1.1 Input Unit - **Function:** Convert external stimuli (key press, mouse motion, network packet) into a standardized binary representation. - **Key Component:** An **Input Interface / Device Controller** acts as a translator and a buffer. Example: the keyboard controller scans a matrix and produces a *scan code*. - **Speed Mismatch Problem:** I/O devices are orders of magnitude slower than the CPU. The interface therefore contains a **data buffer register** to hold the value until the CPU is ready. - **Mechanism:** When the user presses a key: 1. Device controller senses the keystroke. 2. Converts it into an ASCII/Unicode code. 3. Places the code into an **Input Register (INPR)**. 4. Sets a **flag (FGI)** so the CPU knows valid data is waiting. 5. CPU executes an *input instruction* to read INPR and clear the flag. #### 2.1.2 Memory Unit - **Hierarchy (top = fastest, smallest):** **Registers → L1 Cache → L2 Cache → L3 Cache → Main Memory (RAM) → Solid-State Drive → Hard Disk → Magnetic Tape**. - **Two Sub-Classes:** - **Primary (Main) Memory** — directly accessible by the CPU via load/store instructions (e.g., DDR4/DDR5 RAM). - **Secondary Storage** — non-volatile, used for permanent archiving (SSD, HDD). - **Memory Operations (R/W):** - **Memory Read:** Address placed on **Address Bus**, `MEMREAD` signal asserted on **Control Bus**, memory returns the contents on the **Data Bus**. - **Memory Write:** Address + data placed on buses, `MEMWRITE` asserted. - **Capacity Equation:** $$ \text{Total Memory} = 2^{n} \times W \quad \text{bytes} $$ where $n$ = number of address lines and $W$ = word size in bytes. Example: a 32-bit address bus with 8-bit cells addresses $2^{32}$ bytes = **4 GB**. #### 2.1.3 Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU) - Performs **three families** of operations: 1. **Arithmetic:** Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, Division, Increment, Decrement. 2. **Logic:** AND, OR, XOR, NOT, complement. 3. **Shift/Rotate:** Logical shift, arithmetic shift, circular rotate. - **Key Inputs:** Operand A, Operand B, *Operation Code* (from Control Unit). - **Key Outputs:** *Result*, **Zero flag (Z)**, **Carry flag (C)**, **Sign flag (N)**, **Overflow flag (V)** — collectively stored in the **Status Register / Program Status Word (PSW)**. - **Internal Components:** Adders, Multiplexers, Shifters, Complementers, Comparators. - **Example datapath snippet — Addition:** $$ \text{Sum}_i = A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_i \quad ; \quad C_{i+1} = (A_i \cdot B_i) + (C_i \cdot (A_i \oplus B_i)) $$ This is the **Full Adder** recurrence used to build an *n*-bit ripple-carry adder inside the ALU. #### 2.1.4 Output Unit - **Function:** Convert binary results into human-perceivable forms. - **Common Devices:** Monitor (video card buffer), Printer, Speaker (DAC converts digital samples to analog waveforms). - **Mechanism:** CPU writes to an **Output Register (OUTR)**; the output controller polls it and forwards data to the physical device. #### 2.1.5 Control Unit (CU) - The "brain inside the brain." It does **not** process data; it only **orchestrates**. - **Sub-tasks:** 1. **Fetch:** Read next instruction from memory (address held in PC) into **Instruction Register (IR)**. 2. **Decode:** The *Instruction Decoder* interprets the opcode field and consults the **Control Memory / Microprogram ROM** (in microprogrammed CUs) or the hardwired PLA. 3. **Execute / Generate Control Signals:** Asserts precise timing signals (`MEMREAD`, `MEMWRITE`, `ALUOP`, `REGWRITE`, etc.) to all other units. - **Two Implementation Styles:** - **Hardwired Control** — built from fixed combinational logic (FSMs, PLA). Faster, used in RISC cores. - **Microprogrammed Control** — control signals stored as *microinstructions* in a ROM. Slower but flexible, used in CISC processors. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **must-know equation set** for any Module-1 numerical in the KTU ESE / Internal exam. Memorize the variable definitions precisely. | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Definitions | Typical Units | |---|--------------------|---------------------|----------------| | 1 | $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | $N$ = instruction count; $CPI$ = cycles/instr; $T_{clk}$ = clock period | **seconds** | | 2 | $f_{clk} = 1 \,/\, T_{clk}$ | $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency | **Hz (GHz)** | | 3 | $MIPS = N \,/\, (T_{CPU} \times 10^{6})$ | Million Instructions Per Second | **MIPS** | | 4 | $MFLOPS = N_{fp} \,/\, (T_{CPU} \times 10^{6})$ | $N_{fp}$ = floating-point ops | **MFLOPS** | | 5 | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times I_i \,/\, N)$ | Weighted average over $n$ instruction classes | **cycles/instr** | | 6 | $\text{Speedup} = T_{old} \,/\, T_{new}$ | Ratio of execution times | **dimensionless** | | 7 | $\text{Amdahl: } S = 1 \,/\, \bigl[(1 - f) + (f \,/\, k)\bigr]$ | $f$ = enhanced fraction; $k$ = speedup of that fraction | **dimensionless** | | 8 | $P = 1 \,/\, T_{CPU}$ | Performance (inverse of time) | **1/s** | | 9 | $\text{Memory Capacity} = 2^{a} \times w$ | $a$ = address bits; $w$ = word bits | **bits / bytes** | | 10 | $\text{Throughput} = \text{Instructions} \,/\, \text{Second}$ | Often expressed as MIPS | **instr/s** | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding functional units is **not** academic trivia. It directly powers: - **CPU Benchmarking (SPEC, Geekbench):** Engineers measure $T_{CPU}$, CPI, and MIPS to compare architectures. - **Datacenter Procurement:** Cloud architects use **Amdahl's Law** to decide how many cores to deploy when a workload is only 80% parallelizable. - **Embedded Systems Design:** In IoT nodes, designers often *eliminate* the Output Unit (no screen) and shrink the Memory Unit to a few KB of SRAM, but **never** remove the Control Unit. - **GPU Design:** Modern GPUs replicate the ALU into thousands of parallel cores but share a single, centralized Control Unit — a direct application of the functional-unit abstraction at scale. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of CPU Execution Time (The "Iron Triangle") The single most-asked formula in KTU Module 1 is: $$ T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk} $$ **Derivation from first principles:** - Let the CPU execute $N$ instructions. - On average, each instruction consumes $CPI$ clock cycles. - Therefore the total number of clock cycles required is $N \times CPI$. - Each clock cycle lasts $T_{clk}$ seconds. - Hence the total wall-clock execution time equals cycles × duration per cycle: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{CPU} & = (\text{Total Cycles}) \times (\text{Duration per Cycle}) \\ & = (N \times CPI) \times T_{clk} \\ & = N \times CPI \times \frac{1}{f_{clk}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** $N$ counts the *workload*, $CPI$ captures *architectural efficiency*, and $T_{clk}$ captures *technology*. Reducing any of the three speeds the CPU up, but the relationship is **multiplicative**, not additive. Doubling the clock *halves* $T_{clk}$ and therefore *halves* $T_{CPU}$ *only if* $N$ and $CPI$ remain constant. ### 3.2 Derivation of Weighted CPI When a program mixes instruction types, the effective CPI is a **weighted average**: $$ \begin{aligned} CPI_{avg} & = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times I_i)}{N} \\ & = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( CPI_i \times \frac{I_i}{N} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical worked example (KTU-style):** A program contains: - 40 % ALU instructions with CPI = 1 - 30 % Load/Store with CPI = 5 - 20 % Branch with CPI = 3 - 10 % Floating-Point with CPI = 8 The total instruction count is $N = 10^{6}$. The clock period is $T_{clk} = 0.5 \, ns$ (i.e., $f_{clk} = 2 \, GHz$). **Step 1 — Compute the weighted CPI:** $$ \begin{aligned} CPI_{avg} & = (0.40 \times 1) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 8) \\ & = 0.40 + 1.50 + 0.60 + 0.80 \\ & = 3.30 \text{ cycles/instr} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute CPU time:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{CPU} & = N \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clk} \\ & = 10^{6} \times 3.30 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \\ & = 1.65 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\ & = 1.65 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute MIPS rating:** $$ \begin{aligned} MIPS & = \frac{N}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} \\ & = \frac{10^{6}}{1.65 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{6}} \\ & = \frac{1}{1.65} \\ & \approx 0.606 \text{ MIPS} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** MIPS is **not** a reliable performance metric across different ISAs. A RISC-V core and an x86 core may have identical MIPS yet differ in $T_{CPU}$ by 3× because the *x86 instruction does more work per "MIPS-counted" instruction*. KTU examiners love to deduct marks for blindly quoting MIPS without the caveat. ### 3.3 Derivation of Amdahl's Law **Gene Amdahl (1967)** asked: *"If I optimize a fraction $f$ of my program by a factor of $k$, what is the overall speedup?"* Let the original execution time be $T$. The optimized fraction $f$ shrinks to $T \cdot f / k$, while the un-optimized fraction $(1 - f)$ remains $T \cdot (1 - f)$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{new} & = T \cdot (1 - f) + T \cdot \frac{f}{k} \\ & = T \cdot \left[ (1 - f) + \frac{f}{k} \right] \end{aligned} $$ Therefore the speedup is: $$ S = \frac{T}{T_{new}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + (f / k)} $$ **Boundary conditions (must memorize):** - If $f = 1$ (entire program enhanced), $S = k$ (perfect scaling). - If $f = 0$ (nothing enhanced), $S = 1$ (no speedup). - As $k \to \infty$ (infinite enhancement of fraction $f$), $S \to 1 / (1 - f)$. **Worked example (KTU-favorite):** Suppose 40 % of a program can be parallelized and we throw 8 cores at it. The sequential part runs on 1 core, the parallel part runs on 8: $$ \begin{aligned} f & = 0.40, \quad k = 8 \\ S & = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + (0.40 / 8)} \\ & = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.05} \\ & = \frac{1}{0.65} \\ & \approx 1.538 \end{aligned} $$ So 8 cores deliver only a **1.54× speedup**, not the 8× the marketing brochure claims. This is the **fundamental ceiling** of parallelization. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CPU Performance Drivers The following matrix classifies *every lever* an architect can pull, and shows which variable in the iron triangle it touches: | Optimization Lever | Variable Affected | Effect on $T_{CPU}$ | Typical Mechanism | |--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|--------------------| | Increase clock frequency | $T_{clk}$ ↓ | Linear reduction | Smaller process node, pipelining | | Reduce instruction count | $N$ ↓ | Linear reduction | Compiler optimizations, RISC ISA | | Reduce cycles per instruction | $CPI$ ↓ | Linear reduction | Pipelining, caching, branch prediction | | Increase memory bandwidth | $CPI$ (memory part) ↓ | Sub-linear reduction | Wider buses, multi-level cache | | Add more cores | $f$ in Amdahl ↑ | Sub-linear (ceiling $1/(1-f)$) | Parallelism, vector units | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Top-Level Block Diagram of a Computer ```mermaid flowchart LR INU[INPUT UNIT
Keyboard Mouse Scanner] MEM[MEMORY UNIT
RAM Cache Registers] ALU[ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT
ALU Datapath] OUTU[OUTPUT UNIT
Monitor Printer Speaker] CU[CONTROL UNIT
Decoder Timing Logic] EXT[EXTERNAL WORLD
User Devices] INU -- Data Bus --> MEM MEM -- Data Bus --> ALU ALU -- Data Bus --> OUTU CU -. Control Signals .-> INU CU -. Control Signals .-> MEM CU -. Control Signals .-> ALU CU -. Control Signals .-> OUTU CU -- Address Bus --> MEM EXT --- INU EXT --- OUTU classDef unit fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; classDef ext fill:#374151,stroke:#9ca3af,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff; class INU,MEM,ALU,OUTU,CU unit; class EXT ext; ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Internal Architecture — CPU Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PROC["PROCESSOR / CPU"] PC[Program Counter PC] IR[Instruction Register IR] ID[Instruction Decoder] REGFILE[Register File
AC DR IR PC] ALUInt[ALU Core
Add Sub And Or Shift] PSW[Status Register
Z C N V] CUsubsys[Control Unit
Hardwired or Microprogrammed] end subgraph MMU["MEMORY SUBSYSTEM"] RAM[Main Memory RAM] MAR[Memory Address Register] MBR[Memory Buffer Register] end subgraph IO["I/O SUBSYSTEM"] INPR[Input Register INPR] OUTR[Output Register OUTR] IOCTRL[I/O Controller] end PC --> MAR MAR -- Address --> RAM RAM -- Read Data --> MBR MBR --> IR IR --> ID ID -- Opcode --> CUsubsys CUsubsys -- Control Wires --> REGFILE CUsubsys -- ALUOP --> ALUInt REGFILE -- Operand A --> ALUInt REGFILE -- Operand B --> ALUInt ALUInt -- Result --> REGFILE ALUInt -- Flags --> PSW CUsubsys -- MEMREAD MEMWRITE --> RAM INPR --> REGFILE REGFILE --> OUTR OUTR --> IOCTRL classDef cpu fill:#0f766e,stroke:#22d3ee,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; classDef mem fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fdba74,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000; classDef io fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#a78bfa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff; class PC,IR,ID,REGFILE,ALUInt,PSW,CUsubsys cpu; class RAM,MAR,MBR mem; class INPR,OUTR,IOCTRL io; ``` ### 4.3 Instruction Execution Cycle (Fetch–Decode–Execute) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Fetch Fetch: FETCH
AR less than PC
DR less than M AR
PC less than PC plus 1 Decode: DECODE
IR Opcode less than Decoder
Determine Operation Execute: EXECUTE
ALU performs op
Memory R or W
Update Flags Store: STORE
Write back to Register
or Memory Interrupt: INTERRUPT CHECK
Check Flags
Handle Service Request Fetch --> Decode Decode --> Execute Execute --> Store Store --> Interrupt Interrupt --> Fetch ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Each box is a *micro-step* the Control Unit orchestrates. Note that `PC ← PC + 1` happens *during the fetch*, not at the end of execute. Examiners award partial credit even if students draw only the three big boxes (Fetch / Decode / Execute) but lose 1–2 marks if the increment step is omitted. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > In the KTU ESE pattern, Part-A carries 2 marks each, but the **internal continuous evaluation** and module tests commonly allot **3-mark short-answer slots** for definitions and short explanations. The two questions below are calibrated to that style. #### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` Define the term "functional unit" of a computer. List any three functional units and state one function of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A *functional unit* is a logically distinct hardware subsystem that performs a well-defined role in the operation of a digital computer and interacts with the other units through buses and control signals. As per the stored-program concept, every general-purpose computer comprises five basic functional units. | Functional Unit | Function (1 mark per row) | |------------------|----------------------------| | **Input Unit** | Accepts data and instructions from the external environment (user) and converts them into binary form suitable for processing. | | **Memory Unit** | Stores both data and instructions; provides fast random access to the CPU during program execution. | | **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)** | Performs all arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷), logical (AND, OR, XOR, NOT), and shift operations on the data. | *(Any three of the five are acceptable. 1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for unit + ½ mark each for function.)* --- #### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` Distinguish between hardwired control and microprogrammed control. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control | |-----------|-------------------|--------------------------| | **Implementation** | Built from fixed combinational logic (gates, PLA, FSM). | Control signals stored as *microinstructions* in a control memory (ROM/RAM). | | **Speed** | Faster — direct logic generates signals. | Slower — requires a microinstruction fetch cycle. | | **Flexibility** | Difficult to modify; design must be re-done. | Easy to modify by rewriting the microprogram. | | **Used in** | Mostly RISC processors (ARM, RISC-V). | Mostly CISC processors (classic x86). | | **Hardware cost** | Higher (many gates). | Lower (compact ROM). | *(1½ marks for hardwired + 1½ marks for microprogrammed. Tabular comparison earns full credit.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **basic functional units of a computer** and describe the role of the **system buses** interconnecting them. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A digital computer comprises five basic functional units as shown below. They are interconnected by three major buses: 1. **Data Bus** — carries the actual data being transferred (bidirectional). 2. **Address Bus** — carries the memory/I/O address (unidirectional, CPU → Memory/I/O). 3. **Control Bus** — carries timing and command signals (`MEMREAD`, `MEMWRITE`, `IORD`, `IOWR`, `INTA`, etc.). **Functional roles (7 marks distributed):** - **Input Unit (1 mark):** Interfaces external devices (keyboard, mouse) to the CPU through an I/O controller. Converts analog/physical stimuli into binary codes. Example: ASCII for a keypress. - **Memory Unit (1 mark):** Stores active programs and data. Organized as addressable cells. Supports `Read` and `Write` operations controlled by signals on the control bus. - **ALU (1 mark):** The computational core. Takes two operands from the register file, performs the decoded operation, places the result back in the AC (Accumulator), and updates the status flags (Z, C, N, V). - **Output Unit (1 mark):** Receives binary data from the CPU and converts it into human-perceivable form. Examples include a monitor (via video RAM + DAC) and a printer. - **Control Unit (2 marks):** The "orchestrator." It fetches each instruction, decodes the opcode, and generates the precise sequence of control signals (a *control word*) that drives all other units. It uses either hardwired logic or a microprogrammed control store. - **Buses (1 mark):** Provide the *shared communication fabric*. The data bus is bidirectional; the address bus is unidirectional; the control bus is a bundle of unidirectional timing lines. --- **(b)** Explain the **internal organization of the processor** with reference to the registers **PC, AR, IR, DR, AC**, and the common bus system used in the basic computer (Mano's design). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The basic computer proposed by **M. Morris Mano** uses a **single common bus** to interconnect all registers, the ALU, and the memory subsystem. Each register has dedicated load enable inputs and connects to the bus via tri-state buffers. **Key registers and their functions:** | Register | Symbol | Width | Function | |----------|--------|-------|----------| | Program Counter | **PC** | 12 bits | Holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched. | | Address Register | **AR** | 12 bits | Holds the memory address for the current read/write operation. | | Instruction Register | **IR** | 16 bits | Holds the *currently executing* instruction (opcode + operand). | | Data Register | **DR** | 16 bits | Buffer between memory and the processor for data being read/written. | | Accumulator | **AC** | 16 bits | The primary working register; one of the ALU operands usually lives here. | | Temporary Register | **TR** | 16 bits | Scratchpad used internally by the ALU. | | Input Register | **INPR** | 8 bits | Holds the incoming data from an input device. | | Output Register | **OUTR** | 8 bits | Holds data going to an output device. | **Operation (micro-level):** - **Fetch phase:** `AR ← PC`, `IR ← M[AR]`, `PC ← PC + 1`. Three micro-operations. - **Decode phase:** `IR(12-14) → Decoder D₃…D₇`. Identifies the opcode. - **Execute phase:** Depends on opcode. For example, `ADD` performs `DR ← M[AR]`, `AC ← AC + DR`, `SC ← 0` (clear sequence counter). - **Interrupt cycle:** If `IEN` and `FGI`/`FGO` flags are set, `AR ← 0`, `TR ← PC`, `M[AR] ← TR`, `PC ← 0`, `IEN ← 0`, `R ← 1`. The **common bus system** is governed by select lines $S_2, S_1, S_0$ that decide *which* register's output is currently placed on the bus, and load-control inputs that decide *which* register latches the bus value at the next clock edge. *(Each register: ½ mark, fetch-decode-execute flow: 2 marks, common-bus concept: 1 mark.)* --- #### ⭐ Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **working of the Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)**. Discuss the role of **status flags**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The **ALU** is the computational engine of the CPU. It accepts two $n$-bit operands $A$ and $B$, an operation selector line ($S_3 S_2 S_1 S_0$ in a typical 16-function ALU like the 74LS181), and produces an $n$-bit result $F$ plus status flags. **Internal Organization:** - A **logical unit** for AND, OR, XOR, NOT. - An **arithmetic unit** built around an $n$-bit parallel adder with a look-ahead carry generator. - A **shifter** for logical shift, arithmetic shift, and rotate. - A **multiplexer** that selects which unit's output becomes $F$. **Status Flags (4 standard ones):** 1. **Carry (C):** Set when an addition produces a carry-out, or a subtraction needs a borrow. 2. **Zero (Z):** Set when the result of the most recent operation is zero. 3. **Sign (N):** Reflects the most significant bit of the result (in two's complement, this is the sign bit). 4. **Overflow (V):** Set when a signed operation produces a result that cannot fit in $n$ bits. Detected as $V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_n$. **Example — 8-bit signed overflow:** Adding $0111\,1111 \,(+127)$ and $0000\,0001 \,(+1)$ gives $1000\,0000$, which the CPU *interprets* as $-128$. The Carry flag is $0$ but the Sign flag is $1$, so $V = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$, correctly flagging the overflow. **Real-world use of flags:** Branch instructions (`BEQ`, `BNE`, `BLT`, `BGT`) test combinations of these flags to alter program flow. Without status flags, conditional logic would be impossible. *(Diagram: 2 marks, ALU ops explanation: 3 marks, flag descriptions: 2 marks.)* --- **(b)** State and derive **Amdahl's Law**. A program takes **100 s** to run on a single core. **60 %** of it can be parallelized perfectly and is now run on **4 cores**, while the rest remains sequential. Compute the new execution time and overall speedup. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Amdahl's Law (Statement — 2 marks):** The overall speedup $S$ obtained by enhancing a fraction $f$ of a computation by a factor of $k$ is bounded by: $$ S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + (f / k)} $$ As $k \to \infty$, $S$ approaches the hard ceiling $1 / (1 - f)$, meaning *no amount of hardware can overcome the sequential bottleneck*. **Derivation (2 marks):** Let the original execution time be $T$. The optimized fraction takes $T \cdot f / k$; the un-optimized fraction takes $T \cdot (1 - f)$. Adding: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{new} & = T(1 - f) + T \cdot \frac{f}{k} \\ & = T \cdot \left[(1 - f) + \frac{f}{k}\right] \end{aligned} $$ Taking the ratio: $$ S = \frac{T}{T_{new}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + (f/k)} $$ **Numerical Evaluation (3 marks):** Given: $T = 100 \, s$, $f = 0.60$, $k = 4$. **Step 1 — Parallel portion time:** $$ T_{parallel} = \frac{T \cdot f}{k} = \frac{100 \times 0.60}{4} = 15 \, s $$ **Step 2 — Sequential portion time:** $$ T_{seq} = T \cdot (1 - f) = 100 \times 0.40 = 40 \, s $$ **Step 3 — New total time:** $$ T_{new} = T_{seq} + T_{parallel} = 40 + 15 = 55 \, s $$ **Step 4 — Speedup:** $$ S = \frac{T}{T_{new}} = \frac{100}{55} \approx 1.818 $$ **Interpretation:** Using 4 cores on a workload that is only 60% parallel yields only **1.82× speedup**, leaving **55 %** of the original execution time un-recovered. If the parallel fraction could be enhanced to $k \to \infty$, the ceiling would be: $$ S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.60} = 2.5 $$ So even an *infinite* number of cores would deliver at most 2.5× — a sobering result that justifies the famous dictum *"make the common case fast, and the sequential case rare."* --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students *typically* lose marks on Module-1 functional-units questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the three buses.** Many students draw the five blocks connected by a single arrow. KTU examiners specifically look for *Data, Address, Control* buses drawn and labeled. Omitting any one costs **1 mark**. > 2. **Confusing Memory with Storage.** *Memory* = RAM (volatile, fast). *Storage* = HDD/SSD (non-volatile, slow). Conflating them is a classic **−2 mark** deduction. > 3. **Skipping the Control Unit's role.** The CU does *not* process data. Students who write "CU performs ALU operations" lose marks instantly. > 4. **MIPS misuse.** Quoting MIPS without specifying the ISA, instruction mix, or clock rate is considered an *incomplete* answer in 14-mark questions. Always pair MIPS with CPI or $T_{CPU}$. > 5. **Amdahl's Law boundary.** Forgetting that $k \to \infty$ caps the speedup at $1/(1-f)$ is the #1 reason students lose the last 1–2 marks on derivation problems. > 6. **Block diagrams without arrows.** A diagram where arrows are missing or unlabeled earns **0 marks** even if the verbal answer is perfect. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Functional Units (KTU 2024 Scheme)** - **Five functional units:** Input, Memory, ALU, Output, Control. Mnemonic: **I-M-A-O-C**. - **Three system buses:** Data (bidirectional), Address (uni, CPU → outside), Control (timing signals). - **Stored-program concept** = instructions and data share the same memory (von Neumann, 1945). - **CPU time** $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$. Reducing any one factor reduces $T_{CPU}$ linearly. - **MIPS** $ = N / (T_{CPU} \times 10^{6})$ — useful *within* one ISA, misleading across ISAs. - **Amdahl's Law** $S = 1 / [(1 - f) + (f/k)]$; ceiling = $1/(1-f)$ as $k \to \infty$. - **Status flags:** Carry (C), Zero (Z), Sign (N), Overflow (V) — collectively stored in the PSW. - **Control Unit styles:** Hardwired (fast, rigid) vs Microprogrammed (slow, flexible). - **Mano's basic computer registers** (memorize symbols and widths): PC, AR (12-bit); IR, DR, AC, TR, INPR, OUTR (16-bit / 8-bit). - **Performance hierarchy:** $P = 1 / T_{CPU}$; **never** confuse *execution time* (lower = better) with *performance* (higher = better). - **Common exam trap:** Always show the *units* in your final numerical answer (e.g., $T_{CPU} = 1.65 \, ms$, not just $1.65$).

topics.title: Basic Operational Concepts - Bus Structures. Memory Locations and Addresses - Memory Operations, Instructions and Instruction Sequencing, Addressing Modes. Basic Processing Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Operational Concepts - Bus Structures. Memory Locations and Addresses - Memory Operations, Instructions and Instruction Sequencing, Addressing Modes. Basic Processing Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Basic Operational Concepts

A computer system, at its most fundamental level, performs four basic operations:
1. **Data Transfer** between the processor and memory (or I/O devices).
2. **Data Processing** — arithmetic and logical operations on data.
3. **Data Storage** — temporary (registers/cache) and permanent (memory/disk) retention.
4. **Control** — managing and coordinating the above three operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A *processor* executes a sequence of instructions (a program) stored in memory. The basic operational concept revolves around fetching, decoding, and executing instructions in a continuous cycle, governed by a **Program Counter (PC)** and a clock signal.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen
Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** as the computer:
- The **chef (CPU)** follows a *recipe book (program)* kept on a shelf (memory).
- The chef keeps small bowls of ingredients within arm's reach — these are **registers**.
- A **waiter (bus)** ferries ingredients between the shelf and the chef.
- A **head chef (control unit)** shouts out which step of the recipe is next.

The CPU continuously **fetches** the recipe step (instruction), **decodes** what to do (identify the operation and operands), and **executes** it.

### 1.2 Bus Structures

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common lines through which data, addresses, and control signals are transferred among the processor, memory, and I/O devices.

There are three principal types of buses:

| Bus Type | Function | Width (typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus** | Carries actual data between components | 32, 64 bits |
| **Address Bus** | Carries the memory/I/O address | 16–64 bits |
| **Control Bus** | Carries timing & command signals (Read/Write, Interrupt) | varies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The address bus is **unidirectional** (CPU → Memory/I/O), while the data bus is **bidirectional**. The control bus is a *mixed* (asynchronous) channel.

**Bus Organization Variants:**
- **Single Bus Structure:** All units share one common bus. Simple but creates a bottleneck (only one transfer at a time).
- **Multiple Bus Structure:** Dedicated or split buses (e.g., separate local bus for cache) — increases throughput.

### 1.3 Memory Locations, Addresses & Operations

- **Memory Address:** An unsigned binary number that uniquely identifies a memory location. The range is from **0** to **2ⁿ − 1**, where *n* is the address bus width.
- **Memory Word:** The natural unit of organization, typically **32 or 64 bits**.
- **Big-Endian vs. Little-Endian:**
  - *Big-Endian:* MSB stored at the lowest address (used in network byte order, IBM mainframes).
  - *Little-Endian:* LSB stored at the lowest address (used by Intel x86, most ARM in little-endian mode).

> [!NOTE]
> **Endianness = "End-ian-ness"** — Where does the *big end* of the number sit?

### 1.4 Instructions & Instruction Sequencing

An **instruction** is a binary code that specifies:
- The **opcode** (what operation to perform)
- The **operands** (where data comes from / goes to)
- The **addressing mode** (how to interpret the operand)

**Three-Address Format:** `ADD R1, R2, R3` → R2 + R3 → R1

**Instruction Execution Cycle (von Neumann cycle):**
1. **Fetch** the instruction from memory (using PC).
2. **Decode** the instruction.
3. **Execute** — read operands, perform ALU operation.
4. **Store** results.
5. **Update PC** (PC ← PC + 1 normally; PC ← branch target on jump).

### 1.5 Addressing Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Addressing modes specify the *rule* for interpreting the operand field(s) of an instruction to find the effective address (EA).

| Mode | EA Calculation |
|---|---|
| Immediate | Operand = constant in instruction |
| Direct (Absolute) | EA = Address field of instruction |
| Register | Operand is in a register |
| Register Indirect | EA = contents of a register |
| Displacement (Indexed) | EA = A + (R) |
| Relative | EA = PC + offset |
| Auto-increment / Auto-decrement | EA = (R); R ← R ± 1 |
| Indexed | EA = A + Index Register |

### 1.6 Basic Processing Unit

The **CPU internals** consist of:
- **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — performs data operations.
- **Register File** — high-speed on-chip storage (PC, IR, GPRs, MAR, MBR).
- **Control Unit (CU)** — generates timing/control signals.
- **Internal CPU Bus(es)** — connect ALU, registers, and external bus interface.

**Essential Internal Registers (KTU focus):**
- **PC** (Program Counter) — holds address of next instruction.
- **MAR** (Memory Address Register) — holds the address for memory access.
- **MBR** (Memory Buffer Register / MDR) — holds data transferred to/from memory.
- **IR** (Instruction Register) — holds the currently fetched instruction.
- **PSW/Flags** — status flags (N, Z, C, V).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Basic Operational Cycle (Clock-Paced Operation)

Each instruction is processed through a precise sequence of micro-operations. Each micro-operation takes one clock cycle (or one sub-cycle). The **fetch-decode-execute** cycle is universal.

### 2.2 Bus Arbitration & Transfer Protocol

When multiple devices share a bus, an **arbiter** (bus controller) decides *who* drives the bus. Two main schemes:
- **Daisy Chain Arbitration** (priority hard-wired).
- **Centralized Parallel Arbitration** (independent request/grant lines).

**Synchronous Bus** — uses a common clock; transfers occur on clock edges.
**Asynchronous Bus** — uses handshaking signals (Request, Acknowledge) — flexible but slower.

### 2.3 Memory Operations — Read & Write

**Memory Read:**
1. MAR ← address (from PC or instruction)
2. Send *Read* control signal on control bus
3. MBR ← Memory[MAR]
4. Copy MBR to destination (IR for instruction fetch)

**Memory Write:**
1. MAR ← address
2. MBR ← data to be written
3. Send *Write* control signal
4. Memory[MAR] ← MBR

### 2.4 Instruction Formats

| Format | # of Address Fields | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Three-address** | 3 (two source + one destination) | `ADD R1, R2, R3` |
| **Two-address** | 2 (one source-destination, one source) | `ADD R1, R2` → R1 ← R1 + R2 |
| **One-address** | 1 (implicit accumulator) | `ADD X` → AC ← AC + X |
| **Zero-address (Stack)** | 0 (operands on stack) | `ADD` → TOS ← TOS + SOS |
| **RISC Fixed** | 3 (register-register) | `add r1, r2, r3` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** CISC (e.g., x86) historically uses **variable-length 2-/1-address formats**; RISC (e.g., MIPS, ARM) uses **fixed 32-bit 3-address formats**.

### 2.5 Instruction Sequencing

Sequential flow: **PC ← PC + I** where *I* = instruction length in words.
Branching: **PC ← PC + offset** (relative branch) or **PC ← target** (absolute jump).
Subroutine: involves saving PC, jumping, and returning via stored link.

### 2.6 Addressing Modes — Deep Analytical View

| Mode | EA | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | Operand is the value | Loading constants |
| **Register** | R | Fastest; limited # of registers |
| **Direct/Absolute** | A | Single global variable |
| **Indirect** | (A) | Pointer chasing |
| **Register Indirect** | (R) | Pointer in a register |
| **Displacement** | A + (R) or (R) + A | Local arrays, structure access |
| **Indexed** | A + (X) | Array traversal (X = index) |
| **Base-Register** | A + (B) | Base addressing |
| **Relative** | PC + offset | Position-independent branches |
| **Auto-inc/dec** | (R); R←R±1 | Sequential stack pointer, loop counters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked to compute EA, always write the formula and substitute values step-by-step. Marks are awarded for the *formula*, not just the final number.

### 2.7 Basic Processing Unit — Functional Anatomy

**Single-bus CPU organization** (most KTU questions):
- **ALU** connected to a single internal bus via two multiplexers (A and B inputs).
- **Register file** with input (IN) and output (OUT) ports.
- **Status flags** derived from ALU result (N, Z, C, V).

**Multi-bus CPU organization:** Allows simultaneous transfer between two registers to ALU → reduces clock cycles per instruction.

## 🧮 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Equation |
|---|---|---|
| $2^{n}$ | Max memory addresses with n-bit address bus | $\text{Mem}_{max} = 2^{n} \text{ locations}$ |
| $W$ | Memory word size | Total memory = $2^{n} \times W$ bits |
| $BW$ | Bus bandwidth | $BW = f_{bus} \times W$ bytes/sec |
| $EA$ | Effective Address | varies per mode (see table) |
| $T_{exec}$ | Total execution time | $T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ |
| $N$ | Instruction count | given |
| $CPI$ | Cycles per instruction | depends on ISA |
| $T_{clock}$ | Clock period | $1/f_{clock}$ |
| $Amdahl$ | Speedup limited fraction | $S = 1 / \big((1-f) + f/p\big)$ |
| $PC_{next}$ | Next PC value | $PC_{next} = PC + I$ (sequential) |
| $PC_{branch}$ | Branch PC | $PC_{branch} = PC + I + \text{offset}$ |
| $n_{bytes}$ | Bytes per address | $n_{bytes} = 2^{addr\_width - mem\_width}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Addressing modes are the *backbone of compiler code generation*. Compilers choose addressing modes to minimize instruction count and memory accesses. Bus structures determine whether a system is a desktop, server, or embedded SoC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Capacity from Address Bus Width

A system has an **address bus of width *n* bits**. Each unique binary pattern on the address bus selects exactly one memory location.

$$\text{Number of locations} = 2^{n}$$

If each location holds $W$ bits (word size), the total memory capacity is:

$$M_{total} = 2^{n} \times W \text{ bits}$$

Conversion to bytes:

$$M_{bytes} = \frac{2^{n} \times W}{8}$$

**Worked Example (KTU standard):**
A processor has a **24-bit address bus** and a **memory word size of 16 bits**. Find the maximum directly addressable memory.

$$\text{Locations} = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ words}$$

$$\text{Total bits} = 2^{24} \times 16 \text{ bits} = 2^{28} \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{Total bytes} = \frac{2^{28}}{8} = 2^{25} \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ MB}$$

> ✅ **Answer: 16M locations = 32 MB**

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Address for Addressing Modes

**General Displacement Mode:**
$$EA = A + (R)$$

where $A$ is the constant (displacement field) in the instruction and $(R)$ is the contents of register $R$.

**Worked example:**
Register R1 = 4000h, instruction contains displacement A = 2000h. Find EA.

$$EA = A + (R_1) = 2000\text{h} + 4000\text{h} = 6000\text{h}$$

**Relative Addressing for Branches:**
$$EA_{branch} = (PC) + \text{offset}$$

Suppose PC = 3000h (already incremented past the branch instruction), and the offset field = 0050h.

$$EA_{branch} = 3000\text{h} + 0050\text{h} = 3050\text{h}$$

**Indexed Mode:**
$$EA = A + (X)$$

A = 1000h, X = 0100h → EA = 1000h + 0100h = 1100h.

## 3.3 Derivation: Program Counter Update

After fetching an instruction of length $I$ words (typically $I = 1$ for fixed 32-bit instructions, $I = 1$ word = 4 bytes):

$$PC_{next} = PC_{current} + I$$

If the instruction is a branch with offset $d$ (in words):

$$PC_{branch} = PC_{current} + I + d$$

If the instruction is an unconditional jump to absolute address $T$:

$$PC_{jump} = T$$

## 3.4 Derivation: CPU Performance Equation

The time to execute a program of $N$ instructions on a processor with average CPI and clock period $T_{clock}$:

$$T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock} = \frac{N \times CPI}{f_{clock}}$$

**Worked Example (KTU style):**
A program has $N = 10^6$ instructions, CPI = 2.0, clock frequency = 500 MHz.

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{10^6 \times 2.0}{500 \times 10^6} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^8} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Endianness — Multi-byte Word Storage

Suppose we store the 32-bit word `0x12345678` at address `1000h`.

**Big-Endian (MSB first):**
$$\text{Addr } 1000\text{h} \rightarrow 0x12$$
$$\text{Addr } 1001\text{h} \rightarrow 0x34$$
$$\text{Addr } 1002\text{h} \rightarrow 0x56$$
$$\text{Addr } 1003\text{h} \rightarrow 0x78$$

**Little-Endian (LSB first):**
$$\text{Addr } 1000\text{h} \rightarrow 0x78$$
$$\text{Addr } 1001\text{h} \rightarrow 0x56$$
$$\text{Addr } 1002\text{h} \rightarrow 0x34$$
$$\text{Addr } 1003\text{h} \rightarrow 0x12$$

> ✅ **Marking Tip:** Draw the memory table clearly; examiners award 1 mark for the table layout alone.

## 3.6 Derivation: Single-Bus CPU — Register Transfer for ADD

To execute `ADD R1, R2` (i.e., R1 ← R1 + R2) on a **single internal bus** CPU:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: } R2_{out}, \text{Select}_B \rightarrow A_{in} \quad \text{// Copy R2 to ALU input B} \\
&\text{Step 2: } R1_{out}, \text{Select}_A \rightarrow \text{ALU adds A+B} \quad \text{// ALU computes A+B} \\
&\text{Step 3: } \text{ALU}_{out} \rightarrow R1_{in} \quad \text{// Store result in R1} \\
&\text{Step 4: } \text{Update flags (N, Z, C, V)}
\end{aligned}$$

This requires **3 clock cycles** on a single-bus design.

On a **three-bus** CPU, the same ADD takes **1 clock cycle** because both source registers can be routed simultaneously and the result latched back in one step.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Instruction Cycle Comparison

| Step | Three-Bus CPU | Two-Bus CPU | Single-Bus CPU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Read R1 to A-latch | 1 cycle | 1 cycle | 1 cycle |
| Read R2 to B-latch | parallel | 1 cycle | 1 cycle |
| ALU computes | 1 cycle | 1 cycle | 1 cycle |
| Write back to R1 | parallel | parallel | 1 cycle |
| **Total cycles for ADD** | **1** | **2** | **3** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Single-Bus CPU Organization (Basic Processing Unit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT"]
        PC["Program Counter PC"]
        MAR["Memory Address Register MAR"]
        MBR["Memory Buffer Register MBR"]
        IR["Instruction Register IR"]
        ALU["Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU"]
        RF["Register File R0..Rn"]
        CU["Control Unit CU"]
        MUXA["MUX A Input"]
        MUXB["MUX B Input"]
    end
    MEM["MAIN MEMORY"]
    IBUS["INTERNAL CPU BUS"]
    EXT["EXTERNAL SYSTEM BUS"]

    PC --> MAR
    MAR --> EXT
    EXT --> MEM
    MEM --> EXT
    EXT --> MBR
    MBR --> IR
    IR --> CU
    CU -.control signals.-> ALU
    CU -.control signals.-> RF
    RF --> MUXA
    RF --> MUXB
    MUXA --> ALU
    MUXB --> ALU
    ALU --> RF
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: System Bus Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU CHIP"]
    MEM["MAIN MEMORY RAM"]
    IO1["I/O DEVICE 1"]
    IO2["I/O DEVICE 2"]
    DBUS["DATA BUS 64 BITS"]
    ABUS["ADDRESS BUS 32 BITS"]
    CBUS["CONTROL BUS"]

    CPU -- drives --> ABUS
    CPU -- read/write --> DBUS
    CPU -- control signals --> CBUS
    ABUS --> MEM
    ABUS --> IO1
    ABUS --> IO2
    DBUS <--> MEM
    DBUS <--> IO1
    DBUS <--> IO2
    CBUS --> MEM
    CBUS --> IO1
    CBUS --> IO2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Instruction Execution Cycle (Fetch–Decode–Execute)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Fetch Cycle"] --> B["MAR PC content"]
    B --> C["Memory Read using MAR"]
    C --> D["MBR Memory MAR"]
    D --> E["IR MBR"]
    E --> F["PC PC plus 1"]
    F --> G["Decode Opcode in IR"]
    G --> H{"Is it Branch?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["PC PC plus offset"]
    H -- "No" --> J["Read Operands from Registers or Memory"]
    I --> K["Execute ALU Operation if any"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Write Result to Register or Memory"]
    L --> M["Update Status Flags N Z C V"]
    M --> N["Loop back to Start"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Addressing Modes Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Decode Operand Field"] --> Q1{"Is operand literal?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> IMM["Immediate Mode: value in instruction"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Is operand in register?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> REG["Register Mode: operand = R"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Does field hold memory address?"}
    Q3 -- "Direct A" --> DIR["Direct: EA = A"]
    Q3 -- "Indirect via register" --> IND["Indirect: EA = R"]
    Q3 -- "Displacement" --> DIS["Displacement: EA = A + R"]
    Q3 -- "Relative to PC" --> REL["Relative: EA = PC + offset"]
    Q3 -- "Indexed" --> IDX["Indexed: EA = A + X"]
    Q3 -- "Auto-modify" --> AUTO["Auto-inc/dec: EA = R; R = R ± 1"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Read/Write Micro-Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph READ["MEMORY READ"]
        R1["MAR address"] --> R2["Issue Read Signal"]
        R2 --> R3["Wait for Memory Access"]
        R3 --> R4["MBR Data from Memory"]
    end
    subgraph WRITE["MEMORY WRITE"]
        W1["MAR address"] --> W2["MBR Data"]
        W2 --> W3["Issue Write Signal"]
        W3 --> W4["Memory MAR Data"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the terms: *bus*, *address bus*, and *control bus*. Why is the data bus bidirectional while the address bus is unidirectional?

**Model Answer:**

A **bus** is a shared set of communication lines used to transfer data, addresses, and control signals among the CPU, memory, and I/O devices.

- **Address Bus:** Carries the *memory or I/O address* from the CPU to memory/I/O. It is **unidirectional** because the CPU is the *only source* of addresses; memory never initiates an address.
- **Control Bus:** Carries *timing and command signals* such as *Read*, *Write*, *Interrupt Acknowledge*, *Bus Request*, *Bus Grant*.
- **Data Bus:** Carries actual *data* between CPU ↔ memory and CPU ↔ I/O. It is **bidirectional** because data must flow *both ways* — read returns data to CPU, write sends data from CPU to memory.

> **Marks Distribution (3M):** Definition (1M) + Address Bus role (1M) + Justification of bidirectionality (1M).

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - June 2024] Explain the difference between *Big-Endian* and *Little-Endian* byte ordering with an example.

**Model Answer:**

Endianness describes the **byte order** in which a multi-byte word is stored in memory.

- **Big-Endian:** Stores the **most significant byte (MSB)** at the *lowest* address.
- **Little-Endian:** Stores the **least significant byte (LSB)** at the *lowest* address.

**Example:** Store 32-bit word `0x12345678` at base address `1000h`.

| Address | Big-Endian | Little-Endian |
|---|---|---|
| 1000h | 0x12 | 0x78 |
| 1001h | 0x34 | 0x56 |
| 1002h | 0x56 | 0x34 |
| 1003h | 0x78 | 0x12 |

Intel x86 uses Little-Endian; network protocols (TCP/IP) and IBM mainframes use Big-Endian.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

### **Question A — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three-bus organization of a CPU**. Show how an instruction `ADD R1, R2, R3` (R1 ← R2 + R3) is executed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **fetch-decode-execute cycle** with the help of register transfer notation for each instruction phase. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for (a):**

**Three-Bus CPU Organization:**

In a **three-bus CPU**, the register file has two output ports (Bus A, Bus B) feeding the ALU's two inputs simultaneously, and one input port (Bus C) for latching the result.

**Block Diagram Description:**
- **Bus A** carries contents of source register 1 (R2) to ALU input A.
- **Bus B** carries contents of source register 2 (R3) to ALU input B.
- **ALU** performs addition: A + B.
- **Bus C** carries result to destination register (R1).
- **Control Unit** generates signals: SelA, SelB, SelC (register select lines), ALUop, RegWrite.

**Execution of `ADD R1, R2, R3` in ONE clock cycle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{BusA} \leftarrow R2 \\
&\text{BusB} \leftarrow R3 \\
&\text{ALU} \leftarrow \text{ADD}(\text{BusA}, \text{BusB}) \\
&\text{BusC} \leftarrow \text{ALU}_{out} \\
&R1 \leftarrow \text{BusC}
\end{aligned}$$

All these transfers occur in **parallel within a single clock cycle**, hence the three-bus design achieves 1-cycle-per-instruction execution.

**Comparison table:**

| Feature | Single Bus | Two Bus | Three Bus |
|---|---|---|---|
| ADD cycles | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| Hardware | minimal | moderate | high |
| Speed | slowest | medium | fastest |

> ✅ **Marks Allocation:** Diagram (3M) + Explanation of 3 buses (2M) + Step-by-step execution (2M).

---

#### **Model Answer for (b):**

**Fetch Phase:**
$$\begin{aligned}
&MAR \leftarrow PC \\
&MBR \leftarrow Memory[MAR] \quad \text{// Read from memory} \\
&IR \leftarrow MBR \\
&PC \leftarrow PC + 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Decode Phase:**
$$CU \leftarrow \text{Opcode}(IR)$$
$$CU \text{ generates control signals based on opcode}$$

**Operand Fetch Phase:**
- If register operand: $\text{RegA} \leftarrow R_{src1}$
- If memory operand: $\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{Address}(IR)$; $\text{MBR} \leftarrow Memory[MAR]$

**Execute Phase:**
- ALU performs operation on operands.
- Branch instruction: $PC \leftarrow EA_{branch}$

**Store Phase:**
- Result → destination register or memory.
- Update flags: N, Z, C, V.

**Summary (one-line per phase):** **Fetch → Decode → Operand Fetch → Execute → Store.**

> ✅ **Marks Allocation:** Fetch (2M) + Decode (1M) + Operand Fetch (2M) + Execute (1M) + Store (1M).

---

### **Question B — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the various **addressing modes** of a computer with suitable examples and diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A processor has a **16-bit address bus** and a **memory word size of 8 bits**. Find: (i) the maximum directly addressable memory, (ii) the memory capacity in bytes and kilobytes, (iii) the memory capacity in bits. If the clock frequency is **100 MHz** and the CPI is **2.5**, compute the execution time for a program of **5 × 10⁵ instructions**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for (a):**

**Addressing Modes** — refer to SECTION 1.5 and SECTION 2.6 tables.

**Detailed Explanation with Examples (assume A = 2000h, R1 = 4000h, X = 0100h, PC = 3000h, instr = at 3000h, length = 1 word):**

| Mode | Formula | EA / Value | Example |
