|---|---|---|---|
| Immediate | Operand = constant | Operand = 5 | `MOV R1, #5` |
| Direct (Absolute) | EA = A | 2000h | `LOAD R1, 2000h` |
| Register | Operand in R | R1's content | `MOV R1, R2` |
| Register Indirect | EA = (R) | (R1) = 4000h | `LOAD R2, (R1)` |
| Displacement | EA = A + (R) | 2000h + 4000h = 6000h | `LOAD R1, 2000h(R2)` |
| Indexed | EA = A + (X) | 2000h + 0100h = 2100h | `LOAD R1, 2000h(X)` |
| Relative | EA = PC + offset | 3000h + 1 + offset | branch instructions |
| Auto-increment | EA = (R); R = R + 1 | (R1) used, then R1++ | stack pop |
| Auto-decrement | R = R - 1; EA = (R) | R1--, then use (R1) | stack push |

> ✅ **Marks Allocation:** List of 8 modes (4M) + Example per mode (2M) + Diagram (1M).

---

#### **Model Answer for (b):**

**Given:**
- Address bus width: $n = 16$ bits
- Memory word size: $W = 8$ bits = 1 byte
- Clock frequency: $f = 100 \text{ MHz} = 10^8 \text{ Hz}$
- CPI = 2.5
- Instruction count: $N = 5 \times 10^5$

**(i) Maximum directly addressable memory locations:**
$$\text{Locations} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64\text{K locations}$$

**(ii) Memory capacity in bytes and kilobytes:**
$$\text{Capacity} = 2^{16} \times 8 \text{ bits} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

**(iii) Memory capacity in bits:**
$$M_{bits} = 2^{16} \times 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bits} = 512 \text{ Kbits}$$

**Execution Time Calculation:**
$$T_{clock} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{10^8} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock}$$

$$T_{CPU} = (5 \times 10^5) \times 2.5 \times (10 \times 10^{-9}) \text{ s}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 12.5 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 0.0125 \text{ s} = 12.5 \text{ ms}$$

> ✅ **Marks Allocation:** (i) 1M, (ii) 2M, (iii) 1M, Time formula 1M, substitution 1M, final answer 1M.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting PC + I (increment by instruction length)** — branches use PC + 1 + offset, not just PC + offset. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Confusing address bus width with data bus width** — memory capacity depends on BOTH, not just address bus.
> 3. **Mixing up big-endian and little-endian tables** — examiners check each cell; even one wrong byte = half-marks lost.
> 4. **In CPU diagrams:** failing to show the *direction of data flow* (arrows). Always label MUXes, ALU inputs, and result latch.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention MAR/MBR registers** in fetch-decode-execute — these are *mandatory* register transfer notations.
> 6. **Wrong unit conversion:** $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ NOT $65{,}000$; never approximate.
> 7. **CPI calculation:** forgetting to multiply by clock *period* (not frequency directly).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- ✅ **Bus Types:** Data (bidirectional), Address (unidirectional, CPU→Memory), Control (mixed). All three are required for system operation.
- ✅ **Address bus of n bits → 2ⁿ locations.** Memory capacity = 2ⁿ × word size in bits.
- ✅ **Big-Endian:** MSB at lowest address. **Little-Endian:** LSB at lowest address.
- ✅ **Essential Registers:** PC, MAR, MBR, IR. PC holds *next* instruction address; MAR is the *memory access* register; MBR holds *data being transferred*; IR holds the *current instruction*.
- ✅ **Fetch cycle sequence:** `MAR ← PC → Memory Read → MBR ← Memory[MAR] → IR ← MBR → PC ← PC + 1`.
- ✅ **Instruction formats:** Three-address (RISC), Two-address (x86 typical), One-address (accumulator), Zero-address (stack).
- ✅ **Addressing modes** determine EA. Know formulas for: Immediate, Direct, Register, Indirect, Displacement, Indexed, Relative, Auto-inc/dec.
- ✅ **Branch EA** = (PC after increment) + offset. **Jump EA** = absolute target.
- ✅ **Three-bus CPU = 1-cycle ADD**; **Single-bus CPU = 3-cycle ADD**. More buses = faster but costlier hardware.
- ✅ **CPU Performance equation:** $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f_{clock}}$.
- ✅ **Control Unit** decodes IR opcode → generates timing signals to ALU, registers, memory, and bus.
- ✅ **Synchronous bus** uses a shared clock; **Asynchronous bus** uses handshaking (Req/Ack).
- ✅ **Endianness matters** for network protocols, file formats, and cross-architecture data sharing.
- ✅ **Always show micro-operations** step-by-step in CPU execution diagrams; KTU award partial marks for correct sequencing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Computer Organization vs Computer Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Computer Architecture** refers to the *programmer-visible* attributes of a computer system that affect the logical execution of a program. These include the instruction set, the number of bits used to represent data types, I/O mechanisms, and techniques for addressing memory.
> **Computer Organization** refers to the *operational units* and their interconnections that realize the architectural specifications. It includes control signals, interfaces, memory technology, and the use of pipelining/buses that are transparent to the programmer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Invariance Principle:** A family of computers may share the *same architecture* but differ in *organization*. Example: All Intel x86 processors share the same architecture but their internal organization (cache sizes, pipeline depth) varies across generations.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of architecture as the **blueprint of a building** — it specifies the number of rooms, doors, and windows. Organization is the **actual construction** — the type of bricks used, the plumbing layout, the wiring. Two buildings can have the same blueprint (architecture) but very different constructions (organization).

## 1.2 The Von Neumann Architecture (Stored Program Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> The **Von Neumann architecture** is a computer design model where *both program instructions and data* reside in a **single, shared memory** and are accessed over the **same bus**. Proposed by mathematician John von Neumann in **1945**, it is the foundation of nearly all modern general-purpose computers.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine a chef who keeps **all recipes and all ingredients in the same kitchen drawer**. Every time the chef needs to either read a recipe (instruction) or grab flour (data), they reach into the *same drawer* through the *same door*. Slow? Yes. Simple? Absolutely.

**The Von Neumann Bottleneck:**
Because instructions and data share one bus, the CPU cannot fetch an instruction and a data word simultaneously. This limits throughput. **Standard metric: a single shared memory port.**

## 1.3 The Harvard Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> The **Harvard architecture** uses *physically separate* storage and signal pathways for **instructions** and **data**. It requires two distinct memories (Instruction Memory + Data Memory) and two separate buses.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
The chef now has **two drawers** — one for recipes, one for ingredients. The chef can pull a recipe with one hand and flour with the other **simultaneously**. Much faster, but the kitchen is more complex and expensive.

## 1.4 Structural Comparison

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| Memory unit | Single shared memory | Separate I-memory & D-memory |
| Bus count | One (shared) | Two (separate) |
| Speed | Slower (bottleneck) | Faster (parallel access) |
| Cost / Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Used in | General-purpose CPUs (PCs, servers) | DSPs, microcontrollers (e.g., 8051, ARM Cortex-M) |
| Self-modifying code | Possible | Not possible |

## 1.5 Functional Units of a Computer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Fundamental Functional Units (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Input Unit** — accepts coded data from external sources (keyboard, mouse, sensors).
> 2. **Memory Unit** — stores both program instructions and data.
> 3. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations.
> 4. **Control Unit (CU)** — directs all other units; fetches, decodes, and sequences instructions.
> 5. **Output Unit** — returns processed results to the outside world (monitor, printer, actuators).

**Analogy:** The computer is a **restaurant kitchen** — Input = supplier delivery, ALU = cooking stove, Memory = pantry, Control Unit = head chef directing everyone, Output = food served to the customer.

## 1.6 Basic Operational Concept — The Instruction Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> Program execution consists of repeatedly executing the **Instruction Cycle**: **Fetch → Decode → Execute**. Each instruction in memory is fetched, its opcode is decoded, the required operands are located, the operation is performed, and the result is stored.

The clock signal — a periodic pulse with period $T_{clk}$ and frequency $f_{clk}$ — synchronizes every register transfer in the CPU. **Standard metric: clock period measured in nanoseconds (ns), frequency in Gigahertz (GHz).**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CPU Performance Equation (Most Important Derivation)

The total execution time of a program depends on three orthogonal factors:

- **Dynamic Instruction Count ($N$):** total instructions executed during the run.
- **Average Cycles per Instruction ($S$, or CPI):** average clock cycles consumed per instruction.
- **Clock Rate ($R$):** cycles per second (Hz) generated by the system clock.

The clock period is the reciprocal of the clock rate:

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{R}$$

The total CPU time for the program is the product of the total clock cycles and the clock period. We can expand total clock cycles as $N \times S$:

$$T_{CPU} = (N \times S) \times T_{clk}$$

Substituting $T_{clk} = 1/R$ gives the master performance equation used throughout the syllabus:

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{N \times S}{R}$$

**Why this matters (The 'How'):**
- Reducing $N$ → write a better algorithm or use a smarter compiler.
- Reducing $S$ → design a more efficient CPU (fewer cycles per instruction).
- Increasing $R$ → shrink transistors, deepen pipelining.

**Inverse Metric — Throughput in MIPS:**

$$MIPS = \frac{R}{S \times 10^6}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MIPS is NOT a reliable metric across machines** with different ISAs. Use it only as a relative indicator on the *same architecture*.

## 2.2 Amdahl's Law (Speedup with Optimization)

When we improve only a *fraction* $f$ of a task by a factor of $S$, the overall speedup is bounded:

$$Speedup = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{S}}$$

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**

- If $f = 1$ (entire task is improved by $S$): $\text{Speedup} = S$.
- If $S \to \infty$ (fraction becomes infinitely fast): $\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{1-f}$. The un-improved portion dominates.
- If $f = 0$ (nothing improved): $\text{Speedup} = 1$ (no benefit).

> [!NOTE]
> **Amdahl's Law Pitfall:** No matter how aggressively you optimize one part of a system, the *un-improved portion* sets a hard ceiling on overall speedup. This is why diminishing returns are inevitable in CPU design.

## 2.3 Memory Locations, Addresses, and Byte Ordering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every memory location has a unique **address**. A 32-bit machine can address $2^{32}$ bytes = **4 GB** of memory. A 64-bit machine can address $2^{64}$ bytes = **16 EB (Exabytes)**.

### Byte Ordering Conventions

- **Big-Endian:** The *most significant byte* (MSB) is stored at the *lowest* memory address. Used by Motorola, PowerPC, network protocols (TCP/IP).
- **Little-Endian:** The *least significant byte* (LSB) is stored at the *lowest* memory address. Used by Intel x86, AMD processors.

**Example — storing the 32-bit word `0x12345678` at address 1000:**

| Address | 1000 | 1001 | 1002 | 1003 |
|---------|------|------|------|------|
| Big-Endian | 0x12 | 0x34 | 0x56 | 0x78 |
| Little-Endian | 0x78 | 0x56 | 0x34 | 0x12 |

## 2.4 Interrupts and Interrupt Handling

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> An **interrupt** is a signal to the processor emitted by hardware or software indicating an event that needs immediate attention. The CPU suspends the current program, saves its state (PC, status registers) on the **stack**, executes an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, then restores state and resumes.

**Why interrupts matter in real engineering:** Embedded systems, real-time OS, and I/O devices rely on interrupts to handle asynchronous events without wasting CPU cycles on polling.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| Clock period | $T_{clk} = 1 / R$ | seconds |
| CPU execution time | $T_{CPU} = (N \times S) / R$ | seconds |
| MIPS rating | $MIPS = R / (S \times 10^{6})$ | millions of instr/sec |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = 1 / T_{CPU}$ | tasks / second |
| Amdahl's Speedup | $\text{Speedup} = 1 / [(1-f) + (f/S)]$ | dimensionless |
| Memory addressing | $N_{bytes} = 2^{k}$ for $k$-bit address | bytes |
| Speedup ceiling | $\lim_{S \to \infty} \text{Speedup} = 1/(1-f)$ | dimensionless |
| CPU clock cycles | $C = N \times S$ | cycles |
| Relative perf. ratio | $\text{Perf}_{A} / \text{Perf}_{B} = T_{B} / T_{A}$ | dimensionless |

> Use `\vert` for absolute value — e.g., $\vert T_A - T_B \vert$ — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CPU Performance Equation

**Goal:** Express the total CPU execution time $T_{CPU}$ in terms of instruction count, CPI, and clock rate.

**Step 1 — Start from clock period.**
By definition, the clock period $T_{clk}$ is the reciprocal of the clock frequency $R$ (measured in cycles per second, i.e., Hz).

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{R}$$

*Logic:* If the clock ticks 3 billion times per second ($R = 3$ GHz), then each tick lasts one three-billionth of a second.

**Step 2 — Express CPU time as total cycles multiplied by cycle time.**
Every instruction requires at least one clock cycle. If a program requires $C$ total clock cycles to finish, then:

$$T_{CPU} = C \times T_{clk}$$

*Logic:* Multiplying "how many cycles" by "how long each cycle takes" yields total wall-clock time.

**Step 3 — Expand total cycles using instruction count and CPI.**
Each instruction on average consumes $S$ clock cycles. If the dynamic instruction count is $N$, then:

$$C = N \times S$$

*Logic:* If a program executes 100 instructions and each takes 2 cycles on average, total cycles = 200.

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 2.**

$$T_{CPU} = (N \times S) \times T_{clk}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 1 to obtain the canonical CPU performance equation.**

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{N \times S}{R}$$

*Engineering Insight:* This single equation governs CPU design trade-offs. Doubling the clock rate ($R \times 2$) halves the runtime; halving the CPI ($S / 2$) also halves the runtime; rewriting the algorithm to use half the instructions ($N / 2$) again halves the runtime. The *fastest lever* depends on the system.

## 3.2 Derivation of Amdahl's Law

**Goal:** Show how much faster a system becomes when a fraction $f$ of its runtime is improved by a factor $S$.

**Step 1 — Define the original execution time.**
Let the total original execution time be $T_{original}$. The portion that will be optimized is $f \cdot T_{original}$. The remaining portion is $(1-f) \cdot T_{original}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the optimized portion's new time.**
The optimized fraction now runs $S$ times faster, so its new duration is:

$$T_{opt} = \frac{f \cdot T_{original}}{S}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the un-optimized portion's time (unchanged).**

$$T_{unopt} = (1 - f) \cdot T_{original}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two portions to get total optimized time.**

$$T_{new} = T_{opt} + T_{unopt}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and factor out $T_{original}$.**

$$T_{new} = \frac{f \cdot T_{original}}{S} + (1 - f) \cdot T_{original}$$

**Step 6 — Factor $T_{original}$.**

$$T_{new} = T_{original} \left[ (1 - f) + \frac{f}{S} \right]$$

**Step 7 — Speedup is the ratio $T_{original} / T_{new}$.**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{original}}{T_{new}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{S}}$$

*Engineering Insight:* As $S \to \infty$, the term $f/S \to 0$ and Speedup $\to 1/(1-f)$. Hence, if 25% of the program is non-optimizable, the *absolute maximum* speedup attainable is $1/0.75 \approx 1.33\times$, no matter what hardware you add.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Applying Amdahl's Law

> **Numerical Problem:** A program runs in **100 seconds** on a machine. **40%** of the time is spent in a floating-point routine. You replace the hardware so the FP routine runs **5× faster**. Find the overall speedup.

**Given:** $T_{original} = 100$ s, $f = 0.40$, $S = 5$.

**Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Law directly.**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \frac{0.40}{5}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the un-improved portion.**

$$1 - 0.40 = 0.60$$

**Step 3 — Compute the improved portion's contribution.**

$$\frac{0.40}{5} = 0.08$$

**Step 4 — Add them and invert.**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.08} = \frac{1}{0.68} \approx 1.47$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new execution time.**

$$T_{new} = \frac{T_{original}}{\text{Speedup}} = \frac{100}{1.47} \approx 68 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check using ceiling formula.**
Maximum possible speedup if FP became instantaneous ($S \to \infty$) is $1/(1 - 0.40) = 1.67\times$. Our actual speedup $1.47\times$ is correctly below this ceiling.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Equation Variants

| Variant | Formula | When Used |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| Standard CPU time | $T = N \times S / R$ | Generic execution time |
| MIPS rating | $MIPS = R / (S \times 10^{6})$ | Speed indicator (same ISA) |
| MFLOPS rating | $MFLOPS = (\text{FP ops}) / (T \times 10^{6})$ | Floating-point-heavy workloads |
| Speedup vs baseline | $S_{rel} = T_{base} / T_{new}$ | Comparing two implementations |
| Effective CPI | $S_{eff} = \sum_i (CPI_i \times IC_i) / IC_{total}$ | Mixed-instruction mixes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Computer System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the interaction between the **five functional units** via the **system bus** (data, address, and control lines).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode[INPUT UNIT\nKeyboard Mouse Sensors] --> sysBusNode[SYSTEM BUS\nData Address Control Lines]
    memNode[MEMORY UNIT\nRAM ROM Cache] --> sysBusNode
    aluNode[ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT\nALU Registers] --> sysBusNode
    cuNode[CONTROL UNIT\nInstruction Decoder Sequencer] --> sysBusNode
    outNode[OUTPUT UNIT\nMonitor Printer Actuators] --> sysBusNode

    sysBusNode --> cpuNode[CPU CORE\nALU plus CU plus Registers]

    classDef unit fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545;
    classDef bus fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#FB8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#5C2E00;
    class inputNode,memNode,aluNode,cuNode,outNode unit;
    class sysBusNode,cpuNode bus;
```

## 4.2 Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture — Side-by-Side Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph vN[Von Neumann Architecture]
        cpuVN[CPU] --> busVN[Single Shared Bus]
        busVN --> memVN[Memory Unit\nHolds BOTH Instructions AND Data]
        memVN --> busVN
    end

    subgraph hvN[Harvard Architecture]
        cpuHN[CPU] --> busIH[Instruction Bus]
        busIH --> memIH[Instruction Memory]
        cpuHN --> busDH[Data Bus]
        busDH --> memDH[Data Memory]
    end

    classDef vStyle fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#5F2120;
    classDef hStyle fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D652D;
    class cpuVN,busVN,memVN vStyle;
    class cpuHN,busIH,busDH,memIH,memDH hStyle;
```

## 4.3 Instruction Execution Cycle — Fetch-Decode-Execute Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Start Program] --> fetch1[Fetch Instruction from Memory]
    fetch1 --> decode1[Decode Instruction Opcode]
    decode1 --> operand1[Locate Operands in Registers or Memory]
    operand1 --> execute1[Execute Operation in ALU]
    execute1 --> store1[Store Result in Destination Register or Memory]
    store1 --> decision1{More Instructions to Execute?}
    decision1 -- Yes --> fetch1
    decision1 -- No --> haltNode[Halt Program]

    classDef stepStyle fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1;
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#3E2723;
    class fetch1,decode1,operand1,execute1,store1 stepStyle;
    class decision1,haltNode,startNode decisionStyle;
```

## 4.4 Interrupt Handling Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    runNode[CPU Executing Current Program] --> checkNode{Interrupt Signal Received?}
    checkNode -- No --> runNode
    checkNode -- Yes --> saveNode[Save PC and Status Registers to Stack]
    saveNode --> loadNode[Load ISR Address into PC]
    loadNode --> isrNode[Execute Interrupt Service Routine]
    isrNode --> restoreNode[Restore Saved PC and Status from Stack]
    restoreNode --> runNode

    classDef flowNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92;
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C;
    class runNode,saveNode,loadNode,isrNode,restoreNode flowNode;
    class checkNode decisionNode;
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Memory Subsystem Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpuCore[CPU Core] --> l1[L1 Cache\nFew KB\nFastest]
    l1 --> l2[L2 Cache\nHundreds of KB]
    l2 --> l3[L3 Cache\nSeveral MB]
    l3 --> ramNode[Main Memory RAM\nGigabytes]
    ramNode --> diskNode[Secondary Storage SSD or HDD\nTerabytes]
    diskNode --> archiveNode[Archival Storage Tape or Cloud\nPetabytes]

    classDef tier1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef tier2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef tier3 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef tier4 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef tier5 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef tier6 fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0,stroke-width:2px;
    class cpuCore,tier1 tier1;
    class l1,tier2 tier2;
    class l2,tier3 tier3;
    class l3,tier4 tier4;
    class ramNode,tier5 tier5;
    class diskNode,tier6 tier6;
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — **Q1 (3 Marks)**
> **Define Von Neumann architecture. List its two main characteristics and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**
Von Neumann architecture is a stored-program computer design in which **both instructions and data reside in a single shared memory** and are accessed over the **same bus**. Proposed by John von Neumann in 1945.

*Two key characteristics:*
1. Single memory for instructions and data.
2. Sequential fetch-decode-execute cycle controlled by the Control Unit.

*One limitation:*
The **Von Neumann bottleneck** — since instructions and data share one bus, the CPU cannot fetch both simultaneously, limiting throughput.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** — **Q2 (3 Marks)**
> **Differentiate between Big-Endian and Little-Endian byte ordering with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
Byte ordering refers to how multi-byte data is stored in memory.

- **Big-Endian:** Stores the *most significant byte* (MSB) at the *lowest memory address*.
- **Little-Endian:** Stores the *least significant byte* (LSB) at the *lowest memory address*.

*Example:* Storing the 16-bit value `0x4A2C` at address 2000:

| Address | 2000 | 2001 |
|---------|------|------|
| Big-Endian | 0x4A | 0x2C |
| Little-Endian | 0x2C | 0x4A |

Intel x86 uses Little-Endian; PowerPC and network byte order use Big-Endian.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Module 1 Internal Choice]**

### ✅ Choice A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — Explain the basic functional units of a computer system with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A computer system is organized into **five fundamental functional units** that work together to execute programs:

1. **Input Unit:** Accepts raw data and instructions from external devices such as keyboards, mice, scanners, and sensors. Converts these into binary form suitable for internal processing. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Memory Unit (Storage):** Stores both the *program instructions* and the *data* being processed. It includes primary storage (RAM, ROM, cache) and secondary storage (SSD, HDD). Memory is organized as an array of *addressable words*, each with a unique binary address. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs all arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, XOR). Operands are loaded from registers or memory, and the result is stored back. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Control Unit (CU):** Acts as the *central coordinator*. It fetches instructions from memory, decodes them to determine the operation, and generates the appropriate *control signals* that direct the ALU, registers, and memory to execute the instruction. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Output Unit:** Presents the processed results to the outside world via monitors, printers, LEDs, or actuators. Converts internal binary representations into human-readable form. *(1 mark)*

A block diagram should be drawn showing the CPU (containing ALU + CU + Registers) connected to Memory, Input, and Output via the **system bus**. *(Diagram mandatory for full marks.)*

**Part (b) — Derive the CPU performance equation. Discuss the factors affecting CPU performance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The CPU performance equation is derived as follows. Let:

- $N$ = dynamic instruction count (total instructions executed).
- $S$ = average cycles per instruction (CPI).
- $R$ = clock rate in Hz (cycles per second).

The clock period is the reciprocal of the clock rate:

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{R}$$

The total number of clock cycles consumed by the program is $N \times S$ (each of the $N$ instructions takes $S$ cycles on average). Multiplying by the clock period gives total execution time:

$$T_{CPU} = (N \times S) \times T_{clk}$$

Substituting $T_{clk} = 1/R$:

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{N \times S}{R}$$

*Factors affecting CPU performance (3 marks discussion):*

1. **Instruction Count ($N$):** Determined by the *algorithm*, the *programming language*, the *compiler*, and the *ISA*. A well-designed algorithm can reduce $N$ dramatically (e.g., $O(n \log n)$ sort vs $O(n^2)$ sort).

2. **CPI ($S$):** Determined by the *CPU organization* — pipelining, cache hit rates, branch prediction, and instruction complexity (RISC vs CISC). A modern pipelined RISC processor aims for CPI ≈ 1; a non-pipelined CISC may have CPI > 4.

3. **Clock Rate ($R$):** Determined by the *technology* (transistor switching speed), *pipelining depth*, and *power/thermal limits*. As $R$ increases, so does power dissipation ($P \propto C \times V^2 \times f$), leading to the *power wall*.

*Conclusion:* Performance is a *product* of all three factors. Optimizing any one alone yields diminishing returns; balanced improvement across all three is the designer's goal.

---

### ✅ Choice B (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — Explain Von Neumann and Harvard architectures. Compare them with a comparative table. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Von Neumann Architecture (3 marks):**
In the Von Neumann model, both instructions and data are stored in a **single shared memory**, accessed via a **single bus**. The CPU alternates between fetching an instruction and fetching or writing data. Proposed in 1945, it underpins almost all general-purpose CPUs (e.g., Intel Core i7). The main drawback is the **Von Neumann bottleneck** — the bus can only carry one item at a time, limiting throughput.

**Harvard Architecture (3 marks):**
The Harvard model uses **two physically separate memories** — one for instructions, one for data — and **two separate buses**. The CPU can fetch an instruction and a data operand simultaneously, doubling memory bandwidth for the instruction fetch phase. Used in DSPs, microcontrollers (8051, ARM Cortex-M), and high-speed signal processing. Its drawback is *higher cost* and *complexity*.

**Comparative Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| Memory units | 1 (shared) | 2 (instruction + data) |
| Buses | 1 | 2 |
| Parallel I/D access | No | Yes |
| Speed | Slower (bottleneck) | Faster |
| Cost / Complexity | Low | High |
| Self-modifying code | Allowed | Not possible |
| Examples | Intel x86, AMD Ryzen | 8051, ARM Cortex-M, DSPs |

**Part (b) — State and derive Amdahl's Law. A program runs in 200 seconds on a processor. Floating-point instructions account for 30% of the execution time. You replace the FP unit with one that is 10× faster. Find the new execution time and overall speedup. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Amdahl's Law (3 marks derivation):**
Let the original execution time be $T$. A fraction $f$ of this time is spent in code that can be optimized. After optimization, that fraction runs $S$ times faster, taking $fT/S$ instead of $fT$. The remaining $(1-f)T$ is unchanged. The new total time is:

$$T_{new} = \frac{f \cdot T}{S} + (1 - f) \cdot T = T \left[ (1 - f) + \frac{f}{S} \right]$$

The overall speedup is $T / T_{new}$:

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{S}}$$

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**
Given: $T = 200$ s, $f = 0.30$, $S = 10$.

Compute the optimized and un-optimized portions:

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \frac{0.30}{10}}$$

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.03} = \frac{1}{0.73} \approx 1.3699$$

New execution time:

$$T_{new} = \frac{T}{\text{Speedup}} = \frac{200}{1.3699} \approx 146 \text{ seconds}$$

**Sanity Check:** The maximum possible speedup (if FP became instantaneous) is $1/0.70 \approx 1.43\times$. Our result $1.37\times$ is correctly below this ceiling. The un-optimized 70% of the program sets the hard limit on gains.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mismatched Units in CPU Performance Equation:** Students often mix seconds, milliseconds, and MHz/GHz. Always convert clock rate to Hz (or GHz) and CPI to cycles/instruction consistently.
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert MIPS:** When using $MIPS = R / (S \times 10^6)$, ensure $R$ is in Hz. A common error is plugging in MHz directly.
> 3. **Amdahl's Law Misinterpretation:** Students sometimes compute Speedup = $S$ instead of using the formula. The optimization must be applied *only to the fraction $f$*.
> 4. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** In questions on functional units, examiners explicitly demand a diagram. Missing it costs at least 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Confusing Architecture vs Organization:** Architecture is the programmer's view (ISA, addressing modes); organization is the implementation (cache, pipeline). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Architecture** = programmer-visible attributes (ISA, addressing modes, data types).
- **Organization** = hardware implementation details (cache, pipeline, control signals).
- **Von Neumann** = single shared memory, single bus, bottleneck exists.
- **Harvard** = separate I and D memories, parallel access, used in DSPs and MCUs.
- **Five functional units:** Input, Output, Memory, ALU, Control Unit.
- **Clock period** $T_{clk} = 1/R$; **Clock rate** $R = 1/T_{clk}$.
- **Master CPU performance equation:** $T_{CPU} = (N \times S) / R$.
- **MIPS rating:** $MIPS = R / (S \times 10^6)$ — *same-ISA only*.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $\text{Speedup} = 1 / [(1-f) + (f/S)]$.
- **Amdahl's ceiling:** As $S \to \infty$, $\text{Speedup} \to 1/(1-f)$.
- **Big-Endian** = MSB at lowest address (PowerPC, network order).
- **Little-Endian** = LSB at lowest address (Intel x86).
- **Interrupt** = asynchronous signal that suspends the current program to run an ISR.
- **Interrupt handling steps:** Save state → Load ISR address → Execute ISR → Restore state → Resume.
- **Memory hierarchy (top to bottom speed):** Registers → L1 Cache → L2 Cache → L3 Cache → RAM → SSD/HDD → Tape/Cloud.
- **Instruction cycle:** Fetch → Decode → Operand Fetch → Execute → Store Result → Repeat.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Instruction Cycle** (also called the **Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle** or **Instruction Processing Cycle**) is the complete operational sequence performed by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) to fetch a single machine-language instruction from primary memory, decode it to determine the required operation, execute the operation, and store the result. It is the fundamental, atomic cycle of operation that the processor repeats continuously for every instruction in a program.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Canonical):** The instruction cycle is the set of micro-operations performed by the CPU to execute a single computer instruction. It consists of the **Fetch Cycle**, the **Decode Phase**, the **Effective Address Calculation Phase**, the **Execution Phase**, and optionally the **Interrupt Cycle**.

In a stored-program computer (von Neumann architecture), every program — whether a compiler, a web browser, or a kernel module — is executed as an uninterrupted, sequential stream of instruction cycles. The CPU has no concept of "high-level" code; it is a state machine that loops through the instruction cycle billions of times per second.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Chef

Imagine a **head chef in a kitchen** reading a recipe book (memory) to prepare a meal (the program output). For each line of the recipe:

1. **Fetch** — The chef walks to the recipe book, opens it to the page indicated by the order ticket, and copies the next line onto a notepad.
2. **Decode** — The chef reads the notepad and understands what action the line demands (chop onions, boil water, plate the dish).
3. **Operand Fetch / Effective Address Calculation** — If the recipe says "the salt from cabinet 3," the chef must walk to cabinet 3 and bring the salt.
4. **Execute** — The chef performs the action (chop, boil, mix).
5. **Store Result** — The chef places the prepared item in the serving area.
6. **Update Instruction Pointer** — The chef moves the order ticket down one line and repeats.

The chef's **notepad** is the CPU's **Instruction Register (IR)**, the **recipe book** is **Main Memory**, the **order ticket** is the **Program Counter (PC)**, and the **kitchen counter** is the **Accumulator/Registers**. The chef's repetitive loop is the **Instruction Cycle**.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Understanding the instruction cycle is non-negotiable for the following KTU-mapped engineering domains:
> - **Performance Engineering** — CPI (Cycles Per Instruction), MIPS, and Amdahl's Law are all derived from the instruction cycle.
> - **Pipelining & Parallelism** — Without an intimate knowledge of the cycle, you cannot design pipelined, superscalar, or multi-core processors.
> - **Embedded Systems** — Real-time interrupt response is governed directly by the **Interrupt Cycle** sub-phase.
> - **Compilers & ISA Design** — Every addressing mode and instruction format is a direct consequence of micro-operations inside the cycle.

The instruction cycle is, in essence, the **heartbeat of every general-purpose computer**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Sub-Phases

The instruction cycle is decomposed into a strictly ordered set of **micro-operations**. For a generic single-bus CPU organization, the cycle is described in five logical sub-phases:

| # | Sub-Phase | Primary Purpose | Key Registers Used |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Fetch Phase** | Retrieve next instruction from memory at address held in PC | PC, MAR, MBR, IR |
| 2 | **Decode Phase** | Interpret opcode bits of the instruction in IR | IR, Decoder, Control Unit |
| 3 | **Effective Address Calculation** | Resolve operand address using addressing mode rules | IR, addressing-mode hardware |
| 4 | **Execute Phase** | Perform arithmetic, logic, data transfer, or control operation | ALU, registers, MDR |
| 5 | **Interrupt Cycle** (optional) | Service pending hardware interrupts before next fetch | PC, stack pointer, ISR vector |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Focus:** The order *Fetch → Decode → Execute → Interrupt* is inviolable. Examiners frequently ask students to list the micro-operations **in this exact sequence**.

## 2.2 Phase 1 — The Fetch Phase (The Most Critical Phase)

The fetch phase is **identical for every instruction**, regardless of type. This is a foundational KTU 2024 concept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fetch phase is **instruction-agnostic** — it does not know or care whether the next instruction is `ADD`, `JMP`, or `MOV`. This uniformity is what enables the CPU to be a simple, deterministic state machine.

The fetch phase is governed by three canonical micro-operations performed in three clock cycles (T-states) for a single-bus CPU:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &: \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC} \\
T_1 &: \text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]} \;;\; \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1 \\
T_2 &: \text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MBR} \;;\; \text{GoTo } T_3 \text{ for decode}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each step:**
- **T₀ (Address Transfer):** The contents of the Program Counter (which holds the address of the next instruction) are copied to the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**. This places the instruction's address on the memory bus.
- **T₁ (Memory Read & PC Increment):** Memory places the instruction word on the data bus, which is latched into the **Memory Buffer Register (MBR)**. Simultaneously, the PC is incremented so that the *next* fetch will retrieve the next sequential instruction.
- **T₂ (Instruction Register Load):** The instruction in MBR is transferred to the **Instruction Register (IR)**, where it is held for decoding and execution.

## 2.3 Phase 2 — The Decode Phase

In T₃, the opcode field of the IR is fed to the **Instruction Decoder**, which is a combinational logic circuit. The decoder activates a unique output line corresponding to the opcode. This line drives the **Control Unit's** micro-operation sequencer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Note:** In CISC processors (like x86), decoding is complex (multi-byte variable-length opcodes). In RISC processors (like ARM/RISC-V), decoding is hardwired and fast — typically completed in a single clock cycle.

## 2.4 Phase 3 — Effective Address Calculation

Many instructions operate on data that is **not** in a register. The address of that data (the *effective address*) must be computed from fields of the instruction. Common addressing modes include:
- **Direct** — EA = Address field of instruction.
- **Indirect** — EA = M[Address field].
- **Indexed** — EA = Address field + Index Register.
- **Relative** — EA = PC + Offset (used in branch instructions).

## 2.5 Phase 4 — The Execute Phase (Type-Dependent)

The execution phase **varies** based on the instruction class. There are four primary instruction categories recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Instruction Class | Micro-Operation Summary | Examples |
|-------------------|------------------------|----------|
| **Memory-Reference (MR)** | Compute EA, then read/write M[EA] | `LDA`, `STA`, `ADD M` |
| **Register-Reference (RR)** | Operate directly on registers (no memory) | `MOV`, `INC`, `CMP` |
| **Input/Output (IO)** | Initiate I/O transfer via I/O module | `IN`, `OUT` |
| **Branch/Control** | Modify PC if condition is satisfied | `JMP`, `JZ`, `CALL` |

## 2.6 Phase 5 — The Interrupt Cycle

After execution, the CPU checks the **Interrupt Request (IRQ) line**. If asserted, the CPU:
1. Saves the current PC (next instruction's address) onto the system stack.
2. Loads PC with the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) address from the interrupt vector table.
3. Begins fetching from the ISR — effectively starting a *new* instruction cycle dedicated to the interrupt.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Metrics for KTU 2024 Numerical Questions:**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|--------|--------------------|----------------|
| $T_{\text{cycle}}$ | Time period of one clock pulse | seconds |
| $f_{\text{clock}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | Hz (typical: 1–5 GHz) |
| $N_{\text{steps}}$ | Number of T-states for one instruction (typical 3–7) | dimensionless |
| $T_{\text{inst}}$ | $N_{\text{steps}} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$ | seconds |
| $\text{CPI}$ | Cycles Per Instruction (mean over a program) | dimensionless |
| $\text{IPS}$ | $\dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI}}$ | instructions / second |
| $\text{MIPS}$ | $\dfrac{\text{IPS}}{10^6}$ | millions of IPS |
| $\text{Throughput}$ | $\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{inst}}}$ | instructions / second |
| $\text{Performance Ratio}$ | $\dfrac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}}$ | dimensionless |

> **Real-world utility:** These metrics directly power benchmark scores (Dhrystone, CoreMark), and the per-instruction cycle count is the master key used by hardware engineers to size processor pipelines and select cache hierarchies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Complete Micro-Operation Trace for a Generic Single-Bus CPU

We will now derive, step-by-step, the complete micro-operation sequence for a generic single-bus CPU executing a three-address instruction of the form:
$$\text{ADD} \; R_1, \; X \; \text{(where X is a memory operand)}$$

We assume a **single internal bus** architecture (à la Hamacher/Vranesic textbook, the KTU reference text). Each clock cycle can perform exactly **one** bus transfer.

### Phase 1: Fetch (T₀ to T₂)

**Step 1 — T₀: Address out, no operation yet.**
The PC's value is needed at the MAR, but the bus is shared. The CPU drives PC's value onto the bus and latches it into MAR.
$$\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}$$
*Logic:* The Control Unit activates $\text{PC}_{\text{out}}$ and $\text{MAR}_{\text{in}}$ control signals. This places the address of the next instruction on the address lines.

**Step 2 — T₁: Memory Read + PC Increment (single-bus constraint).**
On a single-bus CPU, we cannot simultaneously read memory and increment PC using the bus twice. We split: first read memory, then increment PC.
$$\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}$$
$$\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$$
*Logic:* Memory places the instruction word on the data bus; it is latched into MBR. Then ALU increments PC. Both must fit into T₁.

**Step 3 — T₂: Transfer MBR to IR + Begin decode.**
$$\text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MBR}$$
*Logic:* The instruction is now safely in IR. The decoder begins combinational analysis of the opcode bits.

### Phase 2: Decode (T₃)

**Step 4 — T₃: Opcode decode (no bus transfer needed).**
The Control Unit inspects the opcode and determines that this is an `ADD` to register $R_1$ with a memory operand. The decoder's output activates the relevant control signals for the execute phase.

### Phase 3: Effective Address Calculation (T₄ to T₅)

**Step 5 — T₄: Address field of IR → MAR.**
The address portion of the IR (where X is stored in memory) is sent to MAR. The bus is needed for this transfer.
$$\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{IR}(\text{address field})$$

**Step 6 — T₅: Memory Read for operand → MBR.**
$$\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}$$
*Logic:* The operand X is now in MBR.

### Phase 4: Execute (T₆)

**Step 7 — T₆: Add and store back.**
$$R_1 \leftarrow R_1 + \text{MBR}$$
*Logic:* The ALU adds the contents of R₁ and MBR; the result is stored back into R₁.

**Total T-states: 7 clock cycles** for this specific ADD-with-memory instruction on a single-bus CPU.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CPI and Execution Time Calculation

Given a program of $N$ instructions with a mean CPI of $\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}$ and clock frequency $f_{\text{clock}}$:

**Step 1 — Total clock cycles:**
$$T_{\text{cycles}} = N \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}$$

**Step 2 — Total execution time:**
Since each clock cycle has period $T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clock}}}$:
$$T_{\text{exec}} = T_{\text{cycles}} \times T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{N \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}}{f_{\text{clock}}}$$

**Step 3 — Instruction throughput (instructions per second):**
$$\text{IPS} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{exec}} / N} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}}$$

**Step 4 — MIPS rating:**
$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times 10^6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A 3 GHz processor with CPI = 1.0 achieves exactly 3000 MIPS. With CPI = 2.0, it drops to 1500 MIPS.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — T-State Count by Instruction Class

The following matrix is **examination-critical**. It summarizes the number of T-states per instruction class for a single-bus CPU (KTU-standard textbook organization):

| Instruction Class | Fetch | Decode | Address Calc. | Execute | Interrupt | Total T-states |
|-------------------|-------|--------|---------------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| **Direct Memory Add** | T₀–T₂ (3) | T₃ (1) | T₄–T₅ (2) | T₆ (1) | T₇ (1) | **7** |
| **Register-Register Add** | T₀–T₂ (3) | T₃ (1) | 0 | T₄ (1) | T₅ (1) | **5** |
| **Indirect Memory Load** | T₀–T₂ (3) | T₃ (1) | T₄–T₅ (2) | T₆–T₇ (2) | T₈ (1) | **8** |
| **Branch (Unconditional)** | T₀–T₂ (3) | T₃ (1) | 0 | T₄ (1) | T₅ (1) | **5** |
| **Branch (Conditional, Taken)** | T₀–T₂ (3) | T₃ (1) | 0 | T₄–T₅ (2) | T₆ (1) | **6** |
| **I/O Instruction** | T₀–T₂ (3) | T₃ (1) | 0 | T₄ (1) | T₅ (1) | **5** |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Performance Comparison Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked KTU-style numerical:** A processor runs at 2.5 GHz. A program contains 1,000,000 instructions with the following mix: 40% memory-reference (7 cycles), 30% register-reference (5 cycles), 20% conditional branch taken (6 cycles), 10% I/O (5 cycles).

**Step 1 — Mean CPI calculation:**
$$\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \sum_i (f_i \times \text{CPI}_i)$$
$$\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = (0.40 \times 7) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 5)$$
$$\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = 2.80 + 1.50 + 1.20 + 0.50 = 6.00$$

**Step 2 — Total execution time:**
$$T_{\text{exec}} = \dfrac{N \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}}{f_{\text{clock}}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 \times 6.00}{2.5 \times 10^9}$$
$$T_{\text{exec}} = \dfrac{6{,}000{,}000}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — MIPS rating:**
$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^9}{6.00 \times 10^6} \approx 416.67 \;\text{MIPS}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This problem is **the exact KTU 2024 model**. Memorize the three-step methodology: (1) compute weighted CPI, (2) compute execution time, (3) compute MIPS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure A — Top-Level Flowchart of the Instruction Cycle

The following Mermaid block renders the **canonical instruction-cycle state machine** as a board-style flowchart. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compiler safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1([Processor Reset]) --> n2[Fetch Phase T0 to T2]
    n2 --> n3[Decode Phase T3]
    n3 --> n4{Interrupt Pending Question}
    n4 -->|Yes| n5[Interrupt Cycle Service ISR]
    n4 -->|No| n6{Instruction Type Decode}
    n5 --> n6
    n6 -->|Memory Reference| n7[Effective Address Calculation T4 T5]
    n6 -->|Register Reference| n8[Register Operands Read T4]
    n6 -->|I O Reference| n9[I O Bus Transfer T4]
    n6 -->|Branch Control| n10[Evaluate Branch Condition T4]
    n7 --> n11[Memory Read or Write T6 T7]
    n8 --> n12[ALU Operation on Registers T5]
    n9 --> n13[Complete I O Handshake T5]
    n10 --> n14[Update PC if Branch Taken T5 T6]
    n11 --> n15[Go To Next Instruction]
    n12 --> n15
    n13 --> n15
    n14 --> n15
    n15 --> n2
```

**Reading the diagram:** The processor begins at `n1` (reset) and immediately enters `n2` (Fetch). The instruction in IR is decoded, an interrupt check is performed, and the cycle branches to the appropriate execute path. Every path converges back to the fetch phase, illustrating the **infinite loop** that defines the processor's operational model.

## 4.2 Figure B — Block-Level CPU Organization for a Single-Bus Architecture

This Mermaid block depicts the **internal data-path connectivity** of a single-bus CPU, showing which registers connect to the common bus and how the ALU is fed. The block layout is the standard KTU/ECE-board schematic style.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_BOX [CPU Single Bus Organization]
        pc[PC Program Counter]
        mar[MAR Memory Address Register]
        mbr[MBR Memory Buffer Register]
        ir[IR Instruction Register]
        r1[Register R1]
        r2[Register R2]
        alu[ALU Arithmetic Logic Unit]
        ac[AC Accumulator]
    end
    subgraph MEM_BOX [Main Memory]
        m[M Memory Array]
    end
    subgraph CTRL_BOX [Control Unit]
        cu[Control Sequencer and Decoder]
    end
    bus((Common Internal Bus))
    pc --- bus
    mar --- bus
    mbr --- bus
    ir --- bus
    r1 --- bus
    r2 --- bus
    alu --- bus
    ac --- bus
    m -- Address Lines --> mar
    m -- Data Lines --> mbr
    cu -.->|Control Signals| pc
    cu -.->|Control Signals| mar
    cu -.->|Control Signals| mbr
    cu -.->|Control Signals| ir
    cu -.->|Control Signals| alu
```

**Key observation:** Because there is only one internal bus, only **one register-to-register transfer** can occur per clock cycle. This single-bus limitation is the reason why the fetch phase requires multiple T-states (3 instead of 1) and is the architectural bottleneck that pipelining and multi-bus designs aim to overcome.

## 4.3 Figure C — Timing Diagram Representation of a Single Instruction Cycle

The following Mermaid sequence diagram shows the **temporal ordering** of control signals during one complete instruction cycle. Each lane is a register, and arrows represent bus transfers.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CU as Control Unit
    participant PC as Program Counter
    participant MAR as Memory Address Register
    participant MEM as Main Memory
    participant MBR as Memory Buffer Register
    participant IR as Instruction Register
    participant ALU as ALU
    participant R1 as Register R1

    Note over CU,IR: Phase 1 Fetch Cycle
    CU->>PC: Activate PCout
    CU->>MAR: Activate MARin
    Note right of MAR: T0 MAR receives PC
    CU->>MEM: Read Enable HIGH
    MEM-->>MBR: Instruction Word Placed on Bus
    CU->>PC: Increment PC
    Note right of MBR: T1 MBR receives Memory
    CU->>IR: Activate IRin
    Note right of IR: T2 IR receives MBR

    Note over CU,ALU: Phase 2 Decode
    CU->>IR: Decode Opcode
    Note right of IR: T3 Decoder Activates

    Note over CU,R1: Phase 3 Effective Address
    CU->>IR: Read Address Field
    CU->>MAR: Activate MARin
    Note right of MAR: T4 MAR receives EA
    MEM-->>MBR: Operand X
    Note right of MBR: T5 MBR receives X

    Note over CU,R1: Phase 4 Execute
    CU->>R1: Activate R1out
    CU->>ALU: Select ADD Operation
    ALU-->>R1: Result Stored
    Note right of R1: T6 R1 receives R1 plus MBR
```

**Engineering interpretation:** The diagram reveals the **critical path** of the instruction cycle. Notice that memory operations dominate the cycle time. This is why **cache memory** is placed between the CPU and main memory — to shorten this critical path and dramatically reduce CPI.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]** Define the **Instruction Cycle**. List its main phases in the order they are executed by the CPU.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Instruction Cycle** is the complete sequence of micro-operations performed by the Central Processing Unit to execute a single machine instruction. It begins when the CPU fetches the instruction from memory and ends when the result has been stored and the processor is ready to fetch the next instruction.
>
> The main phases, in canonical order, are:
> 1. **Fetch Phase** — Retrieve the instruction from memory at the address held in the PC.
> 2. **Decode Phase** — Interpret the opcode bits in the Instruction Register.
> 3. **Effective Address Calculation Phase** — Compute the address of any memory operand.
> 4. **Execute Phase** — Perform the operation dictated by the opcode.
> 5. **Interrupt Cycle** — Service any pending hardware interrupt before the next fetch.

**Mark distribution (typical KTU scheme):** Definition (1 mark) + Enumeration with brief description (2 marks).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary Paper]** What is the **Fetch Phase**? Why is it considered **identical for every instruction**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Fetch Phase** is the first sub-phase of the instruction cycle. It retrieves the next instruction from main memory and loads it into the Instruction Register. The three canonical micro-operations are: $\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}$, $\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}$ (with $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$), and $\text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MBR}$.
>
> It is **identical for every instruction** because at the time of fetching, the CPU does not yet know what the instruction is. The PC always points to the next instruction regardless of whether it is an `ADD`, `JMP`, or `LOAD`. This uniformity is a foundational property of the von Neumann stored-program model and simplifies the hardware control logic. **(1.5 marks for the micro-operations + 1.5 marks for the explanation of uniformity.)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam — Module 1, Q1]** Explain the **complete instruction cycle** of a single-bus CPU with the help of a **neat block diagram** and **Register Transfer Language (RTL)** statements. Show the **micro-operations** for (a) a memory-reference instruction and (b) a register-reference instruction.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Memory-Reference Instruction — 7 marks**

The instruction format is: $\text{Opcode} \; \vert \; \text{Address}$ (15-bit address, 1-bit opcode area in our simple model).

*Step 1 — Fetch Phase:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &: \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC} \\
T_1 &: \text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]} \;;\; \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1 \\
T_2 &: \text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MBR}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Decode Phase:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &: \text{Decode IR}(0\text{–}11) \;;\; \text{D}_0 \text{–} \text{D}_7 \leftarrow \text{Decode opcode}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Effective Address Calculation (Direct Addressing):*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_4 &: \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{IR}(\text{address}) \\
T_5 &: \text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Execute Phase (e.g., ADD to AC):*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_6 &: \text{AC} \leftarrow \text{AC} + \text{MBR}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — Interrupt Check:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_7 &: \text{If INT = 1, then } \text{PC} \leftarrow 0 \text{ (or vector addr) and } \text{GoTo Fetch}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total: 7 T-states.** Block diagram: refer to Figure B in Section 4.

**(b) Register-Reference Instruction — 7 marks**

The instruction format is: $\text{Opcode} = 0$ (leftmost bit) plus a register operation code in bits 12–14. The address field (bits 0–11) is repurposed to encode the register operation (e.g., `CLA`, `CMA`, `INC`, `HLT`).

*Step 1 — Fetch Phase:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &: \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC} \\
T_1 &: \text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]} \;;\; \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1 \\
T_2 &: \text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MBR}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Decode Phase:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &: \text{IR}(15) = 0 \implies \text{Register-Reference Instruction} \;;\; \text{Decode IR}(12\text{–}14)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Execute Phase (example: CLA — Clear Accumulator):*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_4 &: \text{AC} \leftarrow 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Interrupt Check:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_5 &: \text{If INT = 1, then service interrupt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total: 5 T-states.** The execution is faster because no memory operand fetch is required — the operation is performed entirely on internal registers.

**Final integrated answer must include:** (1) Block diagram, (2) RTL for fetch, (3) RTL for memory-reference, (4) RTL for register-reference, (5) Justification for different T-state counts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary — Module 1, Q2 Alternative]** (a) Explain the **Interrupt Cycle** of a processor with a suitable block diagram and RTL. (b) A processor has a clock frequency of **2 GHz**. A program consists of **500,000 instructions** with a mean CPI of **3.5**. Calculate the **execution time** and the **MIPS rating**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Interrupt Cycle — 7 marks**

The interrupt cycle is invoked after the **Execute Phase** of the current instruction, but **before** the next fetch. It allows the CPU to respond to asynchronous external events (I/O completion, hardware faults, timer ticks).

*Block Diagram:*
The interrupt cycle requires additional logic — an **Interrupt Request (INT) flip-flop** set by external hardware, an **Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA)** signal from the CPU, and a **stack pointer** to save state.

*RTL of the Interrupt Cycle:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &: \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC} \;;\; \text{PC} \leftarrow 0 \text{ (or ISR vector address from interrupt controller)} \\
T_1 &: \text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]} \;;\; \text{Stack} \leftarrow \text{old PC} \\
T_2 &: \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{MBR} \;;\; \text{GoTo Fetch}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Flow logic:*
1. CPU checks the **INT line** at the end of every instruction.
2. If INT = 1, the CPU saves the current value of PC (the address of the next instruction to be executed) onto the **system stack**.
3. The CPU loads PC with the starting address of the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** from a pre-defined memory location (interrupt vector).
4. The CPU then begins fetching instructions from the ISR — effectively transferring control to the OS kernel's interrupt handler.
5. After the ISR completes, a **Return from Interrupt (RTI)** instruction pops the saved PC back from the stack, and the original program resumes.

*Significance:* The interrupt cycle makes I/O, multitasking, and real-time response possible. Without it, the CPU would have to "poll" devices continuously, wasting cycles.

**(b) Numerical Calculation — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Clock frequency: $f_{\text{clock}} = 2 \;\text{GHz} = 2 \times 10^9 \;\text{Hz}$
- Number of instructions: $N = 500{,}000 = 5 \times 10^5$
- Mean CPI: $\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = 3.5$

**Step 1 — Total clock cycles:**
$$T_{\text{cycles}} = N \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 3.5 = 17.5 \times 10^5 = 1.75 \times 10^6 \;\text{cycles}$$

**Step 2 — Total execution time:**
$$T_{\text{exec}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{cycles}}}{f_{\text{clock}}} = \dfrac{1.75 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.875 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{seconds}$$
$$T_{\text{exec}} = 875 \;\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — MIPS rating:**
$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times 10^6} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^9}{3.5 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{2000}{3.5}$$
$$\text{MIPS} \approx 571.43$$

**Step 4 — Throughput (bonus):**
$$\text{IPS} = \dfrac{N}{T_{\text{exec}}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^5}{8.75 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 5.71 \times 10^8 \;\text{IPS}$$

**Final Answer:** Execution time = **0.875 ms**, MIPS = **571.43**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (as flagged by KTU 2024 board examiners):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the PC increment during fetch.** Students often write $\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}$ but forget the simultaneous $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$. **This alone costs 1 mark** in 7-mark questions.
>
> 2. **Confusing the Interrupt Cycle with the Execute Cycle.** The interrupt cycle is a **separate, conditional sub-phase** that occurs *after* execute, not *during* it. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this mix-up.
>
> 3. **Wrong units in numerical answers.** MIPS must be a *number*, not "MIPS" with units of seconds. Always show the division by $10^6$ explicitly.
>
> 4. **Omitting the block diagram in 14-mark questions.** The block diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself. A textual description alone is insufficient.
>
> 5. **Using pipe symbols in RTL.** KTU answer scripts must use the conventional notation $\text{Reg} \leftarrow \text{Source}$. Writing $\text{Reg} = \text{Source}$ is acceptable but $\text{Reg} := \text{Source}$ is ambiguous and may lose a mark.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Effective Address Calculation phase.** For direct-memory instructions, students often jump straight to execution. The 2-cycle address-calculation step is **mandatory** in any complete answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Instruction Cycle (Module 1)**

- ✅ The **Instruction Cycle** = Fetch → Decode → Execute → Interrupt (and loop).
- ✅ The **Fetch Phase** is identical for every instruction (3 T-states on a single-bus CPU).
- ✅ The three canonical fetch micro-operations are: $\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}$, $\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}$ (+ PC increment), and $\text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MBR}$.
- ✅ **Decode** is purely combinational — no bus transfer is needed; one T-state.
- ✅ **Effective Address Calculation** is required for memory-reference instructions only.
- ✅ **Execute phase duration** depends on instruction class: Register ops are fastest (1–2 T-states), memory ops are slowest (2–3 T-states).
- ✅ **Interrupt Cycle** is optional and conditional; it saves PC and loads the ISR vector address.
- ✅ **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** is the mean weighted by instruction mix — used in all performance calculations.
- ✅ **Execution time** $= \dfrac{N \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}}{f_{\text{clock}}}$ — the master equation of computer performance.
- ✅ **MIPS** $= \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times 10^6}$ — always quote as a pure number.
- ✅ **Single-bus CPUs** require multiple T-states per transfer because only one register-to-register move can occur per cycle.
- ✅ **Cache memory** reduces CPI by shortening memory access in the fetch and operand-fetch steps.
- ✅ **Pipelining** (Module 2) overlaps the instruction cycles of successive instructions — but you must master the *unpipelined* cycle first.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 weightage:** This topic carries **10–15%** of Module 1 marks and is foundational for Module 2 (Pipelining) and Module 3 (Memory Hierarchy).

> **Final Study Tip:** Draw the block diagram, write the RTL for at least three instruction types, and solve two numerical CPI/MIPS problems. This is the **guaranteed 14-mark preparation** for any KTU 2024 Module 1 question on the Instruction Cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Execution of a Complete Instruction - Single Bus and Multiple Bus Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Execution of a Complete Instruction - Single Bus and Multiple Bus Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Execution of a Complete Instruction**: The complete set of micro-operations performed by the processor to carry out a single machine-level instruction. It is universally partitioned into the **Fetch Phase** (common to all instructions) followed by the **Execute Phase** (specific to the opcode).

A **Single Bus Organization** is a CPU datapath architecture in which **all registers share one common internal data bus**. Because the ALU requires two simultaneous operands on its inputs but the common bus can transmit only one operand per clock cycle, two temporary registers (**Y** and **Z**) must be inserted at the ALU inputs and output respectively.

A **Multiple (Three) Bus Organization** is a CPU datapath architecture in which **three independent internal buses** (Bus A, Bus B, Bus C) interconnect the registers, ALU, and memory interface. Because two source operands can be routed simultaneously on Bus A and Bus B, while the result is delivered on Bus C, **most register-transfer operations collapse into a single clock cycle**.

## 1.2 The Standard Reference Standard

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor**: Under Module 1, students are expected to (i) trace the exact *sequence of control signals* for representative instructions on the single-bus datapath, and (ii) compare its performance with the three-bus datapath in terms of clock cycles per instruction.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **single-bus CPU as a kitchen with one chopping board**:
- Only one ingredient can be moved onto the chopping board at a time.
- To add two numbers, you first place `A` on the board, then `B`, then the knife (ALU) chops them together, and finally you scoop the result into the bowl.
- That is **three sequential steps** for one addition.

Now imagine a **three-bus CPU as a modern professional kitchen with three stations**:
- Station A holds ingredient `A`, Station B holds ingredient `B`, and Station C catches the chopped output.
- The knife (ALU) works on `A` and `B` simultaneously and pushes the result to Station C in **a single sweep**.

> [!TIP]
> **Take-away**: Single-bus is *cheap and slow*. Three-bus is *expensive and fast*. The trade-off is hardware cost (multiplexers, bus drivers) versus **clock cycles per instruction (CPI)**.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| PC | Program Counter |
| MAR | Memory Address Register |
| MDR | Memory Data Register (also called DR) |
| IR | Instruction Register |
| AC | Accumulator |
| Y, Z | ALU temporary latch registers |
| R0 … Rn-1 | General Purpose Registers (GPRs) |
| MFC | Memory Function Completed flag |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of Instruction Execution — The Canonical Pipeline

Every machine instruction — irrespective of its opcode — moves through the following **sequential phases**:

1. **Fetch Instruction (F)**: `IR ← M[PC]; PC ← PC + 1`
2. **Decode Instruction (D)**: Determine opcode and addressing mode.
3. **Evaluate Operand Address (EOA)**: For memory-reference instructions, compute the effective address.
4. **Fetch Operands (FO)**: Read register/memory operands into CPU registers.
5. **Execute Operation (EX)**: ALU computes result.
6. **Store Result (SR)**: Write result back to register/memory.
7. **Update PC (UPC)**: Adjust PC for the next sequential or branch target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Steps 1 and 2 are **identical** for every instruction. Steps 3–7 vary based on the opcode and addressing mode.

## 2.2 Single-Bus Internal Organization — The Micro-Architecture

### 2.2.1 Why Two Temporary Registers Are Mandatory

The ALU is a **combinational circuit** that computes:

$$
F = A \;\text{op}\; B
$$

In a single-bus system, the bus can physically carry **only one word** in a given cycle. Yet the ALU needs **two operands simultaneously**. This contradiction is resolved by:

- Latching the **first operand into Y** during cycle 1.
- Driving the **second operand directly onto the bus** during cycle 2 and latching the **ALU result into Z** in the same cycle.
- Writing **Z back to the destination register** during cycle 3.

### 2.2.2 Control Step Vocabulary

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `Riout` | Register Ri drives the common bus |
| `Riin` | Register Ri latches from the common bus |
| `Yin` | Y latch accepts bus data |
| `Zin` | Z latch accepts ALU output |
| `ADD / AND / SUB` | ALU function-select lines |
| `Read / Write` | Memory command lines |
| `MARin`, `MDRinE` | Address and Data register latches |

## 2.3 Three-Bus Internal Organization — The Micro-Architecture

Each general-purpose register has **two output ports** and **one input port**:
- Output port 1 → Bus A
- Output port 2 → Bus B
- Input port ← Bus C

The ALU reads Bus A and Bus B and drives Bus C **in a single clock cycle**. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{Register-to-Register operations complete in 1 clock cycle.}}
$$

The only bottleneck is **memory latency** (MFC wait states), which neither organization can eliminate.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Interpretation |
|---|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $T_{\text{instr}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{cycle}_i}$ | Total instruction time = sum of cycle times |
| 2 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | Cycle time = reciprocal of clock frequency |
| 3 | $\text{Speedup}_{3\text{-bus}/1\text{-bus}} = \dfrac{n_{1\text{-bus}}}{n_{3\text{-bus}}}$ | Performance ratio for same instruction |
| 4 | $n_{\text{ADD, single}} = 3$, $n_{\text{ADD, three}} = 1$ | Cycles for `ADD R1,R2` |
| 5 | $n_{\text{LOAD, single}} \ge 5$, $n_{\text{LOAD, three}} \ge 3$ | Cycles for `LOAD R1, LOC` |
| 6 | $\text{Memory Cycle Time} = T_{\text{MAR}} + T_{\text{MDR}} + T_{\text{MFC}}$ | External memory access time |
| 7 | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\sum n_i}{N}$ | Average clock cycles per instruction |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Single-Bus CPUs** dominate embedded microcontrollers (8051, ARM Cortex-M0) where cost, power, and silicon area outweigh raw speed.
- **Three-Bus / Multi-Bus Datapaths** power modern superscalar processors (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series) where the instruction-issue logic dispatches multiple operations per cycle over dedicated operand and result buses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single-Bus Execution of `ADD R1, R2` (i.e., `R1 ← R1 + R2`)

**Goal**: Add the contents of R2 to R1 and store the sum back in R1.

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Cycle 1 — Latch the first operand into Y**
- Control signals asserted: `R1out, Yin`
- Bus transfers `R1` → `Y`
- Equation: $Y \leftarrow R1$

**Cycle 2 — Drive second operand and latch result into Z**
- Control signals asserted: `R2out, ALUop = ADD, Zin`
- ALU computes `Y + R2` and writes to `Z`
- Equation: $Z \leftarrow Y + R2$

**Cycle 3 — Write Z back to destination**
- Control signals asserted: `Zout, R1in`
- Equation: $R1 \leftarrow Z$

### Closed-Form Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After Cycle 1:} \quad & Y = R1 \\
\text{After Cycle 2:} \quad & Z = Y + R2 = R1 + R2 \\
\text{After Cycle 3:} \quad & R1 = Z = R1 + R2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Total cycles for ADD on single-bus = 3.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Three-Bus Execution of `ADD R1, R2`

**Cycle 1 — All transfers in parallel**
- Control signals: `R1outA, R2outB, ALUop = ADD, R1inC`
- Equation: $R1 \leftarrow R1 + R2$

> **Total cycles for ADD on three-bus = 1.**

**Speedup Factor Computation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{ADD} &= \frac{n_{1\text{-bus}}}{n_{3\text{-bus}}} = \frac{3}{1} = 3 \\
\text{Speedup} &= 3\times
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Single-Bus Execution of `LOAD R1, LOC` (Direct Addressing)

**Instruction semantic**: $R1 \leftarrow M[LOC]$ where `LOC` is embedded in the instruction's address field.

### Micro-Operation Sequence

| Cycle | Control Signals | Micro-Operation |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | `PCout, MARin, Read` | $MAR \leftarrow PC$ ; initiate memory read |
| 2 | `MDRout, IRin` | $IR \leftarrow M[PC]$ |
| 3 | `PCin` (with `PC+1` logic) | $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ |
| 4 | `Addressout, MARin` | $MAR \leftarrow IR_{\text{addr}}$ |
| 5 | `Read, MDRinE` | $MDR \leftarrow M[MAR]$ ; wait for MFC |
| 6 | `MDRout, R1in` | $R1 \leftarrow MDR$ |

### Algebraic Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle 1:} \quad & MAR = PC \\
\text{Cycle 2:} \quad & IR = M[MAR] = M[PC] \\
\text{Cycle 3:} \quad & PC = PC + 1 \\
\text{Cycle 4:} \quad & MAR = IR_{\text{addr}} = LOC \\
\text{Cycle 5:} \quad & MDR = M[MAR] = M[LOC] \\
\text{Cycle 6:} \quad & R1 = MDR = M[LOC] \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Total cycles for LOAD on single-bus = 6** (including MFC wait, often reduced to 5 with overlapped fetch).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Three-Bus Execution of `LOAD R1, LOC`

| Cycle | Control Signals | Micro-Operation |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | `PCoutA, MARinC, Read` | $MAR \leftarrow PC$ ; memory read initiated |
| 2 | `MDRoutA, IRinC` | $IR \leftarrow M[PC]$ |
| 3 | `PCoutA, PCinC` (incrementer) | $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ |
| 4 | `IRoutA, MARinC, Read` | $MAR \leftarrow LOC$ ; memory read initiated |
| 5 | `MDRoutA, R1inC` (after MFC) | $R1 \leftarrow M[LOC]$ |

> **Total cycles for LOAD on three-bus = 5** (reduced to 4 with overlapped address computation).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cycle Count Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table** — Frequently asked in Part B (14 marks).

| Instruction | Single-Bus Cycles | Three-Bus Cycles | Speedup |
|-------------|:-----------------:|:----------------:|:-------:|
| ADD R1, R2 (register-register) | 3 | 1 | 3× |
| LOAD R1, LOC (memory load) | 6 | 5 | 1.2× |
| STORE R1, LOC (memory store) | 5 | 4 | 1.25× |
| MOVE R1, R2 (register copy) | 2 | 1 | 2× |
| Branch (PC ← target) | 3 | 2 | 1.5× |

> **Inference**: The three-bus organization shows **dramatic improvement for ALU-bound operations** but **marginal improvement for memory-bound operations**, because the memory cycle time dominates.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem**: A single-bus CPU runs at 100 MHz. Instruction `ADD R1, R2` requires 3 cycles. Compute the execution time of 10 million ADD instructions.

**Solution**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cycle}} &= \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10\,\text{ns} \\
T_{\text{1 instr}} &= 3 \times 10\,\text{ns} = 30\,\text{ns} \\
T_{\text{10 M instr}} &= 10 \times 10^{6} \times 30\,\text{ns} \\
&= 300 \times 10^{6}\,\text{ns} = 0.3\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the three-bus CPU (1 cycle per ADD):

$$
T_{\text{10 M, 3-bus}} = 10 \times 10^{6} \times 10\,\text{ns} = 0.1\,\text{s}
$$

**Speedup confirmed**:

$$
S = \frac{0.3\,\text{s}}{0.1\,\text{s}} = 3\times \quad \blacksquare
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Phase Flow of Instruction Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START] --> B[Fetch Instruction IR = M of PC]
    B --> C[Decode Opcode]
    C --> D{Addressing Mode}
    D -->|Register| E[Fetch Operands from Registers]
    D -->|Memory| F[Evaluate Effective Address]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Execute Operation in ALU]
    G --> H[Store Result to Register or Memory]
    H --> I[Update PC = PC plus 1 or Branch Target]
    I --> J[END]
```

## 4.2 Single-Bus Internal Organization — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RegBank[General Purpose Registers]
        R0
        R1
        R2
        R3
        PC[PC]
    end
    BusA[Common Internal Bus]
    YLatch[Y Temporary Latch]
    ZLatch[Z Temporary Latch]
    ALU[ALU Block]
    IR[Instruction Register]
    MAR[MAR]
    MDR[MDR]
    ExtBus[External System Bus]

    R0 --> BusA
    R1 --> BusA
    R2 --> BusA
    R3 --> BusA
    PC --> BusA
    IR --> BusA
    MDR --> BusA

    BusA --> YLatch
    BusA --> ALU
    YLatch --> ALU
    ALU --> ZLatch
    ZLatch --> BusA
    BusA --> MAR
    MAR --> ExtBus
    ExtBus --> MDR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Every register can drive the common bus. The bus feeds `Y` and one ALU input. The ALU output feeds `Z`, which drives the bus back to the registers. This forces the **3-cycle minimum** for any binary ALU operation.

## 4.3 Three-Bus Internal Organization — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RegBank2[General Purpose Registers with Dual Ports]
        R0a
        R1a
        R2a
        R3a
        PC2[PC]
    end
    BusA2[Bus A Source 1]
    BusB2[Bus B Source 2]
    BusC2[Bus C Destination]
    ALU2[ALU Block]
    IR2[IR]
    MAR2[MAR]
    MDR2[MDR]

    R0a --> BusA2
    R1a --> BusA2
    R2a --> BusA2
    R3a --> BusA2
    PC2 --> BusA2
    IR2 --> BusA2
    MDR2 --> BusA2

    R0a --> BusB2
    R1a --> BusB2
    R2a --> BusB2
    R3a --> BusB2
    PC2 --> BusB2
    IR2 --> BusB2
    MDR2 --> BusB2

    BusA2 --> ALU2
    BusB2 --> ALU2
    ALU2 --> BusC2
    BusC2 --> R0a
    BusC2 --> R1a
    BusC2 --> R2a
    BusC2 --> R3a
    BusC2 --> PC2
    BusC2 --> MAR2
    BusC2 --> MDR2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key visual difference vs Single-Bus**: Two independent source buses (A and B) feed the ALU simultaneously, while the result rides on Bus C. **No Y or Z latches are required**, eliminating two whole clock cycles for register-register operations.

## 4.4 Timing Diagram Sketch — Single-Bus ADD R1, R2

```mermaid
gantt
    title Single-Bus ADD R1 R2 Timing Across 3 Cycles
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Control Signals
    R1out asserted       :a1, 0, 1
    Yin asserted         :a2, 0, 1
    R2out asserted       :a3, 1, 2
    ADD function select  :a4, 1, 2
    Zin asserted         :a5, 1, 2
    Zout asserted        :a6, 2, 3
    R1in asserted        :a7, 2, 3
    section Data
    Bus carries R1       :b1, 0, 1
    Bus carries R2       :b2, 1, 2
    Bus carries Z result :b3, 2, 3
```

## 4.5 Timing Diagram Sketch — Three-Bus ADD R1, R2

```mermaid
gantt
    title Three-Bus ADD R1 R2 Timing in 1 Cycle
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Control Signals
    R1outA and R2outB    :c1, 0, 1
    ALU ADD select       :c2, 0, 1
    R1inC latch          :c3, 0, 1
    section Buses
    Bus A holds R1       :d1, 0, 1
    Bus B holds R2       :d2, 0, 1
    Bus C holds result   :d3, 0, 1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define the Instruction Cycle **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer**:
The **Instruction Cycle** is the complete sequence of micro-operations required to fetch, decode, and execute a single machine instruction. It comprises the **Fetch Phase** (PC → MAR → Memory → IR), the **Decode Phase** (opcode interpretation), and the **Execute Phase** (address calculation, operand fetch, ALU operation, and result storage). For memory-reference instructions, additional memory-access cycles are inserted to fetch/store operands.

---

### Question 2 — Why are temporary registers Y and Z required in a single-bus CPU? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer**:
