The ALU is a combinational circuit that requires **two operands simultaneously** to produce an output. However, a single common bus can transfer **only one word per clock cycle**. Temporary registers **Y** and **Z** solve this timing conflict:
- **Y** latches the first operand (read in cycle 1) and holds it stable for the ALU.
- **Z** latches the ALU output (computed in cycle 2) so that it can be driven back onto the bus for writing into the destination register in cycle 3.

Without Y and Z, the ALU inputs would change unpredictably and the result would vanish before being captured.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1**: Students frequently forget to **advance the PC (`PC ← PC + 1`)** during the fetch phase when tracing LOAD/STORE instructions. Always include this micro-operation explicitly.
>
> **Common Mistake #2**: Confusing `MDRinE` (enable external data into MDR) with `MDRin` (latch from internal bus). Use `MDRinE` only when memory is driving the bus.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]**

#### (a) Draw the block diagram of a single-bus CPU and explain the execution of the ADD instruction with the complete sequence of control signals. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Block Diagram**: Refer to Section 4.2 above for the single-bus block diagram.

**Execution of `ADD R1, R2` (i.e., `R1 ← R1 + R2`)**:

| Cycle | Control Signals Asserted | Micro-Operation |
|-------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | `R1out, Yin` | $Y \leftarrow R1$ |
| 2 | `R2out, ALUop = ADD, Zin` | $Z \leftarrow Y + R2$ |
| 3 | `Zout, R1in` | $R1 \leftarrow Z$ |

**Justification of each cycle**:
- **Cycle 1**: The first operand `R1` is placed on the common bus and latched into the **Y register** to freeze it for the ALU.
- **Cycle 2**: The second operand `R2` is driven onto the bus; the ALU adds `Y + R2` and the sum is latched into `Z`. Note that `Y` and `Z` are mandatory because the bus carries only one operand at a time.
- **Cycle 3**: The computed sum in `Z` is routed back to the bus and written into the destination register `R1`.

**Total cycles = 3**, including one ALU operation.

---

#### (b) Explain the three-bus internal organization of the CPU with a neat block diagram. Show how the ADD instruction executes in a single cycle. Compare it with the single-bus organization. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Block Diagram**: Refer to Section 4.3 above for the three-bus block diagram.

**Three-Bus Datapath Characteristics**:
- **Bus A** carries source operand 1.
- **Bus B** carries source operand 2.
- **Bus C** carries the destination operand.
- Each register has **two read ports** (one for Bus A, one for Bus B) and **one write port** (from Bus C).
- **No Y or Z latches are required**.

**Execution of `ADD R1, R2` in a single cycle**:

| Cycle | Control Signals | Micro-Operation |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | `R1outA, R2outB, ALUop = ADD, R1inC` | $R1 \leftarrow R1 + R2$ |

**Comparison Table**:

| Parameter | Single-Bus | Three-Bus |
|-----------|:----------:|:---------:|
| Internal buses | 1 | 3 |
| Temporary latches (Y, Z) | Required | Not required |
| ADD cycle count | 3 | 1 |
| LOAD cycle count | 5–6 | 4–5 |
| Hardware cost | Low | High |
| Operating frequency | Lower | Higher |
| Typical use case | Embedded MCUs | High-performance CPUs |

**Conclusion**: The three-bus organization offers a **3× speedup for register-register operations** at the cost of increased multiplexer logic and bus driver hardware.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**

#### (a) List and explain the various phases involved in the execution of a complete instruction. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

| # | Phase | Function | Typical Signals |
|---|-------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Fetch Instruction** | $IR \leftarrow M[PC]$ and $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | `PCout, MARin, Read, MDRinE, MDRout, IRin` |
| 2 | **Decode Instruction** | Opcode interpretation, address-mode determination | Decoder logic; `D0, D1, …, D7` bits |
| 3 | **Evaluate Effective Address** | Compute operand address for memory-reference ops | `IRout(addr), MARin` |
| 4 | **Fetch Operands** | Read source operands from registers/memory | `Riout, Yin` / `Read, MDRinE` |
| 5 | **Execute** | ALU performs arithmetic/logic operation | `ALUop = ADD/SUB/AND`, `Zin` |
| 6 | **Store Result** | Write ALU output to register/memory | `Zout, Rjin` / `MDRout, Write` |
| 7 | **Update PC** | Branch or sequential increment | `PCin` with branch logic |

**Important Observations**:
- Phases 1 and 2 are **universal** for every instruction.
- Phases 3–7 are **opcode-dependent**.
- The **Fetch-Decode-Execute** triplet is the canonical ISA-level pipeline (in classical RISC, this maps directly to IF, ID, EX pipeline stages).

---

#### (b) For a single-bus CPU, write the complete micro-operation sequence for the instructions (i) `ADD R1, R2` and (ii) `LOAD R1, LOC`. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(i) `ADD R1, R2` — R1 ← R1 + R2**:

| Cycle | Control Signals | Micro-Operation |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | `R1out, Yin` | $Y \leftarrow R1$ |
| 2 | `R2out, ADD, Zin` | $Z \leftarrow Y + R2$ |
| 3 | `Zout, R1in` | $R1 \leftarrow Z$ |

**(ii) `LOAD R1, LOC` — R1 ← M[LOC] (direct addressing)**:

| Cycle | Control Signals | Micro-Operation |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | `PCout, MARin, Read` | $MAR \leftarrow PC$ |
| 2 | `MDRoutE, IRin` | $IR \leftarrow M[PC]$ |
| 3 | `Addressout, MARin` (address field of IR) | $MAR \leftarrow LOC$ |
| 4 | `Read, MDRinE` | $MDR \leftarrow M[LOC]$ ; wait for MFC |
| 5 | `MDRout, R1in` | $R1 \leftarrow MDR$ |

**Note**: The PC increment is assumed overlapped with the instruction fetch in many textbooks; explicitly add `PCout, PCin` (with `+1` adder) in cycle 2 if the question demands it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- **Instruction Cycle = Fetch + Decode + Execute**. Fetch and Decode are common; Execute is opcode-specific.
- **Single-Bus**: 1 internal bus → mandatory Y and Z latches → 3 cycles for ADD.
- **Three-Bus**: 3 internal buses (A, B, C) → no Y/Z → 1 cycle for ADD → 3× faster.
- **Y register** holds the **first operand** of an ALU operation.
- **Z register** holds the **ALU result** for bus writeback.
- **`MDRinE`** is used when **memory** drives MDR; **`MDRin`** is used when the **internal bus** drives MDR.
- **MFC (Memory Function Complete)** signal must be asserted before reading MDR.
- **LOAD** involves **two memory accesses**: one for the instruction fetch and one for the operand.
- **STORE** requires `MDR ← AC/Register` first, then `Write` to memory.
- **Branch instructions** only modify the PC; they bypass the ALU.
- **Speedup of 3-bus over 1-bus** is largest for **register-to-register operations** and smallest for **memory-bound operations**.
- **Hardware cost trade-off**: more buses ⇒ more multiplexers, more bus drivers, more silicon area.
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** for the basic single-bus CPU ≈ **3–6** depending on opcode.
- **CPI for the basic three-bus CPU** ≈ **1–5** depending on opcode.
- **Embedded systems** prefer single-bus (low power); **desktop/server CPUs** prefer multi-bus (high throughput).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Pitfall**: In a single-bus system, you **cannot** perform `R1 ← R1 + R1` in just 2 cycles. You still need **3 cycles** because `R1` must be latched into Y first, then the bus drives `R1` again to the ALU, and finally Z writes back. This is a **favourite KTU trick question** — do not fall for it!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter Register Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inter Register Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Inter Register Transfer** is the fundamental micro-operation in a digital computer system where binary information (data, address, or control words) is moved from one processor register to another during the execution of an instruction cycle. These transfers are specified using a symbolic notation known as the **Register Transfer Language (RTL)**, which provides a concise, hardware-oriented method to describe the internal data movement between CPU registers, memory, and the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Inter Register Transfer forms the *foundational vocabulary* for Module 2 (Basic Processing Unit). Every subsequent concept — micro-operations, bus systems, control logic, and microprogrammed control — is built directly upon the RTL notation introduced here. **Mastery of this topic is non-negotiable** for securing full marks in ESE questions.

### 1.2 Register Transfer Language (RTL) — The Symbolic Grammar

The Register Transfer Language uses a strict set of notational conventions, standardized across all computer architecture references. The following table enumerates the canonical symbols:

| Symbol | Description | Hardware Equivalent | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\leftarrow$ | Transfer of data (replacement) | Multiplexer + Load signal | $R2 \leftarrow R1$ |
| $,$ | Simultaneous (parallel) operations | Parallel load enable lines | $R1 \leftarrow R2, R3 \leftarrow R4$ |
| $:$ | Conditional transfer (IF condition) | Control logic gating | $P : R2 \leftarrow R1$ |
| $M$ | Memory location addressed by | Address bus + Memory $RD$ | $R1 \leftarrow M[AR]$ |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Postal Sorting Office

Imagine a **postal sorting office** where letters (data) sit in numbered pigeon-holes (registers $R0, R1, R2, \ldots, R_{n-1}$). Every time the office processes a letter:

1. A sorter (the **Control Unit**) reads a delivery slip (the **micro-instruction**).
2. Based on the slip's instruction, the sorter physically moves a letter from pigeon-hole $R1$ into pigeon-hole $R2$.
3. The act of moving is **destructive at the source** for the original *arrangement* but the *letter is preserved* (registers usually retain a copy; a true swap requires a third register).

This physical movement of a letter from one box to another, governed by explicit instructions, is exactly what an **Inter Register Transfer** does inside your CPU — it is the *atomic act of computation*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A register transfer is a **micro-operation** — it occurs at the *hardware signal level* (single clock cycle) and is invisible to high-level programmers. A `MOV` instruction in C is a *macro-operation* that the assembler decomposes into one or more such micro-operations.

### 1.4 Physical and Architectural Metrics

The following standard metrics characterize inter-register transfers in any practical processor:

- **Register Word-Length (n):** Typically $\mathbf{8, 16, 32,}$ or $\mathbf{64}$ bits in modern CPUs.
- **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$):** The time for valid data to appear at the destination register's input — typically in the order of **nanoseconds** ($1 \text{ ns} = 10^{-9}$ s).
- **Clock Period ($T_{clk}$):** Must satisfy $T_{clk} \geq t_{pd}$ to guarantee a *settled* transfer before the next rising edge.
- **Bus Width:** Must be $\geq n$ bits to allow a single-cycle transfer; otherwise, multiple cycles are required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Register Transfer Statement

A complete RTL statement for an inter-register transfer has three logical components, expressed in the canonical form:

$$
\text{Condition : Destination} \leftarrow \text{Source, Auxiliary operations}
$$

Each component maps onto specific physical hardware:

- **Condition (`P:`):** Implemented as a combinational logic gate (AND, OR, NOT) that *gates* the load enable of the destination register.
- **Destination Register:** Receives data on the *next active clock edge* if its load input is asserted.
- **Source Register / Expression:** Drives the data bus with a stable value throughout the transfer window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the colon notation?** The condition $P$ is a *Boolean control variable*. If $P = 1$, the transfer executes. If $P = 0$, the destination register retains its previous content (its *enable* input is held low). This single mechanism allows one control word to *conditionally trigger* many different micro-operations across separate clock cycles.

### 2.2 Classification of Inter Register Transfers

#### A. Unconditional Transfer
The simplest form, executed every clock cycle when the corresponding control signal is active:

$$
R2 \leftarrow R1
$$

**Hardware:** A direct wire connection (or bus-mediated path) from the $Q$-output of $R1$ to the $D$-input of $R2$, gated by a single load enable $LD_2$.

#### B. Conditional Transfer
Execution depends on the state of one or more control flags:

$$
\overline{T_2} \cdot I : R2 \leftarrow R1
$$

**Interpretation:** If the timing signal $T_2$ is OFF and the instruction register contains a specific opcode pattern $I$, then perform the transfer. This is the bedrock of the *fetch-decode-execute* cycle's micro-operation sequencing.

#### C. Simultaneous (Parallel) Transfer
Two or more independent transfers occur in the **same clock edge**, separated by commas:

$$
R1 \leftarrow R2, \quad R3 \leftarrow R4
$$

**Critical Rule:** The RHS of every transfer must be evaluated using the *values present at the start* of the cycle. The LHS registers update *simultaneously* — there is no race condition as long as destination registers are clocked (edge-triggered) and source registers are level-sensitive during the same edge.

#### D. Transfer with Arithmetic/Logic Function
A functional unit (ALU) is inserted in the data path:

$$
R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2
$$

Here, the ALU computes the sum combinationally, and $R3$ latches the result on the clock edge. The function $+$ is selected by ALU control inputs $S_1, S_0$ (e.g., $00 =$ ADD, $01 =$ SUB, $10 =$ AND, $11 =$ OR).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Boundary Conditions

| Parameter / Equation | Meaning | Units / Boundary Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R_{dest} \leftarrow R_{src}$ | Unconditional single-cycle transfer | Clock period $T_{clk} \geq t_{pd,src} + t_{setup,dest}$ |
| $P : R_{dest} \leftarrow R_{src}$ | Conditional transfer; executes iff $P=1$ | $P \in \{0,1\}$ (Boolean) |
| $R1 \leftarrow R2, R3 \leftarrow R4$ | Simultaneous; same clock edge | $t_{hold} \geq 0$ to avoid metastability |
| $R \leftarrow 0$ | Clear operation | $LD = 0$ for one cycle, or asynchronous $\overline{CLR}$ |
| $R \leftarrow \overline{R}$ | Toggle (complement) | $T_{clk} \geq 2 \cdot t_{pd,\text{XOR}}$ for stable output |
| Bus capacity: $C = n \cdot f_{clk}$ | Bits transferred per second | $n$ = bus width, $f_{clk}$ in Hz |
| $t_{cycle} = t_{pd,\text{setup}} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{pd,\text{FF}}$ | Minimum clock period for safe transfer | Sum of all combinational delays + flip-flop setup |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real VLSI design, inter-register transfer timing directly determines the **maximum operating frequency** of a processor. Modern CPUs use *pipelining* and *register forwarding* to minimize the apparent cost of these transfers, but the fundamental RTL notation remains unchanged.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Notational Element

| Notation Element | Reason for Existence | Real-World Engineering Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\leftarrow$ (arrow) | Distinguishes *assignment* from *equality* | "Set the thermostat TO 22°C" (not "equals 22°C") |
| Comma `,` | Hardware parallelism is cheap; serial is slow | Multiple trucks leaving a warehouse simultaneously |
| Colon `:` | Control logic is fundamentally *gated* | A traffic signal permitting/denying flow |
| Brackets $M[AR]$ | Distinguishes *memory content* from *memory address* | "Read the letter FROM box number AR" |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conditional Transfer Truth Table

**Problem:** Derive the Boolean control expression for the load-enable signal of a destination register $R2$, given that $R2 \leftarrow R1$ must execute only when $P = 1$ AND the timing signal $T_2 = 1$ (i.e., during phase 2 of the clock).

**Step 1: Identify the Boolean condition.**
The transfer is required when the *logical AND* of $P$ and $T_2$ is asserted. For all other combinations, $R2$ must hold its current value.

**Step 2: Construct the load-enable equation.**

$$
LD_2 = P \cdot T_2
$$

**Step 3: Derive the next-state equation for $R2$.**

$$
R2(t+1) = LD_2 \cdot R1(t) + \overline{LD_2} \cdot R2(t)
$$

*Textual explanation:* On the next clock edge ($t+1$), the new content of $R2$ will be:
- The current content of $R1$, **if** $LD_2 = 1$ (i.e., both $P$ and $T_2$ are high).
- The current content of $R2$ (no change), **if** $LD_2 = 0$.

**Step 4: Expand the substitution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R2(t+1) &= (P \cdot T_2) \cdot R1(t) + \overline{P \cdot T_2} \cdot R2(t) \\
&= (P \cdot T_2) \cdot R1(t) + (\overline{P} + \overline{T_2}) \cdot R2(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual explanation:* The first term represents the *active transfer*: data flows from $R1$ into $R2$ only when both control inputs are high. The second term, using De Morgan's theorem ($\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$), represents the *hold condition*: $R2$ retains its previous state whenever the transfer is not authorized.

**Step 5: Identify the required hardware.**
A single 2-input AND gate (for $LD_2$), a 2-to-1 multiplexer (to select between $R1$ and the feedback path of $R2$), and an edge-triggered D flip-flop. This minimal hardware is the **canonical implementation** of any conditional register transfer.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sample Register Transfer Operations

The following table analyzes a typical instruction-level decomposition. Assume a 4-register CPU: $R0, R1, R2, R3$, with an auxiliary register $AR$ (Address Register) and $DR$ (Data Register).

| Instruction (Assembly) | RTL Decomposition | Hardware Activated | Cycle Phase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `MOV R2, R1` | $R2 \leftarrow R1$ | $LD_2 = 1$, $S_{bus} = 01$ | $T_2$ |
| `ADD R3, R1, R2` | $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2$ | $S_1S_0 = 00$ (ADD), $LD_3 = 1$ | $T_3$ |
| `CLEAR R0` | $R0 \leftarrow 0$ | $LD_0 = 1$, Bus driven LOW | $T_1$ |
| `SWAP R1, R2`* | $R1 \leftarrow R2, R2 \leftarrow R1$ | **RACE!** Requires temp register | $T_4$ |
| `LOAD R1, M[AR]` | $R1 \leftarrow M, DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | Memory $READ$, $LD_{DR} = 1, LD_1 = 1$ | $T_4, T_5$ |

\* **Critical Pitfall:** The naive `SWAP` decomposition is **physically unrealizable in a single clock cycle**. The RHS of the second transfer would read the *updated* value of $R1$ (which already received $R2$'s content), resulting in $R1 \leftarrow R1$. The correct decomposition requires an intermediate register:

$$
\text{Temp} \leftarrow R1, \quad R1 \leftarrow R2, \quad R2 \leftarrow \text{Temp}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Common Bus Selection Logic

**Problem:** A CPU has 4 registers ($R0, R1, R2, R3$), each $n$-bits wide. Design the Boolean control logic for the multiplexer that selects which register drives the **common bus**.

**Step 1: Enumerate all valid source selections.**
A 2-bit select line $S_1 S_0$ must select one of the 4 registers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 S_0 = 00 &\Rightarrow \text{Bus} \leftarrow R0 \\
S_1 S_0 = 01 &\Rightarrow \text{Bus} \leftarrow R1 \\
S_1 S_0 = 10 &\Rightarrow \text{Bus} \leftarrow R2 \\
S_1 S_0 = 11 &\Rightarrow \text{Bus} \leftarrow R3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Derive the enable signal for each register's output buffer.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EN}_{R0} &= \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \\
\text{EN}_{R1} &= \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \\
\text{EN}_{R2} &= S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0} \\
\text{EN}_{R3} &= S_1 \cdot S_0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual explanation:* Each register has a tri-state output buffer. The enable signal for a specific register is the **minterm** of the select lines corresponding to that register's binary index. Only one enable is high at any time, ensuring no bus contention.

**Step 3: Generalize to $2^k$ registers.**
For a system with $k$ select bits and $2^k$ registers, the enable for register $R_i$ (where $i$ is in binary as $b_{k-1} \ldots b_1 b_0$) is:

$$
\text{EN}_{R_i} = \prod_{j=0}^{k-1} \text{(select term for bit } b_j\text{)}
$$

This formula is the **direct hardware translation** of any register transfer statement of the form "Bus $\leftarrow R_i$".

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inter-Register Transfer via a Common Bus

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical data-path structure used to implement inter-register transfers in a basic processing unit. It depicts the multiplexed common bus, the register file, the ALU, and the destination load-enable logic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RF["REGISTER FILE\nR0 R1 R2 R3"]:::reg
    SEL["SELECT LOGIC\nS1 S0 Decoder"]:::sel
    BUS{{"COMMON BUS\nn-bit tri-state"}}:::bus
    ALU["ALU\nFUNCTIONAL UNIT"]:::alu
    LD["DESTINATION\nLOAD ENABLE GATE"]:::ld
    DR["DESTINATION\nREGISTER"]:::dr
    CLK(["CLOCK EDGE"]):::clk

    SEL -- "EN_R0" --> RF
    SEL -- "EN_R1" --> RF
    SEL -- "EN_R2" --> RF
    SEL -- "EN_R3" --> RF
    RF -- "single source drives" --> BUS
    BUS -- "operand A" --> ALU
    BUS -- "operand B via second mux" --> ALU
    ALU -- "combinational result" --> LD
    LD -- "gated by LD_dest" --> DR
    CLK -- "rising edge latches" --> DR

    classDef reg fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef sel fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef bus fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:3px,color:#831843
    classDef alu fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef ld fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#6D28D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#4C1D95
    classDef dr fill:#FFE4E6,stroke:#BE123C,stroke-width:2px,color:#881337
    classDef clk fill:#F1F5F9,stroke:#334155,stroke-width:1px,color:#0F172A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **SELECT LOGIC** (a 2-to-4 decoder) asserts exactly one enable line into the register file based on the $S_1 S_0$ control bits.
- The **COMMON BUS** is a tri-state wire; only the enabled register drives it, preventing electrical contention.
- The **ALU** consumes bus data and produces a result combinationally.
- The **LOAD ENABLE GATE** conditions the result with the destination's load signal, and the **CLOCK EDGE** latches the final value into the destination register.

### 4.2 Flowchart: Micro-Operation Sequencing for a Typical Fetch

The following flowchart demonstrates how RTL statements are sequenced across multiple clock phases to execute a single instruction fetch. This is the *practical application* of inter-register transfer notation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Start of Fetch Cycle"]):::start --> B["T1: AR ← PC"]:::phase
    B --> C["T2: DR ← M AR \nMemory Read Activated"]:::phase
    C --> D["T3: IR ← DR \nInstruction Decoded"]:::phase
    D --> E{"Decode Phase:\nOpcode Valid?"}:::decision
    E -- "Yes" --> F["T4: PC ← PC + 1 \nIncrement Counter"]:::phase
    E -- "No" --> G(["Wait / Stall"]):::stall
    F --> H(["End of Fetch \nReady for Execute"]):::end

    classDef start fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef phase fill:#F0FDF4,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:1px,color:#14532D
    classDef decision fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#CA8A04,stroke-width:2px,color:#713F12
    classDef stall fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef end fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#4338CA,stroke-width:2px,color:#312E81
```

**Interpreting the phases:** Each labeled box represents one or more inter-register transfers occurring in a specific clock phase ($T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$). The use of *distinct timing variables* ($T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$) is precisely the *conditional mechanism* introduced in Section 2 — each RTL statement is gated by a unique timing signal, ensuring they execute in strict sequence.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Register Transfer Language (RTL). Explain the conditional transfer statement with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

Register Transfer Language (RTL) is a symbolic notation used to describe the micro-operations and data movements between registers in a digital system. It employs symbols such as $\leftarrow$ for transfer, $,$ for simultaneous operations, and $:$ for conditional execution.

A **conditional transfer** statement takes the form $P : R2 \leftarrow R1$, where $P$ is a Boolean control variable. The transfer executes *only if* $P = 1$. If $P = 0$, register $R2$ retains its current content. For example, $\overline{T_2} \cdot I : AR \leftarrow M[DR]$ means the address register $AR$ is loaded from memory location pointed to by $DR$, only when timing signal $T_2$ is OFF and the instruction register matches pattern $I$. The colon notation allows one control word to selectively activate specific micro-operations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a common bus? How is it implemented using multiplexers? Explain with a block diagram description.*

**Model Answer:**

A **common bus** is a shared data path that allows multiple registers to transfer data to a single destination (such as the ALU or a destination register) without requiring dedicated point-to-point wiring between every source-destination pair. It is implemented using a **multiplexer** whose data inputs are the outputs of all source registers, and whose select lines determine which register's data is routed to the bus.

For $2^k$ registers, a $2^k$-to-$1$ multiplexer of width $n$ bits is used, with $k$ select lines $S_{k-1} \ldots S_0$. The select input $S_{k-1} \ldots S_0$ in binary denotes the index of the source register. For example, with 4 registers, $S_1 S_0 = 10$ selects $R2$ to drive the bus. This drastically reduces wiring complexity from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \log N)$, at the cost of a single multiplexer propagation delay.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the three basic symbols used in Register Transfer Language with hardware equivalents. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A CPU has four registers $R0, R1, R2, R3$. Using RTL notation, write the micro-operations to perform the following: *(i)* Exchange contents of $R1$ and $R2$ using a temporary register. *(ii)* Add the contents of $R1$ and $R2$ and store in $R3$. *(iii)* Increment $R0$ by 1. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The three fundamental RTL symbols are:

1. **Transfer Symbol $\leftarrow$:** Indicates data movement from RHS to LHS. Hardware: multiplexer + clocked flip-flop load input.
   Example: $R2 \leftarrow R1$ — on the next clock edge, $R2$'s flip-flops latch the current value of $R1$'s outputs.

2. **Comma `,` (Simultaneity):** Multiple transfers occur in the *same clock cycle*. Hardware: parallel load enable lines for multiple registers.
   Example: $R1 \leftarrow R2, R3 \leftarrow R4$ — both destination registers update on the *same* rising clock edge.

3. **Colon `:` (Conditional):** Transfer executes only when the Boolean control variable is true. Hardware: AND gate producing the load-enable signal.
   Example: $P : R2 \leftarrow R1$ — $LD_2 = P$, so $R2$ loads $R1$ only when $P = 1$.

**(b)** The micro-operations are:

**(i) Exchange $R1$ and $R2$ using a temporary register $R_{temp}$:**

$$
R_{temp} \leftarrow R1
$$

$$
R1 \leftarrow R2
$$

$$
R2 \leftarrow R_{temp}
$$

**Explanation:** A single-cycle swap is impossible (race condition); three sequential transfers are required.

**(ii) Add $R1$ and $R2$, store in $R3$:**

$$
R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2
$$

**Explanation:** The ALU is configured in ADD mode; its result is routed to $R3$'s data input and latched on the clock edge.

**(iii) Increment $R0$ by 1:**

$$
R0 \leftarrow R0 + 1
$$

**Explanation:** The constant `1` is hard-wired to one ALU input; $R0$ is fed back to the other; the sum replaces $R0$. Alternatively, an incrementer unit may be used in a dedicated increment path.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** What is meant by a bus system? Explain the common bus system with four registers using a 4×1 multiplexer. Derive the Boolean equations for the select logic. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the micro-operations for fetching a word from memory. Write the complete sequence of RTL statements with timing signals $T_1, T_2, T_3$. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A **bus system** is a shared communication pathway that allows multiple registers to send/receive data over a common set of wires, eliminating the need for individual connections between every source-destination pair.

**Common Bus with 4 Registers (4×1 MUX):**

The 4×1 multiplexer has data inputs from $R0, R1, R2, R3$ and select lines $S_1, S_0$. The bus output is $\text{Bus} = \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \cdot R0 + \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \cdot R1 + S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0} \cdot R2 + S_1 \cdot S_0 \cdot R3$.

**Boolean Equations for Select Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bus} \leftarrow R0 &: \quad S_1 S_0 = 00 \\
\text{Bus} \leftarrow R1 &: \quad S_1 S_0 = 01 \\
\text{Bus} \leftarrow R2 &: \quad S_1 S_0 = 10 \\
\text{Bus} \leftarrow R3 &: \quad S_1 S_0 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative — Three-State Bus Implementation:**

Each register $R_i$ has a tri-state buffer; its enable is the minterm corresponding to its binary index. Exactly one buffer is enabled at a time, so only one register drives the bus. This scales better for larger register files.

**(b) Memory Fetch Micro-Operations:**

The standard fetch sequence uses three timing phases:

**Phase $T_1$ — Address Transfer to Memory:**

$$
T_1 : AR \leftarrow PC
$$

**Explanation:** The Program Counter's content (the address of the next instruction) is transferred to the Address Register, which drives the memory address bus.

**Phase $T_2$ — Memory Read and Data Latch:**

$$
T_2 : DR \leftarrow M[AR], \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1
$$

**Explanation:** A memory read is initiated; the addressed word appears on the data bus and is latched into $DR$. Simultaneously, the PC is incremented to point to the next sequential instruction (a classic *simultaneous* transfer).

**Phase $T_3$ — Instruction Register Load:**

$$
T_3 : IR \leftarrow DR
$$

**Explanation:** The fetched instruction word moves from $DR$ to $IR$, where the control unit decodes it to determine the required execute-phase micro-operations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Missing timing variable prefixes:** Writing $AR \leftarrow PC$ instead of $T_1 : AR \leftarrow PC$ costs you the *condition* mark (1–2 marks lost per occurrence).
> 2. **Forgetting simultaneous PC increment:** The fetch sequence $T_2$ is a *parallel* operation — examiners explicitly test whether you used the comma `,` notation.
> 3. **Swapping source and destination order:** Students often write $PC \leftarrow AR$ (incorrect — content flows *from* address register *to* program counter concept is wrong). Remember: $AR$ is loaded *from* $PC$, not vice versa.
> 4. **No mention of hardware implementation:** A 7-mark question demands at least one sentence on the corresponding *multiplexer / tri-state buffer* hardware.
> 5. **Drawing the bus as a single line:** An $n$-bit bus must be shown as a *bundle* (a thick line or labelled as "$n$-bit") — single-line drawings are penalized as ambiguous.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inter Register Transfer**

- **RTL = Hardware Description Notation** — not a programming language; it describes *what the data path does each clock cycle*.
- **Three Core Symbols:** $\leftarrow$ (transfer), $,$ (simultaneity), $:$ (conditional). Memorize their hardware equivalents.
- **Simultaneous Transfers Rule:** RHS is evaluated using *old* (pre-clock-edge) values; LHS updates *together* on the same edge. **Never chain them to imply a swap** — use a temporary register.
- **Conditional Transfers Use Boolean Control Variables:** $P, T_i, I$ are single-bit signals from the control unit. The transfer is *gated* by the AND of all listed conditions.
- **Common Bus Realizations:** (1) Multiplexer-based (small register files), (2) Tri-state buffer-based (large register files, lower cost).
- **Memory Transfer Syntax:** $R \leftarrow M[AR]$ (read), $M[AR] \leftarrow R$ (write). Brackets denote *content at address*, not the address itself.
- **Timing Variables $T_i$:** Enumerate the *clock phases* within an instruction cycle. Each micro-operation belongs to exactly one $T_i$.
- **Hardware Translation:** Every RTL statement maps to a concrete *combinational path* (mux, bus, ALU) plus a *clocked load enable* on the destination register.
- **Common Exam Trap:** "Bus $\leftarrow R_2$" requires $S_1 S_0 = 10$ in a 4-register system. Know the binary mapping cold.
- **Architectural Significance:** Inter-register transfer bandwidth ($n \cdot f_{clk}$) is a *first-order performance metric* — modern CPUs add caches, pipelines, and register renaming to hide its latency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arithmetic, Logic and Shift Micro-Operations. Processor Logic Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic, Logic and Shift Micro-Operations. Processor Logic Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Micro-Operations?

A **micro-operation** is the most elementary operation that can be performed on the data stored in a processor's registers during a single clock pulse (clock cycle). Micro-operations are the basic building blocks of all computer instructions at the hardware level — every machine instruction is decomposed by the control unit into a sequence of micro-operations executed per clock cycle.

In formal RTL (Register Transfer Language) notation, a micro-operation is written as:

$$ R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2 $$

This single statement represents "transfer to register R3 the sum of the contents of registers R1 and R2," and is executed by the hardware in **one clock cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Hierarchy (KTU Board Definition):**
> **Instruction** → (broken down into) → **Micro-operations** → (built from) → **Micro-orders (control signals)**
>
> A machine instruction (like ADD, MOV) takes multiple clock cycles; each cycle executes one or more micro-operations.

## 1.2 The Three Major Families of Micro-Operations

| Family | Purpose | Typical Operations |
|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic Micro-Operations** | Numeric computation in the ALU | Add, Subtract, Increment, Decrement, Add with Carry, Subtract with Borrow, Negate (2's complement) |
| **Logic Micro-Operations** | Bitwise logical manipulation | AND, OR, XOR, NOT/NOT-A, NOT-B, Complement, Transfer (sneak path) |
| **Shift Micro-Operations** | Bit displacement within a register | Logical Shift Left/Right, Circular Shift Left/Right (Rotate), Arithmetic Shift Right |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Factory Assembly Line

Think of a CPU as a high-speed **automated factory floor**:
- **Registers** = workbenches holding the raw materials and parts.
- **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** = the machines bolted to the floor that transform materials (the actual addition, ANDing, shifting).
- **Clock** = the factory whistle — when it blows, every workbench grabs a new item and every machine performs exactly one cut/weld/paint job.
- **Micro-operation** = ONE specific weld, paint, or cut done in ONE whistle-blast.
- **Control signals** = the foreman's shouted commands ("Machine-7, PAINT red on Bench-3 NOW!").

Just as a finished car requires hundreds of timed, parallel/sequential steps, executing `a = b + c; if(a > 0) shift right a;` requires multiple whistle cycles, each carrying out one micro-operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):**
> Students must master (i) classification of micro-operations, (ii) their hardware realizations, and (iii) the design of the **Hardwired Control Unit** that sequences them — which is the central theme of "Processor Logic Design."

## 1.4 Processor Logic Design — The Top-Level View

Processor Logic Design refers to the process of translating each instruction's micro-operation sequence into **Boolean control logic** (combinational and sequential) that generates the proper control signals on each clock cycle. The two main design styles are:

1. **Hardwired Control** — fixed combinational/sequential logic gates and flip-flops. Fast, but rigid.
2. **Microprogrammed Control** — microinstructions stored in a control memory. Flexible, slightly slower.

This module focuses exclusively on **Hardwired Control Logic Design**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arithmetic Micro-Operations — The Complete Set

Let $R_i$ and $R_j$ be two source registers and $R_k$ the destination register. The standard set of arithmetic micro-operations defined by the KTU/standard textbook syllabus is:

| Operation | RTL Statement | Hardware Realization |
|---|---|---|
| Add | $R_k \leftarrow R_i + R_j$ | Binary adder with $S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$ |
| Add with Carry | $R_k \leftarrow R_i + R_j + C$ | Binary adder with carry-in $C$ |
| Subtract (using 1's & 2's comp.) | $R_k \leftarrow R_i - R_j$ | Adder with $\overline{R_j}$ input and $C_{in} = 1$ |
| Subtract with Borrow | $R_k \leftarrow R_i - R_j - \overline{C}$ | Specialized subtractor |
| Increment | $R_k \leftarrow R_i + 1$ | Incrementer (half-adder chain) |
| Decrement | $R_k \leftarrow R_i - 1$ | Decrementer |
| 1's Complement | $R_k \leftarrow \overline{R_i}$ | Inverter array |
| 2's Complement (Negate) | $R_k \leftarrow \overline{R_i} + 1$ | Inverter + incrementer |
| Transfer | $R_k \leftarrow R_i$ | Direct bus connection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtraction trick:** $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$. This is **why every practical ALU uses a single adder to perform both addition and subtraction** — it saves silicon and is the cornerstone of every modern ALU (e.g., MIPS, ARM, x86).

### 2.1.1 Binary Adder-Subtractor Hardware

A single $n$-bit binary adder is converted into an adder-subtractor by:
- Using the carry-in line as the mode selector $M$:
  - $M = 0$ → Add
  - $M = 1$ → Subtract (XOR gates feed $\overline{B}$ into the adder when $M = 1$, and $M$ feeds the $C_{in}$).

The sum/difference equation for each bit position $i$:

$$ S_i = A_i \oplus (B_i \oplus M) \oplus C_i $$

$$ C_{i+1} = (A_i \cdot (B_i \oplus M)) + (C_i \cdot (A_i \oplus (B_i \oplus M))) $$

## 2.2 Logic Micro-Operations

Logic operations are performed **bitwise** (independently on each bit position) and produce no carry between bits. There are exactly **16 possible logic functions** of two variables ($2^{2^2} = 16$); the most important subset is:

| Selector $S_1 S_0$ | Operation | Boolean Function |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | $F \leftarrow A \land B$ | $F_i = A_i \cdot B_i$ |
| 01 | $F \leftarrow A \lor B$ | $F_i = A_i + B_i$ |
| 10 | $F \leftarrow A \oplus B$ | $F_i = A_i \oplus B_i$ |
| 11 | $F \leftarrow \overline{A}$ | $F_i = \overline{A_i}$ |

The general hardware is an $n$-bit array of 2-to-1 multiplexers per bit, where each MUX selects between AND, OR, XOR, or NOT based on $S_1 S_0$. By expanding to a **4-to-1 MUX** per bit with two selector lines, the **standard 4-function logic unit** is realized with only NAND/NOR gates.

### 2.2.1 Applications of Logic Micro-Operations in Practice

- **Selective Set**: $A \leftarrow A \lor B$ — set bits of $A$ where $B$ has 1s.
- **Selective Clear**: $A \leftarrow A \land \overline{B}$ — clear bits of $A$ where $B$ has 1s.
- **Selective Complement (Toggle)**: $A \leftarrow A \oplus B$ — flip bits of $A$ where $B$ has 1s. This is **the universal mask operator**.

## 2.3 Shift Micro-Operations

Shift micro-operations move bits left or right within a register. There are three primary categories:

| Type | RTL | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Logical Shift Left (SHL)** | $R \leftarrow \text{shl}(R)$ | Bits move left; LSB ← 0; MSB lost or to flag $C$ |
| **Logical Shift Right (SHR)** | $R \leftarrow \text{shr}(R)$ | Bits move right; MSB ← 0; LSB lost or to flag $C$ |
| **Circular Shift Left (CIL)** | $R \leftarrow \text{cil}(R)$ | MSB wraps around to LSB |
| **Circular Shift Right (CIR)** | $R \leftarrow \text{cir}(R)$ | LSB wraps around to MSB |
| **Arithmetic Shift Right (ASHR)** | $R \leftarrow \text{ashr}(R)$ | MSB **stays the same** (sign extension); LSB lost |

> [!WARNING]
> **Arithmetic Shift Right preserves the sign bit (MSB)**, because the number is being halved in two's-complement representation. A **Logical** shift right inserts 0 — destroying the sign. This distinction appears in nearly every KTU/board exam problem.

### 2.3.1 Hardware: Bidirectional Shift Unit

A single 4-to-1 MUX per bit combined with a **mode selector** $H_1 H_0$ implements all four shifts plus the "no-shift" path:

| $H_1$ | $H_0$ | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | No shift (direct pass-through) |
| 0 | 1 | Shift Right |
| 1 | 0 | Shift Left |
| 1 | 1 | Circular (Rotate) Right |

## 2.4 The Composite Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)

A **single integrated ALU** combines the adder-subtractor, the logic unit, and the shift unit. A typical selector encoding for the canonical 8-function ALU is:

| $S_2 S_1 S_0$ | $C_{in}$ | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| 000 | 0 | $F = A + B$ (Add) |
| 001 | 0 | $F = A \cdot B$ (AND) |
| 010 | 0 | $F = A \oplus B$ (XOR) |
| 011 | 0 | $F = A$ (Transfer A) |
| 100 | 0 | $F = \overline{A}$ (NOT A) |
| 101 | 0 | $F = A + B + 1$ (Subtract A − B) |
| 110 | 0 | $F = A - 1$ (Decrement) |
| 111 | 0 | $F = A + 1$ (Increment) |

This 8-function ALU plus a status-flag generator produces the four condition codes of the PSW (Program Status Word): **C** (Carry), **V** (Overflow), **Z** (Zero), **N** (Negative/Sign).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Card

| Concept | Formula / Identity | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Subtraction via addition | $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$ | Always valid in 2's complement |
| 2's complement negate | $-A = \overline{A} + 1$ | For an $n$-bit number |
| Overflow detection (signed add) | $V = C_n \oplus C_{n-1}$ | $C_n$ = carry into sign bit, $C_{n-1}$ = carry out |
| Zero flag | $Z = \overline{F_{n-1} + F_{n-2} + \cdots + F_0}$ | All output bits are 0 |
| Sign flag | $N = F_{n-1}$ | MSB of result (2's complement) |
| Carry flag (unsigned) | $C = C_{out}$ of MSB | For $A + B + C_{in}$ |
| Number of Boolean logic micro-ops | $2^{2^n}$ functions of $n$ variables | For $n = 2$: $2^{2^2} = 16$ |
| ALU propagation delay | $T_{ALU} = T_{MUX} + T_{adder}$ | Critical-path delay |
| Control-logic minimization (Quine-McCluskey) | $L_i = \sum_{k} (I_k \cdot T_k \cdot B_k)$ | Sum of products over instruction cycles $T_k$ |
| Hardwired control decoder size | $2^{m}$ rows for $m$ control inputs | Exhaustive truth table |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: This exact ALU architecture (adder-subtractor + logic unit + shifter + flag logic) is the **arithmetic core of every general-purpose CPU** made since the 1970s — from the Intel 8086 to modern ARM Cortex cores. Mastering it is foundational for VLSI design, FPGA programming, and ASIC verification careers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing a 4-Bit Adder-Subtractor

**Problem:** Design a 4-bit adder-subtractor that performs $A + B$ when $M = 0$ and $A - B$ when $M = 1$, using the 2's-complement trick.

### Step 1 — Identify the bitwise logic

For each bit position $i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$, the bit entering the adder from the $B$ register must satisfy:

$$ B'_i = B_i \oplus M $$

This XOR achieves:
- When $M = 0$: $B'_i = B_i$ (normal $B$, used for addition).
- When $M = 1$: $B'_i = \overline{B_i}$ (inverted $B$, used for subtraction).

### Step 2 — Feed $M$ into the carry-in of bit 0

$$ C_{in} = M $$

When $M = 0$: $C_{in} = 0$ → addition.
When $M = 1$: $C_{in} = 1$ → completing the 2's-complement subtraction.

### Step 3 — Bitwise sum and carry equations

For each bit position $i$:

$$ S_i = A_i \oplus B'_i \oplus C_i = A_i \oplus (B_i \oplus M) \oplus C_i $$

$$ C_{i+1} = A_i \cdot B'_i + C_i \cdot (A_i \oplus B'_i) $$

Substituting $B'_i = B_i \oplus M$:

$$ C_{i+1} = A_i \cdot (B_i \oplus M) + C_i \cdot (A_i \oplus B_i \oplus M) $$

### Step 4 — Special handling of the final carry-out

The carry-out $C_4$ from the MSB position becomes:
- For **unsigned** subtraction: the borrow flag is $\overline{C_4}$.
- For **signed** overflow: $V = C_3 \oplus C_4$.

### Step 5 — Numerical verification

Let $A = 0110$ (decimal 6), $B = 0011$ (decimal 3), $M = 1$ (subtract).

**Step 5a:** Invert $B$: $\overline{B} = 1100$.
**Step 5b:** Add $\overline{B} + M = 1100 + 1 = 1101$ (this is $-B$ in 2's complement, equal to $-3$).
**Step 5c:** Compute $A + (\overline{B} + 1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\phantom{+\ }0110 \\
&+\ 1101 \\
&\hline
&1\ 0011
\end{aligned}
$$

Carry-out $C_4 = 1$ is discarded in 2's-complement arithmetic. Result $= 0011 = 3 = 6 - 3$. ✔

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Deriving the Overflow Flag Logic

**Problem:** Derive the Boolean expression for the signed-overflow flag $V$ when adding two 4-bit 2's-complement numbers.

### Step 1 — Define the relevant carries

Let $C_{in}^{(i)}$ be the carry *into* bit position $i$ and $C_{out}^{(i)}$ be the carry *out of* bit position $i$. For 4-bit signed addition:

$$ V = C_{in}^{(3)} \oplus C_{out}^{(3)} $$

The sign bit is bit 3, so we examine the carry entering bit 3 ($C_{in}^{(3)} = C_{out}^{(2)}$) and the carry leaving bit 3 ($C_{out}^{(3)} = C_4$).

### Step 2 — Justification

Overflow occurs only when **two numbers of the same sign produce a result of opposite sign** — which is mathematically impossible and indicates the 5-bit true result exceeded the 4-bit signed range $[-8, +7]$.

| $A_3$ (sign) | $B_3$ (sign) | $C_3$ (in) | $C_4$ (out) | $V = C_3 \oplus C_4$ | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | + + → +, OK |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | + + → −, **overflow** |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | − − → −, OK |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | − − → +, **overflow** |
| 0 | 1 | — | — | 0 | Opposite signs: never overflows |

### Step 3 — Final Boolean expression for $V$

$$ V = C_3 \oplus C_4 $$

Or, expanded in terms of inputs:

$$ V = (A_3 \cdot B_3 \cdot \overline{S_3}) + (\overline{A_3} \cdot \overline{B_3} \cdot S_3) $$

This is the canonical form tested in KTU board exams.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Designing a Single-Bit ALU Slice

**Problem:** Design a 1-bit ALU slice that performs AND, OR, XOR, and ADD on inputs $A$, $B$, and carry-in $C_{in}$. Produce outputs $F$ (result) and $C_{out}$.

### Step 1 — Define the selector

Use $S_1 S_0$:
- 00 → AND
- 01 → OR
- 10 → ADD
- 11 → XOR

### Step 2 — Logic function per bit

Let $Y_i = A_i + S_0 \cdot B_i + S_1 \cdot B_i$ (a clever trick to select between $A_i$, $B_i$, or both — but for clarity we use MUX-based design):

$$ Y_i = \overline{S_0} \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot (A_i \cdot B_i) + S_0 \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot (A_i + B_i) + \overline{S_0} \cdot S_1 \cdot (A_i \oplus B_i) $$

### Step 3 — Sum output for ADD mode

When $S_1 S_0 = 10$, we want full-adder behavior:

$$ F_i = S_1 \cdot (A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_{in}) + \overline{S_1} \cdot Y_i $$

The carry-out is only meaningful during ADD, so:

$$ C_{out} = S_1 \cdot (A_i \cdot B_i + A_i \cdot C_{in} + B_i \cdot C_{in}) $$

### Step 4 — Combine using a final 2-to-1 MUX

A single 4-to-1 MUX with selectors $S_1 S_0$ chooses among the four $F_i$ candidates. This produces a clean, modular ALU slice that can be cascaded into an $n$-bit ALU.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Hardwired Control Design (Processor Logic Design)

**Problem:** A CPU has two registers R0 and R1, an adder, and a shifter. We want to implement the **DCR R1** (Decrement R1) instruction as a 3-cycle micro-operation sequence:

| Cycle $T$ | Micro-operation | RTL | Active control signals |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_0$ | Read R1 into bus | $BUS \leftarrow R1$ | $R1_{out}$ |
| $T_1$ | Decrement via ALU | $AC \leftarrow R1 - 1$ | $R1_{out}$, $ALU_{dec}$, $AC_{in}$ |
| $T_2$ | Write AC back to R1 | $R1 \leftarrow AC$ | $AC_{out}$, $R1_{in}$ |

### Step 1 — Define control inputs

Let $I$ = the "DCR R1" instruction decode output (1 only when this instruction is fetched).
Let $T_0, T_1, T_2$ = timing signals from a 3-bit sequence counter (Moore machine).

### Step 2 — Write Boolean equations for each control output

| Control signal | Active when | Boolean equation |
|---|---|---|
| $R1_{out}$ | $T_0$ or $T_1$ | $I \cdot (T_0 + T_1)$ |
| $ALU_{dec}$ | $T_1$ | $I \cdot T_1$ |
| $AC_{in}$ | $T_1$ | $I \cdot T_1$ |
| $AC_{out}$ | $T_2$ | $I \cdot T_2$ |
| $R1_{in}$ | $T_2$ | $I \cdot T_2$ |

### Step 3 — Sequence counter next-state logic

To cycle $T_0 \to T_1 \to T_2 \to \text{done}$:

$$ T_0^{+} = \text{Start} \cdot \overline{T_2} $$
$$ T_1^{+} = T_0 $$
$$ T_2^{+} = T_1 $$

### Step 4 — Minimize via Karnaugh Map

For $R1_{out} = I \cdot (T_0 + T_1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R1_{out} &= I \cdot T_0 + I \cdot T_1
\end{aligned}
$$

No further minimization is possible (all four product terms cover essential prime implicants). The final gate-level schematic consists of one AND and one OR gate per control signal — the essence of **hardwired control**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Real CPUs have hundreds of such equations summed together, which is why industrial tools (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler) use Quine-McCluskey or Espresso for two-level minimization. The resulting logic is then technology-mapped to NAND/NOR gate libraries for fabrication.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ALU Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Register A] --> M1[Input MUX A]
    B[Register B] --> M2[Input MUX B]
    M1 --> ARITH[Arithmetic Unit Adder Subtractor]
    M2 --> ARITH
    M1 --> LOGIC[Logic Unit AND OR XOR NOT]
    M2 --> LOGIC
    M1 --> SHIFT[Shifter SHL SHR CIL CIR]
    M2 --> SHIFT
    ARITH --> M3[Output MUX]
    LOGIC --> M3
    SHIFT --> M3
    M3 --> OUT[Result Bus]
    ARITH --> FLAGS[Flag Generator C V Z N]
    SEL[Selector S2 S1 S0 Cin] --> M1
    SEL --> M2
    SEL --> M3
    SEL --> ARITH
    SEL --> LOGIC
    SEL --> SHIFT
```

## 4.2 Adder-Subtractor Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A3[A3] --> XOR3[XOR Gate M]
    B3[B3] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> FA3[Full Adder Bit 3]
    A2[A2] --> XOR2[XOR Gate M]
    B2[B2] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> FA2[Full Adder Bit 2]
    A1[A1] --> XOR1[XOR Gate M]
    B1[B1] --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> FA1[Full Adder Bit 1]
    A0[A0] --> XOR0[XOR Gate M]
    B0[B0] --> XOR0
    XOR0 --> FA0[Full Adder Bit 0]
    FA0 -- C1 --> FA1
    FA1 -- C2 --> FA2
    FA2 -- C3 --> FA3
    FA3 -- C4 Overflow and Carry --> OUT_C[Carry Out]
    M[Mode M] --> XOR0
    M --> XOR1
    M --> XOR2
    M --> XOR3
    M --> CIN[Cin of FA0]
    FA0 --> S0[S0]
    FA1 --> S1[S1]
    FA2 --> S2[S2]
    FA3 --> S3[S3]
```

## 4.3 Hardwired Control Unit — Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INST[Instruction Register OP CODE] --> DEC[Instruction Decoder]
    DEC --> D0[D0 DCR R1]
    DEC --> D1[D1 ADD R1 R2]
    DEC --> D2[D2 SHR R1]
    CLK[Clock] --> SEQ[Sequence Counter T0 T1 T2]
    SEQ --> T0N[T0]
    SEQ --> T1N[T1]
    SEQ --> T2N[T2]
    D0 --> AND1[AND gate]
    D1 --> AND2[AND gate]
    D2 --> AND3[AND gate]
    T0N --> AND1
    T0N --> AND2
    T0N --> AND3
    AND1 --> OR[OR gate Sum of Control Outputs]
    AND2 --> OR
    AND3 --> OR
    OR --> CTRL[Control Signals Bus to ALU Registers Bus Shifter]
```

## 4.4 Hardwired Control Unit — Schematic Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS [External Inputs]
        I[Instruction Bits I0 I1 I2]
        T[Timing Bits T0 T1 T2]
        CLK2[Clock]
        RST[Reset]
    end
    subgraph DECODER [Instruction Decoder]
        DEC2[Combinational Logic produces D0 D1 D2 etc]
    end
    subgraph COUNTER [Sequence Counter]
        FF0[D Flip Flop T0]
        FF1[D Flip Flop T1]
        FF2[D Flip Flop T2]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL [Control Logic Matrix]
        LOGIC[AND OR gates implementing sum of products]
    end
    I --> DEC2
    DEC2 --> LOGIC
    T --> LOGIC
    CLK2 --> FF0
    CLK2 --> FF1
    CLK2 --> FF2
    FF0 --> T
    FF1 --> T
    FF2 --> T
    LOGIC --> OUT[Control Outputs R0out R1out ALUop SHIFTop CIN LD etc]
    RST --> FF0
    RST --> FF1
    RST --> FF2
```

## 4.5 Processor Logic Design — Top-Level Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Identify all instructions] --> B[Step 2 Write Micro operation sequence for each]
    B --> C[Step 3 Assign timing cycles T0 T1 T2]
    C --> D[Step 4 List all control signals required]
    D --> E[Step 5 Derive Boolean equations from timing table]
    E --> F[Step 6 Minimize using K map or Quine McCluskey]
    F --> G[Step 7 Implement with gates and flip flops]
    G --> H[Step 8 Verify with timing diagram]
    H --> I[Complete Hardwired Control Unit]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a micro-operation. Differentiate between arithmetic, logic, and shift micro-operations with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **micro-operation** is the most elementary data-processing operation performed on information stored in CPU registers during a single clock cycle. It is the hardware-level building block of every machine instruction.

| Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic** | Numeric computation via the ALU adder | $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2$ (Addition) |
| **Logic** | Bitwise Boolean operation (no carry propagation) | $R3 \leftarrow R1 \oplus R2$ (XOR) |
| **Shift** | Bit displacement left or right within a register | $R3 \leftarrow \text{shr}(R1)$ (Shift Right) |

The three families share the same RTL syntax $R_{dest} \leftarrow f(R_{src})$ but are realized by **different sub-units** inside the ALU — the **adder-subtractor** for arithmetic, the **logic array** for logic, and the **bidirectional shifter** for shifts.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain how subtraction is implemented in a typical ALU using only an adder. Why is this design preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

Subtraction in an ALU is realized using the identity:

$$ A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1 $$

where $\overline{B}$ is the bitwise 1's complement of $B$ and the final $+1$ converts it to the 2's complement.

The hardware uses a single **$n$-bit binary adder**. For each bit $i$, an XOR gate is placed on the $B$ input:

$$ B'_i = B_i \oplus M $$

A mode line $M$ is connected to (i) every XOR input and (ii) the carry-in of bit 0:
- $M = 0$ → XOR passes $B_i$ unchanged, $C_{in}=0$ → operation is $A + B$.
- $M = 1$ → XOR inverts $B_i$ to $\overline{B_i}$, $C_{in}=1$ → operation is $A + \overline{B} + 1 = A - B$.

**Why preferred:** Reusing one adder for both add and subtract halves the silicon area, reduces power consumption, simplifies the control logic, and shortens the design cycle. It is the foundation of every modern ALU.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 (Alternative A). `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** List the four categories of shift micro-operations with RTL notation. Design a **4-bit bidirectional shift register** that supports logical shift left, logical shift right, circular shift right, and no-shift, using 4-to-1 multiplexers. Draw the schematic for one bit-slice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CPU has 8 registers $R_0$–$R_7$, an ALU, and a shifter. Explain step-by-step how you would design a **hardwired control unit** for the instruction `ADD R3, R1, R2` (i.e., $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2$). Provide the timing table, the Boolean equations for control signals, and the sequence-counter next-state logic.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B1(a)

**Step 1 — Categorize the shift micro-operations:**

| Shift Type | RTL | Effect on bit positions |
|---|---|---|
| Logical Shift Left | $R \leftarrow \text{shl}(R)$ | $R_{i+1} \leftarrow R_i$; $R_0 \leftarrow 0$; $R_{n-1}$ lost |
| Logical Shift Right | $R \leftarrow \text{shr}(R)$ | $R_{i-1} \leftarrow R_i$; $R_{n-1} \leftarrow 0$; $R_0$ lost |
| Circular Shift Right | $R \leftarrow \text{cir}(R)$ | $R_{i-1} \leftarrow R_i$; $R_{n-1} \leftarrow R_0$ |
| Circular Shift Left | $R \leftarrow \text{cil}(R)$ | $R_{i+1} \leftarrow R_i$; $R_0 \leftarrow R_{n-1}$ |

**Step 2 — Choose selector encoding:**

| $H_1$ | $H_0$ | Operation | New $R_i$ (general) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | No shift | $R_i$ |
| 0 | 1 | Shift Right | $R_{i-1}$ (for $i>0$), else 0 |
| 1 | 0 | Shift Left | $R_{i+1}$ (for $i<n-1$), else 0 |
| 1 | 1 | Cir Right | $R_{i-1}$ (for $i>0$), else $R_{n-1}$ |

**Step 3 — Single bit-slice design:**

For a generic internal bit $i$ ($0 < i < n-1$), the input to bit $i$ from the MUX is:

$$ D_i = \overline{H_1}\,\overline{H_0} R_i \;+\; \overline{H_1} H_0 R_{i-1} \;+\; H_1 \overline{H_0} R_{i+1} \;+\; H_1 H_0 R_{i-1} $$

For $i = 0$ and $i = n-1$, the boundary inputs (0 or wrap-around) are substituted. A 4-to-1 MUX implements each $D_i$ in one gate-level network. The four outputs $D_0, D_1, D_2, D_3$ are then clocked into the four flip-flops $Q_0, Q_1, Q_2, Q_3$ simultaneously — yielding the desired bidirectional shifter.

**Step 4 — Boundary conditions:**
- For $i = 0$: $D_0 = \overline{H_1}\,\overline{H_0} Q_0 + \overline{H_1} H_0 \cdot 0 + H_1 \overline{H_0} Q_1 + H_1 H_0 Q_3$.
- For $i = n-1$: $D_{n-1} = \overline{H_1}\,\overline{H_0} Q_{n-1} + \overline{H_1} H_0 Q_{n-2} + H_1 \overline{H_0} \cdot 0 + H_1 H_0 Q_0$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B1(b)

**Step 1 — Micro-operation sequence for `ADD R3, R1, R2`:**

| Cycle $T$ | Micro-operation | RTL | Active Control Signals |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_0$ | Read R1 onto bus A | $A \leftarrow R1$ | $R1_{out}$, $A_{in}$ |
| $T_1$ | Read R2 onto bus B | $B \leftarrow R2$ | $R2_{out}$, $B_{in}$ |
| $T_2$ | Perform addition in ALU | $AC \leftarrow A + B$ | $ALU_{add}$, $AC_{in}$ |
| $T_3$ | Write AC to R3 | $R3 \leftarrow AC$ | $AC_{out}$, $R3_{in}$ |

**Step 2 — Define decoder signal:**

Let $I$ = output of instruction decoder for `ADD R3,R1,R2` (1 during the entire execution of this instruction).

**Step 3 — Derive Boolean control equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R1_{out} &= I \cdot T_0 \\
R2_{out} &= I \cdot T_1 \\
A_{in} &= I \cdot T_0 \\
B_{in} &= I \cdot T_1 \\
ALU_{add} &= I \cdot T_2 \\
AC_{in} &= I \cdot T_2 \\
AC_{out} &= I \cdot T_3 \\
R3_{in} &= I \cdot T_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sequence counter next-state logic:**

For a 4-cycle sequence counter (D flip-flops):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0^{+} &= \text{Start} \cdot \overline{T_3} \\
T_1^{+} &= T_0 \\
T_2^{+} &= T_1 \\
T_3^{+} &= T_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Hardware implementation:**

- Each control equation is implemented with a 2-input AND gate feeding a wired-OR output bus.
- The sequence counter is built from three D flip-flops with the next-state equations above feeding back through inverters.

This forms a complete **hardwired control unit** for one instruction. For an entire ISA, the same procedure is repeated for each instruction; OR gates combine outputs of all instructions to form the final control bus.

---

### Question B2 (Alternative B). `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the design of a **4-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** that supports four operations: Add, Subtract, AND, and OR. Show the Boolean function table, selector encoding, and logic equations. Explain how the overflow flag is generated.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **complete processor logic design flow** for a single-accumulator CPU with the following three instructions: `LDA addr` (Load Accumulator from memory), `ADD addr` (Add memory to AC), and `STA addr` (Store AC to memory). Produce the micro-operation table, control signal list, and Boolean equations for each control signal.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B2(a)

**Step 1 — Define the four operations:**

| Selector $S_1 S_0$ | Operation | Equation per bit |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | $F = A + B$ | $F_i = A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_i$ |
| 01 | $F = A - B$ | $F_i = A_i \oplus \overline{B_i} \oplus C_i$, $C_{in}=1$ |
| 10 | $F = A \land B$ | $F_i = A_i \cdot B_i$ |
| 11 | $F = A \lor B$ | $F_i = A_i + B_i$ |

**Step 2 — Combined per-bit Boolean equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_i &= \overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0}\,(A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_i) \\
&\quad + \overline{S_1}\,S_0\,(A_i \oplus \overline{B_i} \oplus 1) \\
&\quad + S_1\,\overline{S_0}\,(A_i \cdot B_i) \\
&\quad + S_1\,S_0\,(A_i + B_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Carry-out generation (only active for Add/Subtract):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{i+1} &= \overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0}\,(A_i \cdot B_i + C_i\,(A_i \oplus B_i)) \\
&\quad + \overline{S_1}\,S_0\,(A_i \cdot \overline{B_i} + 1\cdot(A_i \oplus \overline{B_i}))
\end{aligned}
$$

For $S_1 = 1$ (logic modes), $C_{i+1} = 0$.

**Step 4 — Overflow flag derivation:**

$$
V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_n
$$

where $C_{n-1}$ is the carry into the sign-bit position and $C_n$ is the carry out of it. Equivalently, in terms of sign bits:

$$
V = (A_{n-1} \cdot B_{n-1} \cdot \overline{F_{n-1}}) + (\overline{A_{n-1}} \cdot \overline{B_{n-1}} \cdot F_{n-1})
$$

This signal goes to the status register and triggers an interrupt/trap on signed-overflow.

**Step 5 — Hardware realization:**

- Each bit slice contains: one 4-to-1 MUX (for function selection), one full adder (shared with logic), and one XOR array for the $B$ inverter.
- The carry chain ripples from bit 0 to bit $n-1$ (ripple-carry ALU). Faster designs use **carry-lookahead** or **carry-select** adders.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B2(b)

**Step 1 — Identify the three instructions and their micro-operations:**

| Instruction | Meaning | Cycle $T_0$ | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `LDA addr` | $AC \leftarrow M[addr]$ | $AR \leftarrow PC$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | $AR \leftarrow DR$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $AC \leftarrow DR$ |
| `ADD addr` | $AC \leftarrow AC + M[addr]$ | $AR \leftarrow PC$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | $AR \leftarrow DR$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $AC \leftarrow AC + DR$ |
| `STA addr` | $M[addr] \leftarrow AC$ | $AR \leftarrow PC$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | $AR \leftarrow DR$ | $DR \leftarrow AC$, $M[AR] \leftarrow DR$ |

**Step 2 — List of control signals:**

$PC_{out}$, $PC_{in}$, $AR_{in}$, $AR_{out}$, $DR_{out}$, $DR_{in}$, $AC_{out}$, $AC_{in}$, $ALU_{add}$, $READ$, $WRITE$, $INC_{PC}$.

**Step 3 — Decoder outputs:**

Let $D_0$ = LDA decode, $D_1$ = ADD decode, $D_2$ = STA decode.

**Step 4 — Boolean control equations:**

For cycle $T_0$ (fetch address — common to all three):

$$
PC_{out} = (D_0 + D_1 + D_2) \cdot T_0, \quad AR_{in} = (D_0 + D_1 + D_2) \cdot T_0
$$

For $T_1$ (memory read & PC increment — common):

$$
READ = (D_0 + D_1 + D_2) \cdot T_1, \quad DR_{in} = (D_0 + D_1 + D_2) \cdot T_1, \quad INC_{PC} = (D_0 + D_1 + D_2) \cdot T_1
$$

For $T_2$ (transfer address — common):

$$
DR_{out} = (D_0 + D_1 + D_2) \cdot T_2, \quad AR_{in} = (D_0 + D_1 + D_2) \cdot T_2
$$

For $T_3$ (instruction-specific):

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR_{in} \cdot T_3 &= (D_0 + D_1 + D_2) \cdot T_3 \\
AC_{in} &= D_0 \cdot T_3 + D_1 \cdot T_3 \quad (\text{not }D_2)\\
AC_{out} \cdot T_3 &= D_2 \cdot T_3 \\
WRITE &= D_2 \cdot T_3 \\
ALU_{add} &= D_1 \cdot T_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sequence counter:**

A 4-bit counter that resets at $T_3$ when END is asserted. The next-state logic mirrors the earlier example.

This structured flow — instruction list → micro-operation table → control signal list → Boolean equations → minimization → gate implementation — **is the canonical processor logic design methodology** prescribed by KTU Module 2.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the carry-in mode line $M$**: When asked to design an adder-subtractor, students often forget that the **same $M$** must drive BOTH the XOR gates AND the $C_{in}$ of the LSB. Drawing only the XOR array loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing Logical vs Arithmetic Shift Right**: Many write $R \leftarrow \text{shr}$ but apply it to a signed number without noting that the **sign bit must be preserved**. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Overflow Flag mis-wiring**: The overflow flag is $C_{n-1} \oplus C_n$, NOT $C_n$ alone. Using $C_n$ gives the **carry** flag (for unsigned arithmetic). Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this mix-up.
>
> 4. **Hardwired control — incomplete timing**: A common mistake is providing the micro-operation table but **not deriving the Boolean equations** for each control signal. KTU's Part-B questions for Processor Logic Design always require the equation derivation step.
>
> 5. **Skipping the instruction decoder**: Students often start straight from the micro-operations without defining the decoder output $D_i$ for each instruction. This causes equation errors and loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Unit confusion**: A "micro-operation" is **per cycle**; a "machine instruction" is **multiple cycles**. Mixing the two in the timing table loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ A **micro-operation** = the most basic data-movement/transform operation done in **one clock cycle**.
- ✅ Three families: **Arithmetic** (uses adder, generates carry), **Logic** (bitwise, no carry), **Shift** (bit displacement, may preserve or discard sign/MSB).
- ✅ Subtraction via 2's complement: $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$. Single adder reused via XOR + $C_{in} = M$.
- ✅ The **16** logic micro-operations of two variables come from $2^{2^2} = 16$; commonly the 4-function set AND/OR/XOR/NOT is implemented.
- ✅ Selective-Set: $A \lor B$ | Selective-Clear: $A \land \overline{B}$ | Selective-Complement: $A \oplus B$.
- ✅ Four shift modes: **SHL, SHR, CIL, CIR**; **Arithmetic SHR** preserves the sign bit.
- ✅ Status flags: **C** (unsigned carry), **V** (signed overflow $= C_{n-1} \oplus C_n$), **Z** (zero: NOR of all result bits), **N** (sign: MSB of result).
- ✅ Standard 8-function ALU uses a 3-bit selector $S_2 S_1 S_0$ plus a $C_{in}$.
- ✅ **Hardwired control design flow**: (1) List instructions → (2) Write micro-operations per cycle → (3) Build timing table → (4) Identify control signals → (5) Derive Boolean equations → (6) Minimize via K-map / Quine-McCluskey → (7) Realize with gates + sequence counter flip-flops.
- ✅ Sequence counter uses D flip-flops in a one-hot or binary encoding; each cycle $T_k$ is one flip-flop output.
- ✅ Control signal equation general form: $S_{signal} = \sum_{\text{inst}} D_{inst} \cdot T_k \cdot \text{condition}$.
- ✅ The advantage of hardwired control is **speed** (no microinstruction fetch); the disadvantage is **inflexibility** (any ISA change requires re-fabrication).
- ✅ Always distinguish: **Instruction** (programmer-visible) vs **Micro-operation** (hardware-internal) vs **Micro-order / Control signal** (single wire).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processor Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Processor Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Processor Organization** (also termed *CPU Organization* or *Central Processing Unit Architecture*) refers to the internal structural arrangement, interconnection, and operational interplay of the fundamental subunits that collectively form the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** of a digital computer. It encompasses the design of the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, the **Register File**, the **Control Unit (CU)**, the **Internal CPU Buses**, the **Data Path**, and the **Interface to external memory and I/O subsystems**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 2), Processor Organization specifically addresses *how* the CPU is physically and logically built — the registers it contains, how these registers communicate via internal buses, how the ALU is structured, how instructions are decoded, and how micro-operations are sequenced to execute machine instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEOT403, Module 2):**
> *"Processor Organization — Register Organization, Bus Systems (3-bus, 2-bus, 1-bus), ALU Organization, Control Unit Organization (Hardwired and Microprogrammed), Stack Organization, Instruction Formats, Addressing Modes, and Micro-operations."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Factory Production Line

Imagine a **highly automated automobile assembly plant**:
- The **Raw Material Store** = *Main Memory (RAM)*, holding parts (data and instructions).
- The **Conveyor Belts** = *System and Internal Buses*, transporting parts.
- The **Robotic Welding & Painting Arms** = *ALU*, performing arithmetic and logic transformations.
- The **Supervisor with a Clipboard** = *Control Unit*, dictating each step's sequence and timing.
- The **Small Lockers beside each Robot** = *Registers* (Accumulator, Program Counter, Instruction Register), holding immediate values.
- The **Blueprint** = *Instruction*, fetched and decoded.

**Processor Organization** is the *blueprint of the assembly plant itself* — the architectural plan that defines where every robot, conveyor, locker, and supervisor is placed, and how they interconnect. Just as a poorly designed factory creates bottlenecks, a poorly organized CPU suffers from performance bottlenecks, bus contention, and inefficient instruction execution.

## 1.3 Why Processor Organization Matters in Engineering

The choice of organization directly impacts:
1. **Performance (Clock Cycles Per Instruction — CPI)**
2. **Hardware cost (gate count, silicon area)**
3. **Power dissipation (dynamic switching)**
4. **Instruction throughput and pipelining feasibility**
5. **Compiler optimization opportunities**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — Performance Equation (Foundation for all CPU design):**
>
> $$\text{CPU Execution Time} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}}{\text{Clock Rate}}$$
>
> Processor Organization is the lever an architect uses to **reduce CPI** and **increase Clock Rate**.

## 1.4 Functional Sub-Units at a Glance

| Sub-Unit | Primary Role | Standard Count in KTU-Represented CPU |
|----------|--------------|---------------------------------------|
| **ALU** | Arithmetic & Logic operations | **1** core arithmetic unit |
| **Register File** | Fast on-chip temporary storage | **8–64** general-purpose registers |
| **Control Unit** | Instruction decoding & micro-operation sequencing | **1** (Hardwired or Microprogrammed) |
| **Internal Buses** | Inter-register data movement | **1, 2, or 3** bus architecture |
| **PC, IR, MAR, MBR** | Dedicated control registers | **4** standard |
