## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Processor Design

| Metric | Standard Value / Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Clock Cycle Time (T)** | **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)** | Duration of one clock pulse |
| **Clock Frequency (f)** | **Hz, MHz, GHz** | $f = 1/T$ |
| **Word Length (n)** | **8, 16, 32, 64 bits** | Bits processed per ALU operation |
| **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** | dimensionless | Average cycles to execute one instruction |
| **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)** | $10^6$ instr/sec | Throughput metric |
| **Addressing Modes** | typically **5–12** | Ways to locate operands |

> [!NOTE]
> The two most important **KTU 2024 design philosophies** you must master are **Hardwired Control** and **Microprogrammed Control**. They are the heart of every CPU design question in the ESE paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Processor Unit

The Processor Unit is a synchronous digital system. All operations are broken down into **micro-operations** — elementary register-transfer operations (RTOs) executed in one clock cycle.

### 2.1.1 The Register Set (The CPU's Local Memory)

Registers are the **highest-speed** storage elements in the computer, fabricated directly on the CPU silicon.

**A. User-Visible Registers** (visible to the assembly-language programmer):
- **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** — used for any operation, e.g., AX, BX in x86; R0–R12 in ARM.
- **Data Registers** — hold operands for ALU.
- **Address Registers** — hold memory addresses, e.g., MAR, segment registers.
- **Condition Codes / Flags (PSW)** — single-bit status: Zero (Z), Carry (C), Sign (N), Overflow (V), Parity (P).

**B. Control and Status Registers** (used by the CU / OS):
- **Program Counter (PC)** — address of the next instruction to fetch.
- **Instruction Register (IR)** — holds the currently executing instruction.
- **Memory Address Register (MAR)** — connects CPU to the address bus.
- **Memory Data Register (MDR / MBR)** — connects CPU to the data bus.
- **Program Status Word (PSW)** — collection of all condition flags.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A very common 3-mark question is: *"List the registers in a basic CPU and state the function of each."* Memorize PC, IR, MAR, MDR, AC (Accumulator), and the flag register.

### 2.1.2 The ALU (Arithmetic and Logic Unit)

The ALU is a combinational circuit. It takes two inputs (operands A and B), a function-select code, and produces a result plus status flags.

**Operations supported by a typical ALU:**

| Category | Operations |
|---|---|
| Arithmetic | Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide, Increment, Decrement |
| Logic | AND, OR, XOR, NOT, NAND, NOR |
| Shift | Logical shift left/right, Arithmetic shift, Rotate |

**Status Flags generated by the ALU:**
- **C (Carry)**: $C = 1$ if an unsigned overflow occurred.
- **N (Sign / Negative)**: $N = $ MSB of the result.
- **Z (Zero)**: $Z = 1$ if all result bits are 0.
- **V (Overflow)**: $V = 1$ if a signed overflow occurred.
- **P (Parity)**: $P = 1$ if the result has an even number of 1s.

### 2.1.3 The Control Unit (CU) — The CPU's "Conductor"

The CU is responsible for issuing, in the correct sequence, all the control signals that govern the movement of data between registers, the ALU, and the system bus.

> [!NOTE]
> The two principal design philosophies for the Control Unit are:
> 1. **Hardwired Control** — uses fixed combinational logic (gates, decoders, FSMs).
> 2. **Microprogrammed Control** — uses a microcode ROM/Control Store where every machine instruction is broken into a stored sequence of microinstructions.

## 2.2 The Internal Data Path — The "Nervous System" of the CPU

The data path is the collection of buses and multiplexers that interconnect the registers and the ALU. There are three classic organizations:

### A. Single-Bus Internal Organization
- All registers share **one common internal bus**.
- Only one register can drive the bus at a time.
- **Disadvantage:** Slow — many data movements require multiple cycles.
- **Advantage:** Simple, low hardware cost.

### B. Two-Bus Internal Organization
- Two internal buses allow **two registers to be read simultaneously**.
- Faster: operand fetch + ALU input in a single cycle.
- Slight increase in hardware complexity.

### C. Three-Bus Internal Organization
- **Two source buses** and **one destination bus**.
- The ALU can read both operands and write the result back in **a single clock cycle**.
- **Used in modern high-performance RISC processors** (e.g., MIPS, ARM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A three-bus data path allows the micro-operation `R3 ← R1 + R2` to be completed in **just one clock cycle**, whereas the same operation on a single-bus CPU takes **three cycles** (since the ALU has only one input from the bus).

## 2.3 Micro-Operations and Register Transfer Language (RTL)

Every machine instruction is decomposed into a sequence of micro-operations. The KTU convention is to write them as **register transfer statements**.

**Format:**
$$
\text{Register} \leftarrow \text{Function}(\text{Source Registers}) \; ; \; \text{Control Signals}
$$

**Worked example — Instruction: ADD R1, R2** (meaning R1 ← R1 + R2)

| Step | Micro-operation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MAR ← PC` | Address of next instruction loaded into MAR |
| 2 | `MDR ← M[MAR]` ; `PC ← PC + 1` | Instruction fetched, PC incremented |
| 3 | `IR ← MDR` | Instruction loaded into IR |
| 4 | `A ← R1` ; `B ← R2` | Operands routed to ALU inputs |
| 5 | `R1 ← ALU(A + B)` | ALU performs addition; result stored |

## 2.4 Addressing Modes

Addressing modes specify *how* the operand of an instruction is located. KTU expects you to know each mode with a worked example.

| # | Mode | Effective Address (EA) | Example | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immediate** | Operand is in the instruction itself | `MOV R1, #5` | Loading constants |
| 2 | **Direct (Absolute)** | EA = Address field in instruction | `MOV R1, [2000]` | Accessing globals |
| 3 | **Indirect** | EA = (Contents of address field) | `MOV R1, [R2]` | Pointers |
| 4 | **Register** | Operand is in a register | `ADD R1, R2` | Fast operations |
| 5 | **Register Indirect** | EA = (Contents of register) | `MOV R1, (R2)` | Memory access via pointer |
| 6 | **Displacement (Indexed)** | EA = A + (R) | `MOV R1, 100(R2)` | Array access |
| 7 | **Relative** | EA = PC + Offset | Branch instructions | Loops, conditionals |
| 8 | **Auto-increment / Auto-decrement** | EA = (R) ; R ← R ± 1 | Stack operations | Sequential processing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trick:** Questions often ask: *"For the instruction `ADD R1, 2000(R2)`, identify the addressing mode."* The answer is **Indexed / Displacement Addressing Mode**, because the effective address is `2000 + (R2)`.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Clock frequency | $f = 1/T$ | Hz, where $T$ = clock period |
| CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T$ | Seconds, $N$ = instruction count |
| MIPS Rating | $\text{MIPS} = f / (CPI \times 10^6)$ | Million instructions per second |
| Throughput | $TP = 1 / (CPI \times T)$ | Instructions per second |
| Speedup (S) | $S = T_{old} / T_{new}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| Amdahl's Law | $S_{overall} = 1 / ((1-f) + f/n)$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $n$ = speedup of part |
| 3-bus micro-op time | $t = 1 \text{ cycle}$ | ALU → dest. in one step |
| 1-bus micro-op time | $t = 3 \text{ cycles}$ | For two-operand ALU op |
| 2's complement overflow | $V = C_{in} \oplus C_{out}$ (MSB) | Boolean: XOR of carry into and out of MSB |
| Carry flag | $C = C_{out}$ (MSB) | Boolean: carry out of MSB |
| Zero flag | $Z = \overline{(R_{n-1} + \cdots + R_0)}$ | NOR of all result bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** CPU pipelining (a logical extension of this module) is what enables modern smartphones to execute **billions of instructions per second**. The micro-operation concept you learn here is the *exact* foundation for how RISC-V, ARM, and Intel CPUs internally schedule work.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CPU Execution Time from Fundamental Quantities

We derive the central performance equation used in every computer architecture problem.

**Given:**
- $I$ = total number of instructions executed in a program (Instruction Count).
- $CPI$ = average cycles per instruction.
- $f$ = clock frequency (cycles/second).
- $T$ = clock period (seconds per cycle), where $T = 1/f$.

**Step 1 — Total clock cycles required by the program.**

Every instruction requires on average $CPI$ clock cycles. Therefore, the total number of clock cycles is the product of the number of instructions and the cycles per instruction:

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = I \times CPI
$$

**Step 2 — Convert cycles into seconds.**

Since the clock runs at $f$ cycles per second, one cycle takes $T = 1/f$ seconds. Hence the CPU time is:

$$
T_{CPU} = (\text{Total Cycles}) \times T
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression from Step 1.**

$$
T_{CPU} = I \times CPI \times T
$$

**Step 4 — Replace $T$ with $1/f$ to obtain the frequency-based form.**

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{I \times CPI}{f}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the MIPS rating.**

MIPS is defined as the number of millions of instructions executed per second:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{I}{T_{CPU} \times 10^6}
$$

Substituting $T_{CPU} = I \times CPI \times T$:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{I}{I \times CPI \times T \times 10^6} = \frac{1}{CPI \times T \times 10^6}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $f$:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}
$$

This is the **single most-tested formula** in KTU COA problems. Memorize its three forms.

## 3.2 Derivation: Two's Complement Overflow Detection

When two n-bit signed numbers are added, the Overflow flag $V$ must be correctly computed.

**Define:**
- $C_{in}$ = carry into the most significant bit (MSB).
- $C_{out}$ = carry out of the MSB.

**Logic:** Overflow occurs **only** when the two operands have the same sign, but the result has a different sign. By Boolean analysis, this condition is exactly the XOR of $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ at the MSB position.

$$
V = C_{in} \oplus C_{out}
$$

**Truth table verification:**

| $C_{in}$ | $C_{out}$ | $V$ | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | No overflow |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Overflow (sign changed) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Overflow (sign changed) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | No overflow |

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Micro-Operation Sequence

**Problem:** Execute the instruction `ADD R1, R2` on a **single-bus CPU** with registers PC, MAR, MDR, IR, AC, R1, R2, ALU, and Memory. The instruction format is:
- Bits 15–12: Opcode (ADD = 0001)
- Bits 11–9: Destination register
- Bits 8–6: Source register
- Bits 5–0: Unused

Assume R1 is the destination and R2 is the source. The final operation is `R1 ← R1 + R2`.

**Phase 1 — Fetch**

| T-State | Micro-operation | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| $T_0$ | `MAR ← PC` | Address of next instruction placed on address bus |
| $T_1$ | `MDR ← M[MAR]` ; `PC ← PC + 1` | Memory reads instruction; PC advances |
| $T_2$ | `IR ← MDR` | Instruction loaded into IR for decoding |

**Phase 2 — Decode**

| T-State | Micro-operation | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| $T_3$ | `A ← R1` ; `B ← R2` | Operands placed on the single bus simultaneously using temporary A and B latches |

**Phase 3 — Execute**

| T-State | Micro-operation | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| $T_4$ | `ALU adds A and B` | Combinational operation completes |
| $T_5$ | `R1 ← ALU output` | Result latched back into R1 |

**Total cycles for ADD on a single-bus CPU = 6 T-states.**

On a **3-bus CPU**, the same instruction completes in just 3 T-states (fetch + decode + execute-with-result in one cycle). This **2× speedup** is why modern CPUs use multi-bus architectures.

## 3.4 Derivation: Speedup from Pipelining (KTU Frequently Asked)

For an $k$-stage pipeline processing $N$ instructions:

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = k + (N - 1)
$$

**Derivation:** The first instruction takes $k$ cycles to traverse all stages. Each subsequent instruction completes one stage per cycle. With $N-1$ follow-up instructions, the additional cycles equal $N-1$. Adding:

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = k + N - 1
$$

**Speedup** (for $N \gg k$):

$$
S = \frac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1} \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} k
$$

This shows the theoretical **maximum speedup equals the number of pipeline stages**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Complete CPU

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical **single-bus CPU architecture** taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus. The bus shown in the center is the internal CPU bus; the address and data buses connect to external memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU_BOUNDARY["PROCESSOR UNIT CPU CHIP"]
        direction TB
        PC["PC Program Counter"]
        MAR["MAR Memory Address Register"]
        MDR["MDR Memory Data Register"]
        IR["IR Instruction Register"]
        AC["AC Accumulator"]
        R1["R1 General Register"]
        R2["R2 General Register"]
        R3["R3 General Register"]
        ALU["ALU Arithmetic Logic Unit"]
        FLAGS["FLAGS Z C N V P"]
        CU["CU Control Unit"]
        DEC["INSTRUCTION DECODER"]
    end

    IBUS(("INTERNAL CPU BUS"))

    PC --- IBUS
    MAR --- IBUS
    MDR --- IBUS
    IR --- IBUS
    AC --- IBUS
    R1 --- IBUS
    R2 --- IBUS
    R3 --- IBUS
    ALU --- IBUS

    IR --> DEC
    DEC --> CU
    ALU --> FLAGS
    CU -. control signals .-> PC
    CU -. control signals .-> MAR
    CU -. control signals .-> MDR
    CU -. control signals .-> IR
    CU -. control signals .-> ALU
    CU -. control signals .-> AC

    ABUS(["ADDRESS BUS to Main Memory"])
    DBUS(["DATA BUS to Main Memory"])
    MAR === ABUS
    MDR === DBUS

    MEM["MAIN MEMORY RAM ROM"] === ABUS
    MEM === DBUS
```

## 4.2 Micro-Operation Flow: Instruction Execution Cycle

This Mermaid sequence diagram captures the **Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store** cycle for a typical arithmetic instruction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1(["START Fetch Phase"]) --> A1["T0 MAR PC"]
    A1 --> A2["T1 MDR Memory MAR PC PC+1"]
    A2 --> A3["T2 IR MDR"]
    A3 --> D1(["DECODE Phase"])
    D1 --> D2["Instruction Decoder Reads Opcode from IR"]
    D2 --> D3["Control Unit Generates Micro operation Signals"]
    D3 --> E1(["EXECUTE Phase"])
    E1 --> E2["Operands Read from Registers to ALU Latches A and B"]
    E2 --> E3["ALU Performs Operation and Updates Flags"]
    E3 --> S2(["STORE Phase"])
    S2 --> S3["Result Written Back to Destination Register"]
    S3 --> S4(["END Next Instruction"])
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Three-Bus Internal Data Path

The most advanced (and most KTU-relevant) internal organization.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BUS_A(("SOURCE BUS A"))
    BUS_B(("SOURCE BUS B"))
    BUS_D(("DESTINATION BUS D"))

    R1["R1"] --- BUS_A
    R2["R2"] --- BUS_B
    R3["R3"] --- BUS_A
    R4["R4"] --- BUS_B

    BUS_A --> ALU["ALU"]
    BUS_B --> ALU
    ALU --> BUS_D

    BUS_D --- R5["R5"]
    BUS_D --- R6["R6"]
    BUS_D --- R7["R7"]
    BUS_D --- PC["PC"]
    BUS_D --- IR["IR"]
    BUS_D --- MAR["MAR"]
```

**Explanation:** In one clock cycle, two source registers are placed on buses A and B, the ALU computes a function, and the result is written to a destination register on bus D. This enables single-cycle ALU operations.

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram: Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HARDWIRED["HARDWIRED CONTROL UNIT"]
        H1["Instruction Opcode"] --> H2["Instruction Decoder"]
        H2 --> H3["Timing Generator State Machine"]
        H3 --> H4["Combinational Logic Gates"]
        H1 --> H4
        H4 --> H5["Control Signals to Registers and ALU"]
    end

    subgraph MICRO["MICROPROGRAMMED CONTROL UNIT"]
        M1["Instruction Opcode"] --> M2["Mapping Logic"]
        M2 --> M3["Control Address Register CAR"]
        M3 --> M4["Control Store ROM containing Microcode"]
        M4 --> M5["Control Data Register CDR"]
        M5 --> M6["Control Signals to Registers and ALU"]
        M6 --> M3
    end
```

| Property | Hardwired | Microprogrammed |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Combinational gates, PLA, FSM | ROM with microinstructions |
| Speed | **Faster** (parallel) | Slower (ROM access time) |
| Flexibility | Hard to modify | **Easy** to patch microcode |
| Used in | RISC, embedded cores | CISC (e.g., classic x86) |
| Design effort | Very high | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define the term "Processor Unit" and list its three major sub-units. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **Processor Unit (CPU)** is the central hardware component of a computer system that fetches, decodes, and executes instructions. It is also called the *brain* of the computer. Its three major sub-units are:

1. **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs all arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logic (AND, OR, XOR, shift) operations.
2. **Control Unit (CU):** Generates the control signals that orchestrate the movement of data between registers, ALU, and memory.
3. **Register Set:** High-speed on-chip storage for operands, addresses, and status information.

---

### Question A2: Differentiate between Hardwired and Microprogrammed Control Units. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Fixed combinational logic / PLA / FSM | Microcode stored in Control Store (ROM) |
| Speed | **Faster** (no ROM access) | Slower (requires ROM lookup) |
| Flexibility | Difficult to modify or extend | **Easy to modify** by changing microcode |
| Cost of design | Very high for complex ISA | Moderate |
| Typical use | RISC, pipelined CPUs | CISC processors |
| Control word generation | Logic gates derive each bit | Each microinstruction is one control word |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

### Question A: CPU Architecture, Data Path and Micro-Operations [14 Marks]

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Module 2 Choice 1]`

**Sub-question (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the block diagram of a basic single-bus CPU and explain the function of each register. Explain how the instruction `ADD R1, R2` is fetched, decoded, and executed using a sequence of micro-operations.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 (the single-bus CPU block diagram).

**Function of Each Register:**
- **PC (Program Counter):** Holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched.
- **MAR (Memory Address Register):** Holds the memory address used to access RAM.
- **MDR (Memory Data Register):** Temporarily stores the data read from / written to memory.
- **IR (Instruction Register):** Holds the currently executing instruction.
- **AC (Accumulator):** A primary register for ALU operations.
- **R1, R2, R3 (General Purpose Registers):** Hold operands and intermediate results.
- **FLAGS:** Status bits Z, C, N, V, P reflecting the result of the last ALU operation.

**Micro-Operations for `ADD R1, R2` (R1 ← R1 + R2):**

**Phase 1 — Fetch:**

| T-State | Micro-operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| $T_0$ | `MAR ← PC` | Address of next instruction placed on address bus |
| $T_1$ | `MDR ← M[MAR]` ; `PC ← PC + 1` | Instruction read from memory, PC incremented |
| $T_2$ | `IR ← MDR` | Instruction loaded into IR |

**Phase 2 — Decode:**

The instruction decoder reads the opcode bits of the IR, identifies the operation as ADD, and signals the CU accordingly.

**Phase 3 — Execute:**

| T-State | Micro-operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| $T_3$ | `A ← R1` ; `B ← R2` | Operands copied to ALU latches via the internal bus |
| $T_4$ | `ALU performs A + B` | Addition executed, status flags updated |
| $T_5$ | `R1 ← ALU result` | Sum stored back into R1 |

**Sub-question (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the three-bus internal organization of the CPU. How does it differ from the single-bus organization? Show how the operation `R3 ← R1 + R2` is completed in a single cycle.

**Model Solution:**

**Three-Bus Internal Organization:**
In a three-bus CPU, there are **two source buses (Bus A and Bus B)** and **one destination bus (Bus D)**. The ALU's two inputs are tied to Bus A and Bus B, and its output drives Bus D. All registers have tristate drivers connecting them to these buses.

**Difference from Single-Bus:**

| Feature | Single-Bus | Three-Bus |
|---|---|---|
| Number of internal buses | 1 | 3 (2 source + 1 dest.) |
| Registers readable per cycle | 1 | 2 (one on A, one on B) |
| ALU op + store time | 3 cycles | **1 cycle** |
| Hardware cost | Low | Higher |
| Speed | Slower | **Faster** |

**Single-Cycle Execution of `R3 ← R1 + R2`:**

In **one clock cycle**, the following happens in parallel:
- R1 drives **Bus A**
- R2 drives **Bus B**
- ALU performs `A + B`
- The ALU output drives **Bus D**
- R3 latches the value from Bus D

The entire micro-operation is described in a single RTL statement:

$$
R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2 \quad ; \quad \text{Control signals: } BusA\leftarrow R1,\ BusB\leftarrow R2,\ ALU_{op}\leftarrow ADD,\ BusD\rightarrow R3
$$

This single-cycle parallel execution is the architectural foundation of modern RISC processors.

---

### Question B: Control Unit Design and Addressing Modes [14 Marks]

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2 Choice 2]`

**Sub-question (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the design of a **Microprogrammed Control Unit**. Discuss the role of the Control Store, CAR, and CDR. List its advantages over a Hardwired Control Unit.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.4 (the Microprogrammed Control diagram).

**Components:**
- **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched from the Control Store.
- **Control Store (ROM):** A ROM that contains the microprogram — the binary microinstructions that implement every machine instruction.
- **Control Data Register (CDR):** Holds the microinstruction just read from the Control Store; its individual bits are routed as control signals to the data path.
- **Mapping Logic:** Converts the machine instruction opcode into the starting address of its corresponding microprogram in the Control Store.
- **Sequencer / Next Address Logic:** Determines the address of the next microinstruction (next, branch, or jump).

**Working:**
1. The machine instruction in IR is sent to the Mapping Logic.
2. The Mapping Logic loads the starting microaddress of that instruction's microprogram into the **CAR**.
3. The Control Store is read at that address; the microinstruction flows into the **CDR**.
4. The CDR's bits enable/disable register loading, ALU operations, and bus connections.
5. The sequencer updates the CAR for the next microinstruction. The cycle repeats until the microprogram ends.

**Advantages over Hardwired Control:**
- **Flexibility:** Microcode can be patched or rewritten to add new instructions.
- **Easier design:** No complex combinational logic to design for each instruction.
- **Scalability:** Adding a new instruction only requires adding more microcode in the ROM.
- **Less error-prone:** Software-like development of control logic.
- **Disadvantage:** Slower than hardwired (extra ROM access latency).

**Sub-question (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain any **five addressing modes** with examples. For each mode, state the rule for computing the **Effective Address (EA)**.

**Model Solution:**

| # | Addressing Mode | EA Formula | Example Instruction | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immediate** | Operand is constant in instruction | `MOV R1, #5` | R1 is loaded with the literal value 5 directly; no memory access needed. |
| 2 | **Direct (Absolute)** | EA = Address field of instruction | `LOAD R1, [2000]` | The memory location at address 2000 is fetched into R1. |
| 3 | **Register Indirect** | EA = (Contents of register R) | `LOAD R1, (R2)` | R2 contains the address; the value at that address is loaded into R1. Useful for pointers. |
| 4 | **Indexed (Displacement)** | EA = A + (R) | `LOAD R1, 100(R2)` | Address = constant 100 + contents of R2. Ideal for array access. |
| 5 | **Relative** | EA = PC + Offset | `BRANCH -4` (loop jump) | Used for branch instructions; jumps to a location relative to the current PC. |
| 6 | **Register** | Operand = (Register) | `ADD R1, R2` | Both operands are in CPU registers — fastest mode, no memory access. |
| 7 | **Auto-increment** | EA = (R); then R ← R + 1 | `LOAD R1, (R2)+` | Address comes from R2; after the access, R2 is incremented automatically. Useful for sequential processing. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Recall Point:** For the displacement mode, the effective address is computed as $EA = A + R$, where $A$ is the constant in the instruction and $R$ is the contents of the index register.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in Processor Unit questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the bus counts** — A *single-bus* CPU has ONE internal bus, but a *three-bus* CPU has THREE. Students frequently miscount the destination bus.
> 2. **Forgetting to increment PC** — During the fetch phase ($T_1$), `PC ← PC + 1` must be shown explicitly. The KTU examiner deducts 1 mark if omitted.
> 3. **Confusing Addressing Modes** — "Register Indirect" and "Indexed" look similar but differ: Register Indirect uses the register *content* as the address; Indexed adds the register content to a *constant*.
> 4. **Hardwired vs Microprogrammed** — Students often write "Hardwired is faster" but forget to mention "Hardwired is also more rigid / less flexible." The examiner expects **both** sides.
> 5. **Missing status flag updates** — When describing ALU operations, you must mention that flags (Z, C, N, V) are updated. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Notation errors in RTL** — Always use `←` (the assignment symbol), never `=` or `==`. The KTU standard is strict.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Processor Unit (Module 2)**

- **CPU** = ALU + Control Unit + Register Set.
- **Register hierarchy:** PC, IR, MAR, MDR are control registers; AC, R0–Rn are data/general-purpose registers.
- **Flag register (PSW):** contains Z, C, N, V, P.
- **Three internal bus organizations:** single (slow, cheap), two, three (fast, expensive).
- **Three-bus advantage:** ALU operations complete in **one clock cycle**; single-bus requires 3 cycles.
- **Micro-operation sequence for any instruction:** Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store.
- **Hardwired control** = fast, rigid, combinational logic; used in RISC.
- **Microprogrammed control** = slow, flexible, microcode in ROM; used in CISC.
- **Addressing modes to know (KTU expects 5–7):** Immediate, Direct, Indirect, Register, Register Indirect, Indexed, Relative, Auto-increment.
- **Effective Address (EA)** for displacement mode: $EA = A + (R)$.
- **Overflow detection:** $V = C_{in} \oplus C_{out}$ at the MSB.
- **Carry flag:** $C = C_{out}$ at the MSB.
- **Zero flag:** $Z = 1$ if all result bits are 0.
- **CPU Time formula:** $T_{CPU} = I \times CPI \times T = \dfrac{I \times CPI}{f}$.
- **MIPS formula:** $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$.
- **Pipeline speedup (k stages, N instructions):** total cycles $= k + N - 1$; max speedup $\to k$ as $N \to \infty$.
- **Instruction cycle phases:** Fetch (3 T-states) + Decode + Execute + Store.
- **Modern processors** use multi-bus data paths and pipelined control units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Accumulator (Basic Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Accumulator (Basic Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEOT403 – Computer Organization and Architecture)**, an **Accumulator** is formally defined as:

> A dedicated, high-speed internal **CPU register** that is implicitly used as one of the operands (and the destination) for most arithmetic and logic operations performed by the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU). It forms the central working register of a single-address instruction format.

The accumulator is a **fundamental building block** of a CPU's data path, situated at the heart of the datapath. In accumulator-based architectures (such as early von Neumann machines), the accumulator is the **default operand location** referenced by most instructions without the need to explicitly specify it in the instruction word.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The accumulator is studied as a foundational element of the **CPU Data Path**, **Register Organization**, and **Instruction Execution Cycle**. Understanding the accumulator is a prerequisite for topics like the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**, **ALU design**, and **Microprogrammed Control Units**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the accumulator as the **"tally counter in a cashier's hand"** 🧮:

| Real-World Scenario | Computer Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A cashier keeps a running total on a small handheld counter | The **accumulator** holds intermediate arithmetic results |
| The cashier reads item prices from a price list | Data fetched from **memory (data bus)** |
| The cashier adds each item to the running total | The **ALU** performs the addition with the accumulator |
| The final total is written onto the bill | The accumulator's value is **stored back to memory** |

Just as the cashier's counter is *always* the "default place" where calculations accumulate, the CPU's accumulator is *always* the "default register" used during arithmetic/logic operations.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of an Accumulator

- **Implicit Operand**: The instruction does not need to specify the accumulator's address — it is assumed.
- **Single-Address Format**: Instructions like `ADD X` mean "Add memory location X to the accumulator."
- **Fastest Accessible Register**: It is hardwired to one input of the ALU, providing the shortest critical path.
- **Word-Length Aligned**: Its bit-width is equal to the CPU's **word size** (e.g., **8-bit**, **16-bit**, or **32-bit**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Standard Metric (KTU Typical):** In introductory CPU design problems, an accumulator is most commonly modeled as an **8-bit register**, matching the basic word size of a simple accumulator-based CPU (e.g., the classic 8085 or a pedagogical SAP – Simple As Possible computer).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Role of the Accumulator in CPU Data Path

The accumulator sits at a strategic position in the CPU. Let us analyze its operational role step-by-step:

### Step 1 — **Loading Data from Memory**
The accumulator receives data from either the **data bus** (after a memory read) or directly from an **input device** or another register. This requires a *Load* control signal.

### Step 2 — **Supplying Operand to ALU**
One input of the ALU is **permanently connected** to the accumulator's output. This means that whenever an arithmetic or logical operation is performed, the accumulator provides one of the operands automatically.

### Step 3 — **Capturing the ALU Result**
The output of the ALU is fed back to the accumulator's input via a multiplexer. After the operation, the result is **clocked into** the accumulator, replacing its previous content.

### Step 4 — **Storing the Result**
The accumulator's contents can be routed to the data bus (via a tri-state buffer) and written to memory or to an output port.

## 2.2 Why the Accumulator is Special — The "Why" Behind the Design

> **The "Why":** Why do we need a *special* register when we have many general-purpose registers?
>
> **The "How":** The accumulator simplifies the instruction encoding. Instead of specifying two register addresses (e.g., `ADD R1, R2`), a single-address format (`ADD addr`) suffices because the accumulator is **implicitly known** to both the hardware and the instruction decoder. This drastically reduces instruction length and decoder complexity — critical in early machines with limited word sizes.

## 2.3 Fundamental Operations of an Accumulator

A typical accumulator supports these micro-operations:

| Symbol | Micro-Operation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `LD ACC` | `ACC ← Data` | Load the accumulator from data bus |
| `ST ACC` | `Data ← ACC` | Store accumulator contents to data bus |
| `ADD` | `ACC ← ACC + Operand` | Add operand to accumulator |
| `SUB` | `ACC ← ACC − Operand` | Subtract operand from accumulator |
| `AND/OR/XOR` | `ACC ← ACC OP Operand` | Bitwise logical operation |
| `CMA` | `ACC ← ACC'` | Complement accumulator |
| `SHL/SHR` | `ACC ← shift(ACC)` | Shift accumulator left/right |
| `CLR` | `ACC ← 0` | Clear accumulator to zero |
| `INC` | `ACC ← ACC + 1` | Increment accumulator |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the key quantitative relationships governing accumulator design. These formulas are routinely tested in KTU university examinations.

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Number of distinct states | $N_{states} = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = number of bits in accumulator |
| Unsigned range | $0 \;\text{to}\; 2^{n} - 1$ | Decimal integer range |
| Signed (2's complement) range | $-2^{n-1} \;\text{to}\; 2^{n-1} - 1$ | Decimal integer range |
| 8-bit unsigned max value | $2^{8} - 1 = 255$ | Decimal |
| 8-bit signed range | $-128 \;\text{to}\; +127$ | Decimal |
| Storage capacity (bits) | $C = n \;\text{bits}$ | Total flip-flop count |
| Flip-flops required | $n$ | One D-FF per bit |
| Multiplexer input lines | $2 \;\text{sources} \times n\;\text{bits}$ | For source selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When the question says *"Design an 8-bit accumulator"*, the student is **expected to draw** a block diagram with **8 D flip-flops**, a **2-to-1 input multiplexer**, a **tri-state output buffer**, and label the control signals. This is a **frequently repeated university exam question**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Accumulator-based design principles are not just historical. They appear in modern contexts:
- **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs)** often use accumulator registers for **MAC (Multiply-Accumulate)** operations central to filters and FFTs.
- **SIMD architectures** use wide accumulators (e.g., 256-bit or 512-bit) to prevent overflow during parallel additions.
- **AI/ML accelerators** (TPUs, NPUs) employ massive accumulator arrays for **matrix multiplication**.
- **Embedded microcontrollers** (8051 family) still retain an accumulator (`A` register) for instruction efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Storage Capacity of an n-bit Accumulator

### Step 1 — Start with a single flip-flop
A single D flip-flop can store one bit, which has exactly **2 distinct states**:
$$N_{1} = 2^{1} = 2 \;\text{states}$$

### Step 2 — Extend to n flip-flops
When $n$ flip-flops are connected in parallel, each bit is independent. By the multiplication rule of counting:
$$N_{n} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{n \;\text{times}} = 2^{n}$$

Therefore, the general formula for the **number of distinct values** an $n$-bit accumulator can hold is:
$$N_{states} = 2^{n}$$

### Step 3 — Compute for the standard 8-bit case (KTU-favored)
Substituting $n = 8$:
$$N_{states} = 2^{8} = 256 \;\text{distinct values}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to numeric range

**Case A — Unsigned interpretation:**
$$0 \leq ACC \leq 2^{8} - 1 = 255$$

**Case B — Signed (2's complement) interpretation:**
$$-2^{8-1} \leq ACC \leq 2^{8-1} - 1$$
$$-128 \leq ACC \leq +127$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic:** The lower bound of the signed range is $-2^{n-1}$ because the most-significant bit (MSB) carries a weight of $-2^{n-1}$ in 2's complement representation, not $+2^{n-1}$ as in unsigned.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hardware Resource Count for an 8-bit Accumulator

### Step 1 — Count the flip-flops
For an 8-bit accumulator:
$$N_{FF} = 8 \;\text{D flip-flops}$$

### Step 2 — Count the multiplexer resources
The input multiplexer must select between **2 sources** (Data Bus and ALU Output) for each of the **8 bits**:
$$N_{MUX} = 8 \times (2\text{-to-1 multiplexer})$$

### Step 3 — Count the output buffer resources
A single **8-bit tri-state buffer** is required to drive the data bus:
$$N_{BUF} = 1 \times 8\text{-bit tri-state buffer}$$

### Step 4 — Identify the control signals
- `LD_ACC` (Load Accumulator) — Enables writing into the flip-flops.
- `OE_ACC` (Output Enable) — Enables the tri-state buffer to drive the bus.
- `CLK` (Clock) — Synchronizes the flip-flop operation.
- `SEL` (Source Select) — Chooses ALU output or Data Bus as the input source.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sources of Accumulator Input

| Source | Selected When | Operation Performed | KTU Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus** (`SEL = 0`) | Loading from memory or input port | `ACC ← Memory[addr]` | LDA, IN instructions |
| **ALU Output** (`SEL = 1`) | After arithmetic/logic operation | `ACC ← ACC op Operand` | ADD, SUB, AND instructions |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):** Calculate the total number of flip-flops required to build a **16-bit accumulator** that can store both the operand and the result of an addition without overflow, given that the result may need to be held in 2's complement form.

### Step 1 — Identify the bit-width
We need $n = 16$ flip-flops.

### Step 2 — Apply the storage formula
$$N_{states} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \;\text{distinct values}$$

### Step 3 — Signed range
$$-32{,}768 \leq ACC \leq +32{,}767$$

### Step 4 — Hardware summary
- Flip-flops: $n = 16$
- 2-to-1 MUX: $16$ lines, each 2-to-1
- Tri-state buffer: $1 \times 16$-bit
- Total storage capacity: **64 KB equivalent** in terms of unique word patterns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic:** A 16-bit accumulator can hold any one of 65,536 different bit patterns, which is mathematically equivalent to the addressable range of a 16-bit memory system. This is why CPU word size and address bus width were historically tied together in accumulator-based architectures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Internal Structure of an 8-bit Accumulator

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **internal block-level functional architecture** of an 8-bit accumulator, showing the data flow, control signals, and connection to the surrounding CPU subsystem (ALU, Data Bus, Control Unit).

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "EIGHT BIT ACCUMULATOR UNIT"
        muxUnit["2 TO 1 INPUT MULTIPLEXER"]
        regUnit["EIGHT D FLIP FLOPS REGISTER BANK"]
        triBuf["EIGHT BIT TRI STATE OUTPUT BUFFER"]
    end

    dBus["CPU DATA BUS 8 LINES"]
    aluOut["ALU RESULT OUTPUT 8 LINES"]
    ctrlUnit["CONTROL UNIT"]

    selSig["SEL SIGNAL"]
    ldSig["LD ACC SIGNAL"]
    oeSig["OE ACC SIGNAL"]
    clkSig["CLOCK"]

    dBus --> muxUnit
    aluOut --> muxUnit
    muxUnit --> regUnit
    regUnit --> triBuf
    triBuf --> dBus

    ctrlUnit --> selSig
    ctrlUnit --> ldSig
    ctrlUnit --> oeSig
    ctrlUnit --> clkSig

    selSig --> muxUnit
    ldSig --> regUnit
    oeSig --> triBuf
    clkSig --> regUnit

    aluOut -.ALU FEEDBACK.-> regUnit
    triBuf -.DRIVES BUS WHEN OE 1.-> dBus
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide (for KTU exam):**
> - The **2-to-1 Multiplexer** selects between the *Data Bus input* and the *ALU output* as the source feeding the flip-flops.
> - The **8 D Flip-Flop Bank** holds the actual stored value, one flip-flop per bit.
> - The **Tri-State Output Buffer** allows the accumulator to drive the data bus when enabled and remain in high-impedance otherwise.
> - The **Control Unit** generates the `SEL`, `LD_ACC`, and `OE_ACC` signals to orchestrate the data flow.

## 4.2 System-Level Context: Accumulator in the CPU Data Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    mem["MAIN MEMORY"]
    pc["PROGRAM COUNTER"]
    ir["INSTRUCTION REGISTER"]
    cu["CONTROL UNIT"]
    acc["ACCUMULATOR ACC"]
    alu["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT ALU"]
    dr["DATA REGISTER DR"]
    addrReg["ADDRESS REGISTER AR"]

    pc --> addrReg
    addrReg --> mem
    mem --> ir
    mem --> dr
    ir --> cu
    cu -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> acc
    cu -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> alu
    cu -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> dr
    dr --> alu
    acc --> alu
    alu --> acc
    alu --> dr
    acc --> dr
    dr --> mem
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Diagrams:** The accumulator is shown as the **central pivot** of the CPU data path. It supplies one operand to the ALU, captures the ALU's result, and exchanges data with memory through the data register. This is the classic single-accumulator (von Neumann) data path.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an accumulator. List any four micro-operations performed by it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An accumulator is a special-purpose register inside the CPU that is implicitly used as one of the operands (and the destination) for arithmetic and logic operations. It is the default working register in single-address instruction format machines.
>
> **Four Micro-operations (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. `LD ACC` — Load the accumulator from the data bus.
> 2. `ADD` — Add an operand to the accumulator (`ACC ← ACC + Operand`).
> 3. `ST ACC` — Store the accumulator contents into memory.
> 4. `CMA` — Complement the contents of the accumulator.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a general-purpose register and an accumulator register.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | General-Purpose Register | Accumulator Register |
|---|---|---|
| Implied in instructions | No — must be explicitly named | Yes — implicit operand |
| Number | Multiple (R0, R1, … Rn) | Typically one per CPU |
| ALU connection | Selected via multiplexer | **Direct hardwired** to one ALU input |
| Role | Temporary storage | Default operand + result holder |
| Instruction encoding | Needs extra bits to specify | Saves instruction bits |

> The accumulator's **hardwired ALU connection** and **implicit addressing** make it faster and reduce instruction length.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the design of an **8-bit accumulator**. Clearly label all control signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the sequence of operations performed by the accumulator during the execution of the instruction `ADD M` (Add memory contents to accumulator). **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and Design (7 marks)**

The 8-bit accumulator consists of three sub-blocks:

1. **Input Multiplexer (8 × 2-to-1 MUX)** — 2 marks
   - Selects between the **Data Bus** (when `SEL = 0`) and the **ALU Output** (when `SEL = 1`).
2. **Register Bank (8 D Flip-Flops)** — 2 marks
   - Stores 8 bits of data; loaded on the **rising edge of `CLK`** when `LD_ACC = 1`.
3. **Tri-State Output Buffer (8-bit)** — 1 mark
   - Drives the data bus when `OE_ACC = 1`; otherwise remains in high-impedance.

**Control Signals (2 marks):**

| Signal | Function |
|---|---|
| `SEL` | Selects input source (0 = Data Bus, 1 = ALU Output) |
| `LD_ACC` | Load enable for the flip-flop bank |
| `OE_ACC` | Output enable for the tri-state buffer |
| `CLK` | Synchronization clock for the register |

---

**Part (b) — Execution of `ADD M` (7 marks)**

| Step | Cycle | Micro-Operation | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fetch — T1 | `AR ← PC` | Address of instruction sent to memory |
| 2 | Fetch — T2 | `IR ← Memory[AR], PC ← PC + 1` | Instruction fetched |
| 3 | Fetch — T3 | `Decode: IR` | Control unit decodes `ADD M` |
| 4 | Execute — T4 | `DR ← Memory[AR]` | Operand fetched from address M |
| 5 | Execute — T5 | `ACC ← ACC + DR` | ALU adds DR to ACC, result latched into ACC |
| 6 | End | Next fetch | PC points to next instruction |

> **The accumulator is automatically the destination** because `LD_ACC = 1` is asserted by the control unit during T5.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the role of the accumulator in an accumulator-based CPU with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the data path of a basic accumulator-based CPU and explain how the accumulator interfaces with the ALU and memory. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Role of the Accumulator (7 marks)**

The accumulator is the **central computational register** in a single-address CPU. It:
1. Holds **one operand** for every arithmetic/logic operation.
2. **Receives the result** of every such operation.
3. Acts as the **bridge** between the ALU and the rest of the system.

**Example:** To compute `Z = X + Y`, a two-instruction sequence is used:
- `LDA X` → `ACC ← Memory[X]` (Accumulator loads X)
- `ADD Y` → `ACC ← ACC + Memory[Y]` (Accumulator becomes X + Y)
- `STA Z` → `Memory[Z] ← ACC` (Result stored)

> Only **one explicit address** is needed per instruction because the accumulator is implicit.

---

**Part (b) — Data Path Design (7 marks)**

The data path consists of:
- **Accumulator (ACC)** — 8-bit register, one ALU input hardwired to it.
- **ALU** — Performs ADD, SUB, AND, OR, etc. between ACC and a second operand.
- **Data Register (DR)** — Temporarily holds the memory operand.
- **Memory** — Connected via address and data buses.
- **Control Unit** — Generates timing and control signals.

**Interface Description (2 marks):**
- `ACC → ALU` is a **direct 8-bit line** (no multiplexer needed).
- `ALU → ACC` goes through a **2-to-1 MUX** (selecting between ALU result and Data Bus input).
- `ACC → Data Bus` is gated by a **tri-state buffer** controlled by `OE_ACC`.

**Timing Summary (1 mark):** A typical fetch-execute cycle completes in **3 + N clock periods**, where N depends on addressing mode.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the tri-state buffer** in the block diagram — costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Not labeling all control signals** (`SEL`, `LD_ACC`, `OE_ACC`, `CLK`) — costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing accumulator with general-purpose register** in definitions — partial credit only.
> 4. **Skipping the MUX** that selects between ALU output and Data Bus as input — a frequently missed element.
> 5. **In execution cycles**, students often miss asserting `PC ← PC + 1` during fetch — loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Calculation errors** in 2's complement range — verify by plugging $n = 8$ into $-2^{n-1}$ to $+2^{n-1} - 1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Design of Accumulator (Basic Concept Only)**

- [x] An **accumulator** is a *single, special-purpose CPU register* that acts as the **implicit operand and result holder** for most ALU operations.
- [x] It is a cornerstone of the **single-address instruction format** and is the defining feature of classical **accumulator-based CPUs** (e.g., 8085).
- [x] The accumulator has **three internal sub-blocks**: **Input MUX**, **Register Bank** (D flip-flops), and **Tri-state Output Buffer**.
- [x] **Control signals**: `SEL` (source select), `LD_ACC` (load enable), `OE_ACC` (output enable), `CLK` (clock).
- [x] The accumulator's output is **directly hardwired to one ALU input** — this is its key architectural advantage.
- [x] **Storage formula**: $N_{states} = 2^{n}$. For **8-bit**: **256** states, range **0–255** (unsigned) or **−128 to +127** (signed).
- [x] **Hardware count for n-bit accumulator**: $n$ flip-flops + $n$ 2-to-1 MUX lines + one $n$-bit tri-state buffer.
- [x] The accumulator **cannot drive the data bus** when `OE_ACC = 0` (high-impedance state) — this prevents bus contention.
- [x] Typical micro-operations: `LD`, `ST`, `ADD`, `SUB`, `CMA`, `CLR`, `INC`, `SHL`, `SHR`.
- [x] Execution of `ADD M` is a **2-step memory access**: first fetch the instruction, then fetch the operand, then perform ALU addition and write back to ACC.
- [x] Modern relevance: **MAC units** in DSPs, **wide accumulators** in SIMD/GPU/AI accelerators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardwired Control - Microprogrammed Control - Microinstructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hardwired Control - Microprogrammed Control - Microinstructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hardwired Control Unit (HCU)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Hardwired Control Unit** is a control unit implementation in which the binary information required for the proper operation of the processor control logic is permanently stored in the form of combinational and sequential logic circuits (gates, decoders, flip-flops, and ROM-based look-up tables). The control logic is physically *wired* using Boolean expressions to generate control signals as a direct function of the current instruction, machine state, and timing inputs (clock and T-state sequence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The control unit is the *operational brain* of the CPU. It dictates when each register loads data, when the ALU performs an operation, and when memory is read/written. In hardwired control, this decision-making is done entirely by physical hardware gates — no stored micro-program exists.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Traffic Signal Controller (Hardwired):**
Imagine a classic traffic light at a fixed intersection. The sequence RED → GREEN → YELLOW → RED is **hard-wired** into an electronic timer circuit. The transition rules (e.g., "If timer = 30s AND no manual override THEN show GREEN") are physically implemented with gates and counters. If you want to change the sequence, you must physically rewire the circuit. Similarly, in a Hardwired Control Unit, every micro-operation's enabling condition is a Boolean equation of the inputs — the logic is *baked into the silicon*.

---

### 1.2 Microprogrammed Control Unit (MCU)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Microprogrammed Control Unit** is a control unit implementation in which the control signals for instruction execution are not generated by hardwired logic but are instead **stored as binary words (microinstructions)** in a dedicated memory called the **Control Memory (CM)**. Each machine instruction is interpreted (interpreted architecture) by a *microprogram* — a sequence of microinstructions that produces the required control word for every clock cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Instead of designing complex Boolean logic, the designer "writes a program" (microprogram) that lives in ROM. The control unit becomes a simple "engine" that fetches and executes this microprogram.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Vending Machine Controller (Microprogrammed):**
A modern vending machine has a list of drinks stored as a lookup table in its memory. When you press "B1" (Coca-Cola), the controller does not use complex wiring; instead, it executes a small embedded program: "Step 1: Drop cup. Step 2: Pour syrup. Step 3: Pour soda. Step 4: Dispense cup." This stored recipe is the *microprogram*. The CPU's machine instruction is "B1" and the control memory contains the recipe. If you need to add a new drink, you just update the ROM — no rewiring required.

---

### 1.3 Microinstruction

**Formal Definition:**
A **Microinstruction** is a binary-encoded control word, stored in one location of the Control Memory, which specifies the set of micro-operations to be executed during a single clock (micro-cycle) and, in some formats, also encodes the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched. A collection of microinstructions that implement one machine instruction is called a **Microprogram**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro-operation vs Microinstruction:**
> - *Micro-operation*: A single elementary register-transfer action (e.g., `R2 ← R1 + R3`).
> - *Microinstruction*: The encoded control word that activates one or more micro-operations simultaneously.

**Intuitive Analogy — A Musical Score:**
Think of a microinstruction as **one beat of a song**. The score (microprogram) tells the orchestra (CPU) which instruments play on which beat. Each measure (machine instruction) is composed of many beats (microinstructions), and the conductor (Control Unit) simply reads the score bar-by-bar, beat-by-beat.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Word Width (n)** | 20 – 80 bits (typical) | Number of parallel control signals per microinstruction |
| **Control Memory Size** | 2^k words × n bits | k = address bits, n = word size |
| **Micro-cycle time** | ≈ 1 system clock period | Time to fetch and execute one microinstruction |
| **Sequencer cycle** | Fetch → Decode → Execute → Next Address | Standard 4-phase sequence |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hardwired Control — Design Methodology

The hardwired control unit is a sequential finite-state machine (FSM). The design follows these structured steps:

**Step 1 — Enumerate Micro-Operations**
For every machine instruction, list the register-transfer micro-operations and the timing signals (T-states) in which they must be active.

**Step 2 — Derive Boolean Equations**
For each control signal `C_i`, express it as a Boolean function of:
- The opcode field of the IR (Instruction Register).
- The current step / timing decoder output (T1, T2, T3…).
- Status flags (Z, N, C, V from the Condition Code Register).
- External inputs (Interrupt, Reset).

$$C_i = f(\text{Opcode}, T_j, \text{Flags}, \text{Inputs})$$

**Step 3 — Realize the Circuit**
The Boolean expressions are then implemented using a *PLA* (Programmable Logic Array), a *ROM*, or discrete gates + flip-flops.

**Step 4 — Generate Timing**
A ring counter or 4-bit binary counter driven by the master clock produces the timing signals T0, T1, T2, T3, T4 that partition each instruction cycle.

**Block-Level Operation:**

$$ \text{Output} = \text{Decoder}(\text{IR Opcode}) \cdot \text{Timing}(T_j) \cdot \text{Condition}(\text{Flags}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a PLA?** A PLA (Programmable AND/OR matrix) is the most practical way to implement HCU. It replaces hundreds of individual gates with a single IC. The product terms are the AND of the inputs, and the sum terms are the OR of the products.

---

### 2.2 Microprogrammed Control — Architecture

The MCU is essentially a "miniature computer" inside the CPU that reads control words from a ROM.

**Key Components:**

1. **Control Memory (CM):** A ROM (or Writable Control Store in advanced designs) holding the microprogram. Each location stores one microinstruction (the *control word*).
2. **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched. Equivalent to the PC of the micro-engine.
3. **Control Data Register (CDR):** Holds the microinstruction currently being executed. Equivalent to the IR of the micro-engine.
4. **Sequencer / Next Address Generator (NAG):** A combinational circuit that determines the next value of the CAR based on the current microinstruction, the opcode, and the condition flags.
5. **External Inputs to CAR:** Instruction Opcode (for dispatch), Branch addresses, Loop counters.

**Operational Cycle:**
1. **CAR → CM:** The address in the CAR is sent to the Control Memory.
2. **CM → CDR:** The microinstruction at that address is loaded into the CDR.
3. **CDR → Control Signals:** The control field of the microinstruction is decoded to activate the corresponding control lines.
4. **Sequencer → CAR:** The next-address logic computes the value for CAR (either CAR+1, or a branch target, or a dispatch from opcode).
5. **Repeat.**

---

### 2.3 Microinstruction Formats

There are two principal formats. The choice of format is a *trade-off between parallelism and word width*.

#### (A) Horizontal Microinstruction
- Each bit of the control word directly controls one specific control signal in the CPU.
- **High parallelism:** many micro-operations can be specified simultaneously.
- **Wide control word:** if the CPU has N control signals, the word is N bits long.
- **Little or no decoding** is required.

#### (B) Vertical Microinstruction
- The control word is divided into short *fields*. Each field is decoded to activate a specific group of signals.
- **Limited parallelism:** only one signal per field can be active at a time.
- **Narrow control word:** typically 16–32 bits.
- **Heavy decoding** is required.

| Feature | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| **Word Width** | Very Wide (50–100+ bits) | Narrow (16–32 bits) |
| **Encoding** | Direct (1 bit = 1 signal) | Heavily Encoded (Field-decoded) |
| **Parallelism** | Maximum | Restricted (1 signal/field) |
| **Decoding** | Minimal | Significant |
| **Sequencing** | Usually explicit in word | Usually implicit (CAR+1) |
| **Microprogram Size** | Smaller (more in 1 instr) | Larger (more instr needed) |
| **Speed of Execution** | Fast (no decode) | Slower (decode delay) |
| **Analogy** | A marching band — every instrument has its own part on the score | A piano roll — one note at a time |

---

### 2.4 Microinstruction Encoding Schemes

1. **Direct Encoding (No Encoding):** Every control signal has a unique bit. Simple but wide.
2. **Field Encoding (n-hot, group-encoded):** Control signals are grouped; each field is decoded into one of 2^k signals.
3. **Indirect Encoding (Pla-based):** A small ROM/PLA holds the truth table mapping encoded fields to control signals.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the most-tested quantitative aspects in KTU examinations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Control Memory Size** | $S_{CM} = 2^{k} \times n$ | k = address bits, n = word width | bits |
| 2 | **Number of Microinstructions** | $N_{\mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} L_i$ | m = no. of machine instructions, L_i = length of microprogram for instruction i | count |
| 3 | **Encoding Compression** | $\text{Gain} = \dfrac{N_{\text{direct bits}} - N_{\text{encoded bits}}}{N_{\text{direct bits}}} \times 100$ | bits before/after encoding | % |
| 4 | **Bits per Field (k-hot encoding)** | $b = \lceil \log_{2}(g) \rceil$ | g = number of signals grouped in the field | bits |
| 5 | **Total Bits for Field Encoding** | $n_{\text{enc}} = \sum_{j=1}^{F} \lceil \log_{2}(g_j) \rceil$ | F = number of fields, g_j = group size | bits |
| 6 | **Address Dispatch (Mapping)** | $A_{\text{first}} = \text{Opcode} \times \text{Stride}$ | Opcode × microprogram length offset | — |
| 7 | **Time to Execute Microprogram** | $T_{\mu} = N_{\mu} \times T_{\text{clock}}$ | T_clock = micro-cycle period | seconds |
| 8 | **Branch Address Generation (relative)** | $A_{\text{next}} = A_{\text{curr}} + 1 + \text{Offset}$ | Offset = signed displacement in instr | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid using the vertical pipe `|` in tables.** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation in LaTeX tables, e.g., $\vert x \vert$ instead of `|x|`.

---

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Hardwired Control** is used in high-performance RISC processors (early MIPS, ARM7) and ASICs where speed is critical.
- **Microprogrammed Control** dominated in CISC processors (Intel 8086, VAX, IBM 360) because it allowed complex instruction sets to be implemented reliably and updatable even after silicon fabrication.
- The technique of *microprogramming* was first articulated by **Maurice Wilkes** in 1951 and is the conceptual ancestor of firmware, BIOS, and microcode updates (e.g., the Intel microcode patches for Spectre/Meltdown mitigations).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Control Memory Sizing for a Given Microprogram Set

**Problem Setup:**
A processor has 32 machine instructions. The longest microprogram requires 64 microinstructions. Each microinstruction is 32 bits wide. The microprogram ROM must be addressed by a 6-bit address. Determine the total control memory size and the average microprogram length.

**Step 1 — Identify the Address Space**

$$ 2^{k} = 2^{6} = 64 \text{ words} $$

The control memory has 64 addressable locations, each holding one microinstruction.

**Step 2 — Compute Word Width**

$$ n = 32 \text{ bits per word} $$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Storage in Bits**

$$ S_{CM} = 2^{k} \times n = 2^{6} \times 32 = 64 \times 32 = 2048 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to Bytes**

$$ S_{CM} = \dfrac{2048}{8} = 256 \text{ bytes} $$

**Step 5 — Average Microprogram Length**

Assuming 32 instructions, the average length of a microprogram (in microinstructions) is:

$$ \bar{L} = \dfrac{Total\ Microinstructions}{Number\ of\ Instructions} = \dfrac{64 \times 32}{32} = 64\ \text{microinstr/instr} $$

This is the worst-case bound; in practice it is averaged across all instructions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Microinstruction Field Encoding

**Problem Setup:**
A CPU has **48 distinct control signals**. The designer decides to group them into **6 fields** with the following group sizes: g1 = 4, g2 = 8, g3 = 2, g4 = 16, g5 = 4, g6 = 14.

**Step 1 — Compute Direct Encoding Width**

$$ n_{\text{direct}} = 48 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 2 — Compute Encoded Width Per Field**

$$ b_j = \lceil \log_{2}(g_j) \rceil $$

For each field:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \lceil \log_{2}(4) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits} \\
b_2 &= \lceil \log_{2}(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits} \\
b_3 &= \lceil \log_{2}(2) \rceil = 1 \text{ bit} \\
b_4 &= \lceil \log_{2}(16) \rceil = 4 \text{ bits} \\
b_5 &= \lceil \log_{2}(4) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits} \\
b_6 &= \lceil \log_{2}(14) \rceil = 4 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Encoded Widths**

$$ n_{\text{enc}} = \sum_{j=1}^{6} b_j = 2 + 3 + 1 + 4 + 2 + 4 = 16 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 4 — Compute Encoding Compression Gain**

$$ \text{Gain} = \dfrac{n_{\text{direct}} - n_{\text{enc}}}{n_{\text{direct}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{48 - 16}{48} \times 100 = 66.67\% $$

**Interpretation:** Encoding reduced the control word from 48 bits to 16 bits — a 66.67% reduction in width, with the trade-off of requiring a decoder and reduced simultaneous micro-operation capability.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Next-Address Generation Logic

**Problem Setup:**
A microprogrammed control unit uses a 6-bit CAR. The microinstruction format contains:
- 3 bits for the function code (F)
- 3 bits for the branch address (B)
- 1 bit for the branch condition (CD — "Condition Disable/Enable")

**Conditional branch rules:**
- If `CD = 0` (branch disabled): `Next = CAR + 1`
- If `CD = 1` and `Status = 1` (branch taken): `Next = B`
- If `CD = 1` and `Status = 0` (branch not taken): `Next = CAR + 1`

**Step 1 — Express as Boolean Functions**

Let `A_0, A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, A_5` be the current CAR bits. Let `B_0, B_1, B_2` be the branch target bits. Let `S` be the status flag.

**Step 2 — Derive Each Bit of Next Address**

For the three least-significant bits (i = 0, 1, 2):

$$ N_i = A_i \oplus (CD \cdot S) \oplus B_i \cdot CD \cdot S $$

For the three most-significant bits (i = 3, 4, 5), no branch is possible (they are 0 in B):

$$ N_i = A_i \oplus (CD \cdot S) $$

Where the XOR-based addition handles the `CAR + 1` increment, and the `B_i · CD · S` term injects the branch target.

**Step 3 — Special Case: B = 0 and CD = 0**

If `B = 000` and `CD = 0`, then `N = A + 1`, giving a pure sequential increment — the natural CAR increment used in vertical microinstructions.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control

| Attribute | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | Gates, PLA, ROM, Flip-Flops | Control Memory + Sequencer |
| **Flexibility** | Fixed at fabrication | Easily modifiable (change ROM) |
| **Speed** | Faster (no memory fetch) | Slower (CM access adds delay) |
| **Design Complexity** | High for CISC | Manageable even for CISC |
| **Word Width Required** | N/A (logic gates) | 20 – 80 bits typical |
| **Cost / Area** | Higher logic density | More memory, less logic |
| **Diagnostic / Testability** | Difficult (logic buried) | Easy (microprogram is observable) |
| **Use Cases** | RISC, DSP, embedded SoCs | CISC mainframes, early microprocessors |
| **Architect** | Electrical engineer | Firmware / systems programmer |
| **Time to Market** | Long (full re-spin) | Short (just rewrite CM) |
| **Power Consumption** | Lower | Slightly higher (CM access) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hardwired Control Unit (HCU)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IR[Instruction Register - IR] --> OPC[Opcode Decoder 3-to-8]
    TIMING[Timing Generator T0 T1 T2 T3] --> STEP[Step Decoder]
    FLAGS[Condition Flags Z N C V] --> COND[Condition Logic]
    OPC --> AND1[AND Matrix - PLA]
    STEP --> AND1
    COND --> AND1
    AND1 --> OR1[OR Matrix]
    OR1 --> OUT[Control Signals to CPU]
    CLK[Master Clock] --> TIMING
    RST[Reset Signal] --> TIMING
```

**Explanation:** The HCU is a combinational PLA. The opcode is decoded, the timing ring counter produces a step number (T0–T3), and condition flags are OR'd in. Their product terms are summed in the OR-matrix to produce the 20+ control lines that drive the datapath.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Microprogrammed Control Unit (MCU)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CAR[Control Address Register - CAR 6-bit] --> CM[Control Memory CM - ROM 64x32]
    CM --> CDR[Control Data Register - CDR]
    CDR --> DEC[Field Decoder]
    DEC --> CTRL[Control Signals to Datapath]
    CDR --> NAG[Next Address Generator NAG]
    NAG --> MUX[Address Source MUX]
    MUX --> CAR
    IR[Instruction Register] --> MAP[Mapping Logic - Opcode to Micro Address]
    MAP --> MUX
    FLG[Status Flags] --> NAG
    CLK[Clock] --> CAR
    RST[Reset] --> CAR
```

**Explanation:** The MCU is a stored-program engine. The CAR drives the Control Memory, which feeds the CDR. The CDR's control field is decoded to drive the CPU; its address/sequencing field drives the NAG, which determines the next CAR value (either +1, branch, or new dispatch from opcode).

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Microinstruction Execution Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Micro-cycle] --> B[CAR content sent to CM]
    B --> C[CM outputs Microinstruction to CDR]
    C --> D[Decode Control Field]
    D --> E[Activate CPU Control Signals]
    E --> F[Compute Next Address via NAG]
    F --> G[Load Next Address into CAR]
    G --> H{End of Machine Instruction?}
    H -- No --> A
    H -- Yes --> Z[Fetch Next Machine Instruction]
```

**Explanation:** This is the *micro-fetch-decode-execute* loop. It runs at the micro-cycle rate, which is typically equal to (or a small multiple of) the system clock period.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Microprogrammed Next-Address Decision

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FetchInit
    FetchInit --> Decode : CAR = 0 after Reset
    Decode --> Execute1 : T0 starts
    Execute1 --> CheckBranch : T1
    CheckBranch --> BranchTaken : CD = 1 AND Status = 1
    CheckBranch --> Sequential : CD = 0 OR Status = 0
    BranchTaken --> Execute1 : Load CAR with B
    Sequential --> Execute1 : CAR becomes CAR + 1
    Execute1 --> Done : All T-states complete
    Done --> [*]
```

**Explanation:** The sequencer is a small finite-state machine that decides whether the next address is `CAR + 1` (sequential flow) or `B` (branch target) or a new dispatch from the IR opcode (start of a new microprogram).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Hardwired Control and Microprogrammed Control. List any three points of comparison.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Control logic is implemented using fixed combinational and sequential circuits (gates, PLA, ROM). | Control logic is implemented using a microprogram stored in Control Memory (ROM/RAM). |
| 2 | Difficult to design, modify, or debug for complex instruction sets. | Easy to design, modify, and update by rewriting the microprogram. |
| 3 | Faster execution since no microinstruction fetch is required during the instruction cycle. | Slower due to the extra microinstruction fetch from the control memory. |
| 4 | Used in RISC processors (e.g., ARM, MIPS). | Used in CISC processors (e.g., Intel 8086, VAX). |

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark per correct point × 3 points = 3 marks.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a microinstruction? How does it differ from a machine instruction?**

**Model Answer:**

A **microinstruction** is a binary control word stored in the Control Memory of a microprogrammed control unit. It specifies the set of micro-operations (register transfers, ALU actions, memory accesses) to be performed in a single micro-cycle, and may also specify the address of the next microinstruction.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Microinstruction | Machine Instruction |
|---|---|---|
| Level | Low-level (micro-architecture) | High-level (ISA, visible to programmer) |
| Storage | Control Memory (ROM) | Main Memory (RAM) |
| Size | Very short, fixed micro-cycle | Variable, multi-cycle execution |
| Decoded by | Control Unit (hardware) | CPU (hardware + microcode) |
| Visibility | Programmer cannot access | Programmer writes them |

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + 2 differences (1 mark each) = 3 marks.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the organization of a microprogrammed control unit with a neat block diagram. Label the major components: Control Memory (CM), Control Address Register (CAR), Control Data Register (CDR), and the Next Address Generator (NAG). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CPU has 64 control signals. Design a field-encoded microinstruction format. The signals are grouped as follows: ALU group = 16 signals, Shift group = 4 signals, Memory group = 8 signals, Register group = 32 signals, I/O group = 4 signals. Calculate the total control word width and the encoding gain. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The microprogrammed control unit is a stored-program controller consisting of the following major blocks:

1. **Control Memory (CM):** A ROM (or Writable Control Store) that stores the microprogram. Each location holds one microinstruction. Typical size: **2^k words × n bits**.

2. **Control Address Register (CAR):** A 6- to 10-bit register that holds the address of the *next* microinstruction to be read. It functions like a Program Counter for the micro-engine.

3. **Control Data Register (CDR):** A register that holds the microinstruction currently being executed. It buffers the output of the CM so the control signals remain stable throughout the micro-cycle.

4. **Next Address Generator (NAG) / Sequencer:** A combinational block that determines the value to be loaded into the CAR at the end of the current micro-cycle. Inputs include: (i) the address/branch field of the current microinstruction, (ii) the opcode of the IR for initial dispatch, and (iii) external status flags for conditional branches.

5. **Mapping Logic:** Converts the opcode of the machine instruction into the starting address of its microprogram (e.g., for an ADD instruction, jump to address 0x10).

**Operation (micro-cycle):**
- Step 1: CAR content → address bus of CM.
- Step 2: CM[CAR] → CDR.
- Step 3: CDR control field → decode → activate CPU control signals.
- Step 4: NAG computes Next = f(CDR, IR, Flags).
- Step 5: Next → CAR. Repeat.

**Marks Distribution (a):** Block diagram with labels (3 marks) + Explanation of each block (3 marks) + Operation cycle (1 mark) = 7 marks.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Group Sizes:**
- g1 (ALU) = 16
- g2 (Shift) = 4
- g3 (Memory) = 8
- g4 (Register) = 32
- g5 (I/O) = 4

**Step 2 — Compute Bits Per Field:**

$$ b_j = \lceil \log_{2}(g_j) \rceil $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{\text{ALU}} &= \lceil \log_{2}(16) \rceil = 4 \text{ bits} \\
b_{\text{Shift}} &= \lceil \log_{2}(4) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits} \\
b_{\text{Mem}} &= \lceil \log_{2}(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits} \\
b_{\text{Reg}} &= \lceil \log_{2}(32) \rceil = 5 \text{ bits} \\
b_{\text{IO}} &= \lceil \log_{2}(4) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Encoded Width:**

$$ n_{\text{enc}} = \sum_{j=1}^{5} b_j = 4 + 2 + 3 + 5 + 2 = 16 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 4 — Compute Direct Encoding Width (for comparison):**

$$ n_{\text{direct}} = 64 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the Encoding Gain:**

$$ \text{Gain} = \dfrac{n_{\text{direct}} - n_{\text{enc}}}{n_{\text{direct}}} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{Gain} = \dfrac{64 - 16}{64} \times 100 = \dfrac{48}{64} \times 100 = 75\% $$

**Conclusion:** The control word was reduced from 64 bits (direct) to 16 bits (field-encoded), a saving of 75% in storage. Each field is decoded by a 2-to-4, 3-to-8, or 5-to-32 decoder as appropriate to drive the actual control lines.

**Marks Distribution (b):** Group identification (1 mark) + Per-field bit calculation (3 marks) + Total width (1 mark) + Gain calculation (2 marks) = 7 marks.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the design of a Hardwired Control Unit. Discuss the role of the Instruction Register (IR), Timing Generator, Decoder, and Programmable Logic Array (PLA). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of Horizontal and Vertical microinstruction formats. Compare them based on word width, parallelism, decoding requirement, and microprogram size. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The Hardwired Control Unit is a pure-hardware implementation of the control logic. It is essentially a finite-state machine whose outputs (control signals) are Boolean functions of its inputs (IR opcode, timing, flags).

**Major Components:**

1. **Instruction Register (IR):** Holds the current machine instruction. The *opcode* field is fed to the control logic; the *operand* fields are routed to the datapath.

2. **Timing Generator (Ring Counter / 4-bit Counter):** Produces a sequence of timing pulses (T0, T1, T2, T3, ...) that partition each instruction cycle into fixed time slots. The output of the counter is decoded into a "step" signal.

3. **Opcode Decoder (3-to-8 or 4-to-16):** Decodes the n-bit opcode into 2^n "instruction class" lines. Each line represents one type of machine instruction.

4. **Programmable Logic Array (PLA):** The heart of the HCU. It is a two-level AND-OR array that implements the Boolean equations for every control signal. The AND-plane generates the product terms (combinations of opcode, step, and condition flags), and the OR-plane sums the relevant product terms to drive each control line.

5. **Condition Code Flags (Z, N, C, V):** Status outputs of the ALU. They are used to gate control signals for conditional branches and conditional micro-operations.

**Block Diagram (described):**
`IR_opcode → Decoder → AND_plane ← Timing_Steps ← Ring_Counter` and `Flags → AND_plane`; then `AND_plane → OR_plane → Control_Signals_to_CPU`.

**Boolean Equation Example (for `PC_out` signal in a fetch cycle):**
$$ PC_{out} = T_0 \cdot \text{ANY\_INSTRUCTION} $$

**Marks Distribution (a):** Block diagram (3 marks) + Role of each component (3 marks) + Example Boolean equation (1 mark) = 7 marks.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Horizontal Microinstruction Format:**
- Each bit of the control word directly drives one specific control signal in the CPU.
- Maximum parallelism: any combination of micro-operations can be specified in a single microinstruction.
- Word width is very large (typically 50–100+ bits for a moderately complex CPU).
- Minimal or no decoding is required.
- Microprograms are shorter (more work per microinstruction).

**Vertical Microinstruction Format:**
- The control word is divided into short fields; each field is decoded by a decoder into a specific control signal.
- Limited parallelism: only one signal per field can be active at a time.
- Word width is small (16–32 bits typical).
- Significant decoding is required, which adds delay.
- Microprograms are longer (each microinstruction does less work).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| **Word Width** | Very Wide (50–100+ bits) | Narrow (16–32 bits) |
| **Encoding** | Direct (1 bit = 1 signal) | Field-encoded (decoder per field) |
| **Parallelism** | Maximum | Restricted |
| **Decoding** | None / Minimal | Heavy |
| **CM Access Time** | Slightly longer (wider word) | Shorter (narrower word) |
| **Microprogram Length** | Shorter | Longer |
| **Speed of Micro-execution** | Fast (no decode) | Slower (decode delay) |
| **Design Effort** | Easier (no encoding logic) | Harder (must design fields) |

**Marks Distribution (b):** Explanation of each format (2+2 = 4 marks) + Comparison table (2 marks) + Conclusion (1 mark) = 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Micro-operation with Microinstruction:** A micro-operation is a *single* register-transfer action; a microinstruction is a *control word* that may activate *several* micro-operations at once. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Using `|` in Markdown Tables:** The vertical pipe inside LaTeX absolute values (`|x|`) breaks the table syntax in markdown, causing rendering failure. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$.
> 3. **Forgetting Decoder Time in Vertical Format:** When comparing speeds, remember to add the decoder propagation delay in vertical microinstruction execution. Horizontal format skips this delay.
> 4. **Mixing up CAR and CDR:** The CAR is the address pointer (like PC), and the CDR is the instruction register of the micro-engine. Students often swap these.
> 5. **Skipping the Boolean Equation in HCU:** For a 7-mark HCU question, you must show at least one Boolean equation or PLA product term. A block diagram alone is worth only 3 marks.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Control Unit:** The hardware that generates the timing and control signals for all CPU operations.
- **Hardwired Control:** Logic-gate / PLA / ROM-based. *Fast* but *inflexible*. Used in RISC and high-speed designs.
