> Forwarding eliminates most RAW hazards but **cannot eliminate the load-use hazard** (a value loaded from memory is not available at the EX stage of the load itself). The load-use hazard **always requires one stall cycle**, even with full forwarding.

### 2.5 Hazard Detection Unit (HDU) — Hardware Logic

The HDU sits between the **ID** and **EX** stages. It examines:
- `ID/EX.RegRt` and `ID/EX.MemRead` (the instruction leaving ID)
- `IF/ID.RegRs` and `IF/ID.RegRt` (the instruction entering ID)

**HDU Output (Stall Control Signals):**

| Signal | Action | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `PCWrite = 0` | Hold PC | IF stage does not fetch new instr. |
| `IF/IDWrite = 0` | Hold IF/ID | ID stage re-reads same instr. |
| `ID/EX.Control = 0000` | Insert NOP | EX stage executes bubble |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Speedup}_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ | Speedup of k-stage pipeline on n instructions | dimensionless |
| $\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} + \sum \text{Stall cycles per instr.}$ | Effective CPI with hazards | cycles/instr. |
| $\text{Stall cycles} = \text{Branch freq.} \times \text{Branch penalty} + \text{LU freq.} \times 1$ | Total stall cycles per instruction | cycles |
| $\text{Pipeline throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Instruction count}}{\text{Total cycles} \times \text{Cycle time}}$ | Instructions per second | IPS |
| $\text{Branch penalty} = (\text{Cycles wasted}) \times \text{Cycle time}$ | Time lost per branch | seconds |
| $\text{Amdahl ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Enhanced fraction}}{\text{Original time}}$ | Limit of optimization | dimensionless |
| $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{\text{CPI}_{\text{no pipeline}} \times t_{\text{non-pipe}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{pipeline}} \times t_{\text{pipeline}}}$ | True pipeline speedup | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using** the pipe `|` symbol inside any table cell. The LaTeX delimiter `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` is used in place of `|` to prevent markdown table parser corruption.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> Hazard detection and forwarding hardware exists in **every modern commercial CPU** (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series, ARM Cortex-A). Modern out-of-order processors use **Tomasulo's algorithm** with a **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** to handle hazards dynamically — the conceptual descendant of the basic forwarding logic taught in KTU Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ideal vs. Actual Pipeline Speedup

**Step 1 — Define ideal speedup of a k-stage pipeline executing n instructions.**

For an unpipelined processor, each of the n instructions takes k cycle-times, $t$, sequentially:

$$
T_{\text{non-pipe}} = n \cdot k \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Define the pipelined execution time for the same n instructions.**

In an ideal k-stage pipeline with no hazards, the first instruction completes after $k$ cycles, and each subsequent instruction completes after 1 additional cycle. So total cycles equal $k + (n - 1)$:

$$
T_{\text{pipe, ideal}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ideal speedup ratio.**

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{T_{\text{non-pipe}}}{T_{\text{pipe, ideal}}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot t}{(k + n - 1) \cdot t}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Speedup}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the limit for large n (deep pipeline assumption).**

As $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{n \cdot \left(1 + \frac{k-1}{n}\right)} = k
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum theoretical speedup of a k-stage pipeline is **k**, achieved only in the absence of any hazards and with $n \gg k$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Effective CPI with Hazards

**Step 1 — Start from the base pipeline CPI.**

For an ideal 5-stage MIPS pipeline:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Add the cost of structural hazards.**

Let $s$ be the fraction of instructions that cause a structural hazard of 1-cycle penalty. Then:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{struct}} = 1 + s \cdot 1
$$

**Step 3 — Add the cost of data hazards (load-use + other stalls).**

Let $d$ be the fraction of instructions that incur data hazard stalls. With forwarding, the load-use penalty is 1 cycle, others are 0:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{data}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{struct}} + d \cdot 1
$$

**Step 4 — Add the cost of control hazards (branches).**

Let $b$ be the branch frequency and $p$ the average branch penalty in cycles:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{data}} + b \cdot p
$$

**Step 5 — Combine the full expression.**

$$
\boxed{\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = 1 + s + d + b \cdot p}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: CPI with Branch Stall

**Given:** A 5-stage MIPS pipeline with the following workload statistics:
- 20% of instructions are branches
- Each branch resolves in the EX stage → **2-cycle branch penalty** if flushed
- 5% of instructions are loads that are immediately followed by a dependent instruction (**1-cycle load-use stall**)
- **No structural hazards** (Harvard-style separate I/D caches)

**Find:** The effective CPI.

**Step 1 — Compute branch contribution.**

$$
\text{Stalls}_{\text{branch}} = b \cdot p = 0.20 \cdot 2 = 0.40
$$

**Step 2 — Compute load-use contribution.**

$$
\text{Stalls}_{\text{load-use}} = d \cdot 1 = 0.05 \cdot 1 = 0.05
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all contributions.**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = 1 + 0.40 + 0.05 = 1.45
$$

**Step 4 — Compute speedup vs. unpipelined version.**

A single-cycle (non-pipelined) MIPS implementation has CPI = 5 (one cycle per stage), so:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{\text{CPI}_{\text{non-pipe}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{pipelined}}} = \frac{5.00}{1.45} \approx 3.45
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Branch Penalty with Predict-Not-Taken

**Step 1 — Define branch outcome distribution.**

Let $p_t$ = probability branch is taken; $p_{nt} = 1 - p_t$ = probability not taken.

**Step 2 — Compute the average branch penalty.**

When we **predict not taken**, the penalty is incurred **only when the branch is actually taken** (we must flush the speculatively-fetched instructions):

$$
\text{Average branch penalty} = p_t \cdot \text{flush\_cycles} + p_{nt} \cdot 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Average penalty} = p_t \cdot k}
$$

where $k$ is the number of pipeline stages between IF and branch resolution.

**Step 3 — Numerical example.**

For 60% taken branches, with 2-cycle resolution latency:

$$
\text{Average penalty} = 0.60 \cdot 2 = 1.2 \text{ cycles per branch instruction}
$$

If branches are 20% of all instructions, the contribution to CPI is:

$$
\text{Stall contribution} = 0.20 \cdot 1.2 = 0.24 \text{ cycles/instruction}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Hazard Resolution Trade-offs

| Resolution Method | Hardware Cost | Performance Gain | Compiler Support | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Forwarding (EX→EX)** | Medium (2 mux + paths) | Eliminates ~80% data hazards | Not needed | ★★★★★ |
| **Load-Use Stall (1 bubble)** | Low (HDU + stall control) | Eliminates load-use hazard | Insert NOP | ★★★★★ |
| **Predict Not Taken** | Very low (flush logic) | ~50% reduction in branch penalty | None | ★★★★ |
| **Delayed Branch** | Zero (compiler only) | Full elimination | **Required** | ★★★★★ |
| **Branch Target Buffer** | High (CAM/associative cache) | Near-zero penalty for hits | None | ★★★ |
| **Resource Duplication** | High (extra HW) | Eliminates structural hazards | None | ★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> The 2024 KTU exam typically asks students to **compare** two of these methods and justify which is better for a given scenario.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Standard 5-Stage MIPS Pipeline with Hazard Detection Unit

```mermaid
graph LR
    IF[IF: Instruction Fetch] --> REG1[IF/ID Register]
    REG1 --> ID[ID: Instruction Decode + Reg Read]
    ID --> REG2[ID/EX Register]
    REG2 --> EX[EX: ALU Execute]
    EX --> REG3[EX/MEM Register]
    REG3 --> MEM[MEM: Data Access]
    MEM --> REG4[MEM/WB Register]
    REG4 --> WB[WB: Write Back]
    WB --> RF[Register File]

    HDU[Hazard Detection Unit] -.-> REG1
    HDU -.-> REG2
    ID --> HDU
    REG2 --> HDU
    FWD[Forwarding Unit] -.-> EX
    REG3 --> FWD
    REG4 --> FWD
    REG2 --> FWD
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Forwarding Path (EX-to-EX Bypass)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ALU1[ALU of Instruction A in EX stage] -->|Result computed| MUX1[Forward MUX A]
    ALU2[ALU of Instruction B in MEM stage] -->|ALU result delayed| MUX2[Forward MUX B]
    MUX1 --> ALU3[ALU of Instruction C in EX stage - current]
    MUX2 --> ALU3
    ALU3 -->|Result| RFDest[Register File Writeback]
    ALU3 -.->|Load-Use detected?| HDU2[Stall Control]
    HDU2 -->|Yes| Bubble[Insert 1 NOP Bubble]
    HDU2 -->|No| Normal[Normal Execution]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Branch Resolution with Delayed Branch

```mermaid
graph LR
    B[Branch Instruction in EX] -->|Resolve outcome| DS[Delay Slot Instruction]
    DS -->|Always executes| NEXT[Next Pipeline Stage]
    B -.->|If taken| TGT[Branch Target PC]
    TGT -.->|Fetch new instr.| IF2[IF Stage]
    B -.->|If not taken| PC4[PC + 4]
    PC4 -.->|Fetch sequential| IF3[IF Stage]
    B -->|Compiler fills delay slot| FILL[Useful / NOP / Instr from before branch]
    FILL --> DS
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Hazard Detection Unit Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Cycle Tick in ID Stage] --> Check1{ID/EX.MemRead == 1?}
    Check1 -->|No| Check2{EX/MEM.RegWrite AND EX/MEM.RegRd == IF/ID.RegRs?}
    Check2 -->|Yes| FWDA[ForwardA = 10 binary: EX/MEM ALU result]
    Check2 -->|No| Check3{MEM/WB.RegWrite AND MEM/WB.RegRd == IF/ID.RegRs?}
    Check3 -->|Yes| FWDB[ForwardA = 01 binary: MEM/WB result]
    Check3 -->|No| NoFwd[ForwardA = 00: Register file value]
    Check1 -->|Yes| LoadUse[Load-Use Hazard Detected]
    LoadUse --> StallAction[Stall: PCWrite=0, IF/IDWrite=0, ID/EX Ctrl=0]
    FWDA --> Exe
    FWDB --> Exe
    NoFwd --> Exe[Proceed to EX Stage Normally]
    StallAction --> Wait[Wait 1 Cycle and Re-decode]
    Wait --> Exe
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of All Hazards

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[Pipeline Hazards] --> SH[Structural Hazard]
    H --> DH[Data Hazard]
    H --> CH[Control Hazard]

    SH --> SHR[Resource Conflict Example: Single Memory]
    SHR --> SHR1[Resolution: Harvard Cache OR Stall]

    DH --> RAW[RAW: Read After Write - True Dep.]
    DH --> WAR[WAR: Write After Read - Anti Dep.]
    DH --> WAW[WAW: Write After Write - Output Dep.]

    RAW --> RAWr[Resolution: Forwarding + Load-Use Stall]
    WAR --> WARr[Resolution: Register Renaming in OoO]
    WAW --> WAWr[Resolution: Register Renaming in OoO]

    CH --> CHR[Branch / Jump Outcome Unknown]
    CHR --> CHR1[Resolution: Prediction + Delay Slot + BTB]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric only (`IF`, `ID`, `EX`, `MEM`, `WB`, `HDU`, `FWD`, `ALU1`, etc.).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
> - All node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown bold/italics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define a pipeline hazard. List the three principal types of hazards with one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A pipeline hazard is any condition in a pipelined processor that prevents the next instruction from executing in its designated clock cycle, thereby disrupting the steady-state flow and increasing the actual CPI above the ideal value of 1.

The three principal types are:

1. **Structural Hazard:** Two pipeline stages compete for the same hardware resource in the same cycle. *Example:* A unified memory being accessed simultaneously by the IF stage (instruction fetch) and the MEM stage (data read/write).

2. **Data Hazard:** An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that has not yet completed. *Example:* A RAW dependency — `ADD R1, R2, R3` followed by `SUB R4, R1, R5`, where SUB needs R1 before it has been written back.

3. **Control Hazard:** The pipeline does not yet know which instruction to fetch next due to a branch or jump. *Example:* A conditional branch instruction whose outcome is determined in the EX stage, but two instructions have already been speculatively fetched after it.

**(Full 3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for the list + 1 for examples.)**

---

**Q2. Explain how forwarding resolves a RAW data hazard. Why does forwarding not eliminate the load-use hazard?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Forwarding (Bypassing)** is a hardware technique that routes the result of an ALU or memory operation from a later pipeline stage **back to the ALU input of an earlier stage** through a multiplexer, so the dependent instruction does not have to wait for the result to be written back to the register file.

For a RAW hazard like `ADD R1, R2, R3` followed by `SUB R4, R1, R5`, the result of ADD is available at the **end of its EX stage** and can be forwarded **directly to the ALU input of the SUB instruction in its own EX stage** — eliminating the 2-cycle bubble that would otherwise be required.

**However, forwarding cannot eliminate the load-use hazard.** The result of a `LW R1, 0(R2)` load instruction is only available at the **end of the MEM stage** (after memory access). The dependent instruction `ADD R3, R1, R4` needs R1 at the **start of its EX stage**, which occurs one cycle earlier. Therefore, one stall cycle (bubble) must be inserted, even with full forwarding. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** With a neat pipeline timing diagram, explain how a RAW data hazard between the following two MIPS instructions is detected and resolved using **forwarding with one stall cycle**.

$$
\texttt{1. LW R1, 0(R2)}
$$
$$
\texttt{2. ADD R3, R1, R4}
$$

Assume a classic 5-stage pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) with no forwarding, and then upgrade to a system with full EX-to-EX and MEM-to-EX forwarding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-stage pipelined processor runs a benchmark where **22% of instructions are branches**, **6% are loads followed immediately by a dependent instruction**, and the **branch outcome is known at the end of the EX stage (2-cycle penalty if no prediction is used)**. Compute the **effective CPI** assuming full forwarding is implemented and the branch is resolved by stalling. If a **predict-not-taken** strategy is used, compute the new CPI given that **65% of branches are actually taken**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Draw the pipeline timing without forwarding.**

```
Cycle:    1   2   3   4   5   6   7
LW  :    IF  ID  EX  MEM WB
ADD :        IF  ID  -   -   EX  MEM WB   ('-' = stall/bubble)
```

The ADD instruction in cycle 3 (EX) needs the value of R1, but the LW instruction does not write R1 until **cycle 5 (WB)**. Therefore, the ID stage of ADD must wait through cycles 3 and 4. **Total stall: 2 cycles without forwarding.** *(1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for explanation)*

**Step 2 — Apply forwarding.**

With forwarding, the LW result is available at the **end of MEM (cycle 4)**, and can be forwarded to the EX stage of the ADD instruction. However, the ADD enters EX in **cycle 3**, and MEM of LW completes in **cycle 4** — still 1 cycle too late.

```
Cycle:    1   2   3   4   5   6
LW  :    IF  ID  EX  MEM WB
ADD :        IF  ID  *   EX  MEM WB   ('*' = 1-cycle stall, bubble)
```

**Step 3 — Detection logic.**

The **Hazard Detection Unit (HDU)** detects this in cycle 3 by checking:
- `ID/EX.MemRead = 1` (the instruction leaving ID is a load) **AND**
- `ID/EX.RegisterRt = IF/ID.RegisterRs` (R1 is a source of the next instruction)

When true, the HDU asserts `PCWrite=0`, `IF/IDWrite=0`, and forces `ID/EX.Control=0` (NOP) for one cycle. *(2 marks for logic)*

**Step 4 — Final result.**

The load-use hazard requires **1 stall cycle** even with full forwarding, because memory access happens in the MEM stage, but the dependent operand is needed in the EX stage. *(1 mark for conclusion + 2 marks for accurate timing diagram and HDU output signal listing)*

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Define parameters.**
- Branch frequency: $b = 0.22$
- Load-use frequency: $d = 0.06$
- Branch penalty (no prediction): $p = 2$ cycles
- Full forwarding: ALU-to-ALU hazard cost = 0
- Only load-use and branch hazards remain.

**Step 2 — Compute CPI without prediction (stall on branch).**

$$
\text{CPI} = 1 + d \cdot 1 + b \cdot p
$$

$$
\text{CPI} = 1 + 0.06 \cdot 1 + 0.22 \cdot 2
$$

$$
\text{CPI} = 1 + 0.06 + 0.44 = 1.50
$$

*(2 marks for correct substitution and final CPI = 1.50)*

**Step 3 — Compute CPI with Predict-Not-Taken.**

With predict-not-taken, penalty is incurred **only when the branch is actually taken** (we must flush the 2 wrongly-fetched instructions):

$$
p_{\text{new}} = p_t \cdot 2 = 0.65 \cdot 2 = 1.30 \text{ cycles per branch}
$$

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{pnt}} = 1 + 0.06 + 0.22 \cdot 1.30
$$

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{pnt}} = 1 + 0.06 + 0.286 = 1.346
$$

*(3 marks for prediction formula and substitution)*

**Step 4 — Improvement.**

$$
\Delta \text{CPI} = 1.50 - 1.346 = 0.154 \text{ cycles/instruction}
$$

Predict-not-taken reduces CPI by ~10.3%. *(2 marks for comparison and final conclusion)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Describe the **branch delay slot** technique. With a suitable code example, show how the compiler schedules instructions to fill the delay slot. What happens if no useful instruction is available? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-stage pipelined processor has the following hazard profile: 30% loads, 10% stores, 15% branches (3-cycle penalty), and 5% jumps (2-cycle penalty). 20% of loads are followed by a dependent instruction. Compute the **CPI without forwarding** and the **CPI with full forwarding**. Assume branches are resolved in EX and use **stall-on-branch**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Define delayed branch.**

In a delayed branch architecture, the instruction in the **delay slot** (the instruction immediately following the branch in program order) **always executes**, regardless of whether the branch is taken or not taken. This shifts the responsibility of branch hazard resolution from hardware to the **compiler**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Code example (MIPS assembly).**

Original code:
```
        BEQ R1, R2, LABEL
        ADD R3, R4, R5      <-- This is the delay slot
        SUB R6, R7, R8
LABEL:  LW R9, 0(R10)
```

After compiler scheduling, the compiler tries to fill the delay slot with a useful instruction from **before** the branch:
```
        ADD R3, R4, R5
        BEQ R1, R2, LABEL      <-- branch
        SUB R6, R7, R8         <-- moved into delay slot
LABEL:  LW R9, 0(R10)
```

Now `ADD R3, R4, R5` executes before the branch, and `SUB R6, R7, R8` executes in the delay slot — its execution is unaffected by the branch outcome. *(3 marks for correct example)*

**Step 3 — What if no useful instruction is available?**

The compiler inserts a **NOP (no-operation)** into the delay slot. This wastes one cycle but preserves correctness. Modern compilers also have a third option: an instruction from the **branch target** (scheduled from below the branch) can be moved into the delay slot, exploiting the fact that it will execute before control flows there. *(2 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Define hazards.**

| Hazard | Frequency | Penalty (no FW) | Penalty (with FW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load-use (1 of 0.20 of loads) | $0.30 \times 0.20 = 0.06$ | 2 cycles | 1 cycle |
| Other load-use of nearby ALU | small fraction, ignored | 2 cycles | 0 cycles |
| Branch | 0.15 | 3 cycles | 3 cycles |
| Jump | 0.05 | 2 cycles | 2 cycles |

**Step 2 — CPI without forwarding.**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{no FW}} = 1 + 0.06 \cdot 2 + 0.15 \cdot 3 + 0.05 \cdot 2
$$

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{no FW}} = 1 + 0.12 + 0.45 + 0.10 = 1.67
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — CPI with full forwarding.**

With full forwarding, the only remaining data hazard is the **load-use** (1 cycle penalty, because memory result is not available in time):

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{FW}} = 1 + 0.06 \cdot 1 + 0.15 \cdot 3 + 0.05 \cdot 2
$$

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{FW}} = 1 + 0.06 + 0.45 + 0.10 = 1.61
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Analysis and conclusion.**

Forwarding reduces CPI from 1.67 to 1.61, a savings of 0.06 cycles per instruction. To reduce CPI further, **branch prediction or delayed branching** must be applied. *(3 marks for analysis, identification of which hazards are unaffected, and final conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the load-use stall:** Many students incorrectly claim that **full forwarding eliminates ALL data hazards**. It does **NOT** — the load-use hazard always requires **one bubble cycle**. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
>
> 2. **Confusing hazard types:** WAR and WAW are **not** hazards in a classic 5-stage in-order MIPS pipeline. They only appear in **out-of-order** or multi-issue processors. Stating otherwise loses marks.
>
> 3. **Wrong branch penalty formula:** The branch penalty is incurred **per branch instruction**, not per program. The contribution to CPI is `b × p`, not just `p`.
>
> 4. **Mishandling the delay slot:** Students often write that the delay slot is skipped if the branch is taken. **It is NOT skipped** — it **always** executes, regardless of branch outcome. This is the most common 3-mark loss in delayed-branch questions.
>
> 5. **Missing the structural hazard resolution:** When asked to resolve a structural hazard, the answer is **resource duplication** (e.g., separate I-cache and D-cache), not just stalling. Both are valid, but duplication is the textbook-correct permanent fix.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam**
>
> ✅ **Three hazard types:** Structural (resource), Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), Control (branch/jump).
>
> ✅ **RAW is the only data hazard in classic 5-stage in-order MIPS pipeline.**
>
> ✅ **Forwarding paths:** EX/MEM → EX ALU input, and MEM/WB → EX ALU input.
>
> ✅ **Load-use hazard always needs 1 stall**, even with full forwarding.
>
> ✅ **HDU detection condition:** `ID/EX.MemRead = 1` AND (`ID/EX.RegRt = IF/ID.RegRs` OR `ID/EX.RegRt = IF/ID.RegRt`).
>
> ✅ **HDU control outputs when stall:** `PCWrite = 0`, `IF/IDWrite = 0`, `ID/EX Control = 0` (NOP).
>
> ✅ **Delayed branch:** Delay slot instruction **always executes**, irrespective of branch outcome.
>
> ✅ **Predict-not-taken penalty formula:** Average penalty = $p_t \times k$ cycles.
>
> ✅ **Effective CPI formula:** $\text{CPI} = 1 + \text{data stalls} + \text{branch freq} \times \text{branch penalty}$.
>
> ✅ **Ideal speedup formula:** $\dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$, limit as $n \to \infty$ equals $k$.
>
> ✅ **Branch resolution location in 5-stage MIPS:** EX stage → 2-cycle penalty if stall-on-branch.
>
> ✅ **Structural hazard fix:** Harvard (separate I/D caches) or insert bubble.
>
> ✅ **Modern CPU extension:** Tomasulo's algorithm with Reorder Buffer handles all three hazard types dynamically in out-of-order processors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Memory (Concepts Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types of Memory (Concepts Only) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Computer Organization and Architecture**, **Memory** refers to the fundamental hardware subsystem responsible for the **storage, retention, and retrieval** of binary data (instructions and operands) required by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) during program execution. The classification of memory into distinct *types* is dictated by three cardinal engineering parameters: **access time**, **storage capacity**, and **cost per bit**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** > Computer memory is a hierarchical collection of storage devices organized to balance the conflicting demands of **speed**, **capacity**, and **cost**, thereby bridging the *processor-memory performance gap* (often called the **Memory Wall**). ## 1.2 Classification of Memory — The Master Taxonomy Computer memory is broadly bifurcated based on **volatility** (data retention upon power loss) and **proximity to the CPU**: | Tier | Broad Category | Sub-Types | Volatility | |:----:|:---------------|:----------|:----------:| | **Tier 1 (Internal)** | Primary Memory | RAM (SRAM, DRAM), ROM (PROM, EPROM, EEPROM), Cache (L1, L2, L3) | Volatile / Non-Volatile | | **Tier 2 (External)** | Secondary Memory | Magnetic Disk, Optical Disk, SSD, Flash Drive | Non-Volatile | | **Tier 3 (Logical)** | Tertiary / Virtual | Virtual Memory, Cloud Storage | Off-line / Distributed | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Desk" Model Imagine a student's study desk: - **Registers & Cache (L1/L2)** → The **notepad** lying open on the desk. Lightning-fast access, but you can only write a few lines. - **Primary Memory / RAM** → The **drawer right next to the desk**. Quick to open, holds all the books currently being read, but empties the moment you switch off the room light (power off). - **Secondary Memory (HDD/SSD)** → The **bookshelf across the room**. Slow to walk to, but holds your entire library *permanently*. - **Virtual Memory / Cloud** → The **public library downtown**. It seems infinite in capacity, but reaching it takes considerable time and effort (page faults, network latency). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The fundamental design principle is **locality of reference** — *temporal* (recently accessed data will likely be accessed again soon) and *spatial* (data near the accessed address will likely be needed). This principle justifies the entire memory hierarchy. ## 1.4 Primary Memory (Main Memory) — Detailed ### 1.4.1 RAM (Random Access Memory) **Random Access** signifies that any memory cell can be accessed directly in **O(1) constant time** given its address — unlike sequential access media (e.g., magnetic tape). - **Static RAM (SRAM):** Built using **6 MOSFET flip-flops per bit**. Retains data as long as power is supplied. Used in **CPU cache**. Speed: **1–10 ns**. - **Dynamic RAM (DRAM):** Stores each bit in a **tiny capacitor + 1 access transistor**. Requires periodic **refresh** (every ~64 ms). Used in **main memory**. Speed: **~50–70 ns**. ### 1.4.2 ROM (Read-Only Memory) Non-volatile memory used to store **firmware** and **boot loaders (BIOS/UEFI)**. Sub-types: - **MROM (Mask ROM):** Hard-wired at fabrication; cannot be altered. - **PROM (Programmable ROM):** Fuse-based, programmable *once* using a ROM burner. - **EPROM (Erasable PROM):** Erased via **UV light** through a quartz window; reprogrammed electrically. - **EEPROM (Electrically EPROM):** Erased *byte-by-byte* using electrical signals. Precursor to flash memory. ## 1.5 Secondary Memory (Auxiliary Storage) | Medium | Principle | Capacity | Access Time | |:-------|:----------|:---------|:------------| | **Hard Disk Drive (HDD)** | Spinning magnetic platters + read/write head | Up to **20 TB** | **5–15 ms** | | **Solid State Drive (SSD)** | NAND Flash memory cells (no moving parts) | Up to **8 TB** | **0.05–0.1 ms** | | **Optical Disk (CD/DVD/Blu-ray)** | Laser reading pits on reflective surface | **700 MB / 4.7 GB / 25 GB** | **80–200 ms** | | **Magnetic Tape** | Sequential polyester tape with magnetic coating | Up to **18 TB** (LTO-9) | **10–100 s** (search time) | ## 1.6 Cache Memory — A Specialization of SRAM Cache is a small, ultra-fast memory placed *between* the CPU and RAM. It exploits **locality of reference**. Architecturally, three mapping strategies exist: **Direct**, **Associative**, and **Set-Associative**. ## 1.7 Associative Memory (Content-Addressable Memory — CAM) A specialized memory where data is retrieved based on **content matching**, not address. Used in: - **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** in virtual memory - **Network routers** (MAC address lookup) - **CPU branch prediction tables** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU:** *Random Access* (RAM) ≠ *Cache*. RAM is random by **address**; Cache is *also* random by address but stores a *copy* of data likely to be used; Associative memory is random by **content**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Memory Hierarchy — Operating Principle The memory hierarchy is built on a **trade-off triangle**: - **Top of pyramid (Registers, Cache):** Ultra-fast, tiny capacity, expensive per bit. - **Bottom of pyramid (Tape, Cloud):** Slow, vast capacity, cheap per bit. - As we descend, **latency increases** but **cost per bit decreases**. The movement of data *upward* (toward CPU) is managed by the **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** and **hardware cache controller**. ## 2.2 SRAM vs DRAM — Engineering Comparison | Parameter | **SRAM** | **DRAM** | |:----------|:--------|:---------| | **Basic cell** | 6 MOSFETs (1 flip-flop) | 1 MOSFET + 1 capacitor | | **Density** | Low (large cell area) | High (compact cell) | | **Refresh** | Not required | Required (~every 64 ms) | | **Power consumption** | Higher (continuous current) | Lower (except during refresh) | | **Speed / Access time** | **Fast (1–10 ns)** | Slower (50–70 ns) | | **Cost per bit** | **High** | **Low** | | **Typical use** | L1/L2/L3 Cache | Main Memory (RAM modules) | | **Volatility** | Volatile | Volatile | ## 2.3 ROM Family — Evolution and Use Cases | ROM Type | Programming | Erasing | Reprogrammable? | Typical Use | |:---------|:------------|:--------|:----------------|:------------| | MROM | At fabrication | N/A | ❌ | Mass-produced firmware | | PROM | Electrically (fuse) | N/A | ❌ (one-time) | Prototype firmware | | EPROM | Electrically | **UV light** (~20 min) | ✅ (limited cycles) | Old BIOS chips | | EEPROM | Electrically | **Electrically** (byte-wise) | ✅ (high cycles) | BIOS, microcontrollers | | **Flash (NAND)** | Electrically | Electrically (block-wise) | ✅ (~10⁵ cycles) | SSD, USB drives, smartphones | ## 2.4 Cache Memory — Mapping Techniques (Conceptual) When the CPU requests a word from main memory, the cache controller checks if a copy exists in cache (**cache hit**) or not (**cache miss**). ### 2.4.1 Direct Mapping Each main memory block maps to **exactly one** cache line. Determined by: $$\text{Cache Line Index} = (\text{Main Memory Block Number}) \bmod (\text{Number of Cache Lines})$$ - **Pros:** Simple, fast hardware. - **Cons:** High miss rate due to *conflict misses* (even when other lines are free). ### 2.4.2 Fully Associative Mapping A main memory block can be placed in **any** cache line. - **Pros:** Lowest conflict misses. - **Cons:** Requires parallel comparison of the *tag* with every cache line — expensive hardware. ### 2.4.3 Set-Associative Mapping (N-way) A *compromise*: cache is divided into **S sets**, each holding **N lines**. A memory block maps to a specific set, but can occupy any line within it. $$\text{Set Index} = (\text{Main Memory Block Number}) \bmod S$$ - **L1 caches:** Typically **4-way or 8-way**. - **L2/L3 caches:** **8-way, 12-way, or 16-way**. ## 2.5 Virtual Memory — Concept Virtual memory creates an **illusion of a large, contiguous address space** for each process, even if physical RAM is smaller. It uses: - **Paging** (fixed-size blocks, typically **4 KB**) - **Segmentation** (variable-size logical units) - **Page Table** maintained by the **MMU** - **TLB** (Translation Lookaside Buffer) — a fast CAM-based cache for page translations. When a required page is **not in RAM**, a **page fault** occurs → the OS retrieves the page from secondary storage (**demand paging**). ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit | Remarks | |:-:|:--------|:-----------------|:-----|:--------| | 1 | **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** | $\text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H)(T_c + T_m)$ | seconds (s) | $H$ = hit ratio, $T_c$ = cache time, $T_m$ = main memory time | | 2 | Simplified AMAT | $\text{AMAT} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m$ | s | Used in KTU derivations | | 3 | Hit Ratio | $H = \dfrac{N_{\text{hits}}}{N_{\text{hits}} + N_{\text{misses}}}$ | dimensionless | 0 ≤ H ≤ 1 | | 4 | Miss Penalty | $T_{\text{penalty}} = T_m - T_c$ | s | Time lost per miss | | 5 | Direct Mapping Index | $i = (B_{\text{mm}}) \bmod (L_c)$ | — | $B_{\text{mm}}$ = MM block, $L_c$ = cache lines | | 6 | Set-Associative Set | $S_i = (B_{\text{mm}}) \bmod S$ | — | $S$ = number of sets | | 7 | Effective Access Time (with TLB) | $\text{EAT} = (1 - P) \cdot (T_{\text{TLB}} + T_m) + P \cdot (2T_m + T_{\text{TLB}})$ | s | $P$ = page fault rate | | 8 | Tag Size (Direct) | $\text{Tag} = (A - i - b)$ bits | bits | $A$ = address bits, $i$ = index bits, $b$ = block offset | | 9 | Power Dissipation (DRAM) | $P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | watts (W) | Refresh dominates | | 10 | Disk Capacity | $C = S \cdot T \cdot P \cdot \text{Sector\_Size}$ | bytes | $S$ = sectors/track, $T$ = tracks, $P$ = platters | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** AMAT and EAT are the *single most asked numerical concepts* in KTU board exams for this module. Always substitute the **miss path** correctly — it includes the cache check *plus* the memory fetch. ## 2.7 Write Policies (Conceptual) - **Write-Through:** Every write to cache is *also* written to main memory immediately. Slower but simpler consistency. - **Write-Back:** Writes are deferred to main memory until the cache line is evicted. Faster, but requires a **dirty bit** per line. ## 2.8 Performance Drivers — Why Hierarchy Works - **Temporal Locality:** Loop variables, instruction pointers. - **Spatial Locality:** Array traversals, sequential instructions. - **Sequential Prefetching:** Modern CPUs fetch 64-byte *cache lines* anticipating spatial need. > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **conceptual understanding** of these memory types — students are expected to *compare and contrast*, not merely *list*. Expect 7-mark comparison questions like "Differentiate SRAM and DRAM" or "Explain cache mapping techniques." --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > *Per the KTU 2024 "Concepts Only" directive, this section focuses on the **derivation of performance equations** rather than circuit-level transistor analysis.* ## 3.1 Derivation of Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup A system has a **CPU ↔ Cache** and **Cache ↔ Main Memory** interface. - Let $T_c$ = cache access time - Let $T_m$ = main memory access time (includes transfer) - Let $H$ = cache **hit ratio** (probability of finding data in cache) - Let $(1 - H)$ = cache **miss ratio** ### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the two possible events:** - Event A: *Cache hit* with probability $H$ — total access time is just $T_c$. - Event B: *Cache miss* with probability $(1 - H)$ — access time is $T_c$ (to detect miss) plus $T_m$ (to fetch from main memory). **Step 2 — Write the time consumed in each event:** $$ T_{\text{hit}} = T_c $$ $$ T_{\text{miss}} = T_c + T_m $$ **Step 3 — Compute the expected (average) time as the probability-weighted sum:** $$ \text{AMAT} = P(\text{hit}) \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + P(\text{miss}) \cdot T_{\text{miss}} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute the expressions:** $$ \text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot (T_c + T_m) $$ **Step 5 — Expand the second term:** $$ \text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m $$ **Step 6 — Factor out $T_c$:** $$ \text{AMAT} = T_c \cdot [H + (1 - H)] + (1 - H) \cdot T_m $$ **Step 7 — Recognize that $H + (1 - H) = 1$:** $$ \text{AMAT} = T_c \cdot 1 + (1 - H) \cdot T_m $$ **Step 8 — Final canonical KTU result:** $$ \boxed{\;\text{AMAT} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m\;} $$ > **Interpretation:** Average access time is *always* ≥ $T_c$, and the excess is governed by the **miss penalty** $(1 - H) \cdot T_m$. ## 3.2 KTU Numerical Worked Example — AMAT **Problem (Board Pattern):** A system has a cache access time of **5 ns**, main memory access time of **50 ns**, and a hit ratio of **0.90**. Find the AMAT and the speedup over a cache-less system. ### Solution **Step 1 — Identify parameters:** - $T_c = 5 \text{ ns}$ - $T_m = 50 \text{ ns}$ - $H = 0.90$, so $(1 - H) = 0.10$ **Step 2 — Apply the AMAT formula:** $$ \text{AMAT} = 5 + (0.10)(50) $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate:** $$ \text{AMAT} = 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 4 — Compute speedup.** Without cache, every access costs $T_m = 50$ ns. $$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{T_m}{\text{AMAT}} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 $$ > **Conclusion:** The cache system is **5× faster** despite a hit ratio of only 90% — illustrating the *amplification effect* of even modest hit rates when $T_m \gg T_c$. ## 3.3 Effective Access Time with TLB (Conceptual Derivation) For virtual memory systems, address translation requires a **TLB lookup**: - $T_{\text{TLB}}$ = TLB access time - $T_m$ = main memory access time - $P$ = page fault rate **Derivation steps:** **Step 1** — TLB hit (probability $1 - P$): One TLB access + One memory access. $$ T_{\text{case 1}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + T_m $$ **Step 2** — TLB miss / page fault (probability $P$): One TLB access + Two memory accesses (one for page table entry, one for actual data). $$ T_{\text{case 2}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + 2T_m $$ **Step 3 — Combine using expectation:** $$ \text{EAT} = (1 - P) \cdot (T_{\text{TLB}} + T_m) + P \cdot (T_{\text{TLB}} + 2T_m) $$ **Step 4 — Final KTU form:** $$ \boxed{\;\text{EAT} = T_{\text{TLB}} + (1 + P) \cdot T_m\;} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Comparison (Board-Ready) | Feature | Register | Cache (SRAM) | Main Memory (DRAM) | SSD | HDD | Tape | |:--------|:--------:|:------------:|:------------------:|:---:|:---:|:----:| | **Access time** | < 1 ns | 1–10 ns | 50–70 ns | 50–100 µs | 5–15 ms | seconds | | **Capacity (typical)** | < 1 KB | 4–32 MB | 4–64 GB | 256 GB–8 TB | 500 GB–20 TB | LTO: 18 TB | | **Volatile?** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | **Cost/bit** | Highest | Very High | Medium | Low | Very Low | Lowest | | **Access type** | Direct | Random | Random | Random (block) | Random (block) | Sequential | | **Moving parts** | None | None | None | None | Yes (motor) | Yes (motor) | | **Latency driver** | CMOS speed | SRAM cells | Capacitor refresh | NAND bus | Seek + rotation | Tape spool | ## 3.5 Cache Tag-Size Derivation (Direct Mapping) **Problem context:** A 32-bit address space, cache of 64 KB organized in 16-byte blocks. **Step 1** — Calculate number of cache lines: $$ L_c = \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ B}} = \frac{65{,}536}{16} = 4096 \text{ lines} $$ **Step 2** — Number of index bits (line selector): $$ i = \log_2(4096) = 12 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3** — Number of block-offset bits (byte within block): $$ b = \log_2(16) = 4 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 4** — Remaining bits become the **tag**: $$ \text{Tag} = 32 - 12 - 4 = 16 \text{ bits} $$ > **General Rule:** $\text{Tag} + \text{Index} + \text{Offset} = \text{Address Width}$ ## 3.6 Capacity Planning for Secondary Storage **Example:** A 4-platter HDD, 16,000 tracks/surface, 1,024 sectors/track, 512 B/sector. **Step 1 — Bytes per track:** $$ B_{\text{track}} = 1024 \times 512 = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 2 — Bytes per surface (one platter side):** $$ B_{\text{surface}} = 16{,}000 \times 524{,}288 = 8.39 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} \approx 8 \text{ GB} $$ **Step 3 — Total capacity (4 platters, typically 8 surfaces):** $$ C_{\text{total}} = 8 \times 8.39 \times 10^9 \approx 67.1 \text{ GB} $$ > [!NOTE] > Such derivation problems are common in KTU Module 4 (memory & I/O). Always express final capacity in **standard SI prefixes** (KB, MB, GB using powers of 2 where context demands). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid ```mermaid graph TD A["REGISTERS
Capacity 128B to 1KB
Access less than 1 ns"] --> B["LEVEL 1 CACHE L1
Capacity 32 to 64 KB
Access 1 to 3 ns"] B --> C["LEVEL 2 CACHE L2
Capacity 256 KB to 1 MB
Access 4 to 10 ns"] C --> D["LEVEL 3 CACHE L3
Capacity 4 to 32 MB
Access 10 to 20 ns"] D --> E["MAIN MEMORY DRAM
Capacity 4 to 64 GB
Access 50 to 70 ns"] E --> F["SOLID STATE DRIVE
Capacity 256 GB to 8 TB
Access 50 to 100 microseconds"] F --> G["HARD DISK DRIVE
Capacity 500 GB to 20 TB
Access 5 to 15 ms"] G --> H["MAGNETIC TAPE CLOUD
Capacity Petabytes
Access seconds to minutes"] style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#ff9f43,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#feca57,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#48dbfb,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#0abde3,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F fill:#10ac84,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#5f27cd,stroke:#000,color:#fff style H fill:#341f97,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > **Reading the pyramid:** As we go **up**, speed rises and capacity falls. As we go **down**, capacity rises and cost-per-bit falls. The CPU operates at the apex; data migrates **upward** on demand. ## 4.2 SRAM Cell vs DRAM Cell — Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph SRAM["SRAM CELL 6 TRANSISTORS"] S1["M1 PULL UP"] --- S2["M2 PULL UP"] S2 --- S3["M3 CROSS COUPLED"] S3 --- S4["M4 CROSS COUPLED"] S4 --- S5["M5 ACCESS LEFT"] S5 --- S6["M6 ACCESS RIGHT"] end subgraph DRAM["DRAM CELL 1T 1C"] D1["WORD LINE"] --- D2["ACCESS TRANSISTOR"] D2 --- D3["STORAGE CAPACITOR"] D3 --- D4["BIT LINE GND"] end style SRAM fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style DRAM fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 ``` > **Insight:** SRAM uses 6 transistors to *statically* hold a bit (no refresh). DRAM uses one capacitor (which *leaks*) and a single transistor to *dynamically* store charge — hence the **refresh** requirement. ## 4.3 Cache Read Operation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P["CPU generates address"] --> Q["Cache Controller receives address"] Q --> R{"TAG MATCH in cache?"} R -- YES HIT --> S["Deliver data to CPU
Time TC"] R -- NO MISS --> T["Fetch block from Main Memory
Time TM"] T --> U["Update cache line
Replacement policy LRU FIFO"] U --> V["Deliver data to CPU
Time TC plus TM"] S --> W["Continue execution"] V --> W["Continue execution"] style P fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S fill:#55efc4,stroke:#000,color:#000 style T fill:#fab1a0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style W fill:#dfe6e9,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Virtual Memory — Address Translation ```mermaid graph LR VA["VIRTUAL ADDRESS
32 bit or 64 bit"] --> TLBCHK{"TLB HIT?"} TLBCHK -- YES --> PTEA["Retrieve PTE from TLB"] TLBCHK -- NO --> PTAB["Lookup Page Table in DRAM"] PTAB --> PTEB["Retrieve PTE from DRAM"] PTEA --> PA["Form PHYSICAL ADDRESS"] PTEB --> PA PA --> PCHK{"PAGE IN RAM?"} PCHK -- YES --> DATAF["Access data in RAM"] PCHK -- NO PF --> DISK["Page Fault
OS loads from SSD or HDD"] DISK --> UPD["Update PTE and TLB"] UPD --> DATAF style VA fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#000,color:#fff style TLBCHK fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#000,color:#000 style PCHK fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#000,color:#000 style DISK fill:#ff7675,stroke:#000,color:#fff style DATAF fill:#55efc4,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > **Flow Narrative:** A virtual address is first checked against the TLB (a fast CAM). On miss, the MMU consults the page table in DRAM. If the page is *not* in DRAM, a **page fault** triggers a disk access — the most expensive event in this hierarchy. ## 4.5 ROM Evolution Timeline ```mermaid graph LR A["MROM Mask ROM 1970s"] --> B["PROM Fuse based 1971"] B --> C["EPROM UV erase 1975"] C --> D["EEPROM Byte erase 1980s"] D --> E["FLASH NAND Block erase 1990s"] E --> F["3D NAND V NAND 2010s"] style A fill:#dfe6e9,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#b2bec3,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#0984e3,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#00b894,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F fill:#6c5ce7,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > **Engineering Trajectory:** Each generation increased **erasability granularity** (chip → block) and **reprogram cycles** (10⁰ → 10⁵). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Q1]** > **Q:** Differentiate between **SRAM** and **DRAM** in terms of construction, speed, and typical application. > > **Model Answer:** > | Parameter | SRAM | DRAM | > |:----------|:-----|:-----| > | Construction | 6 MOSFET flip-flops per bit | 1 MOSFET + 1 capacitor per bit | > | Refresh | Not required | Required (~64 ms cycle) | > | Speed | Faster (1–10 ns) | Slower (50–70 ns) | > | Density | Lower | Higher | > | Cost/bit | Higher | Lower | > | Use | Cache memory (L1/L2/L3) | Main system memory (RAM modules) | > > *(Marks: 1 construction + 1 speed + 1 use = 3 marks)* > **[KTU University Exam – Model Q2]** > **Q:** What is **Cache Memory**? List its three levels. > > **Model Answer:** Cache memory is a small, high-speed SRAM-based memory that stores a *subset* of main memory data, exploiting the **principle of locality**. It acts as a buffer between the CPU and RAM. > - **L1 (Level 1):** Built into the CPU core, 32–64 KB, fastest. > - **L2 (Level 2):** On-die or on-package, 256 KB–1 MB. > - **L3 (Level 3):** Shared across cores, 4–32 MB, slightly slower than L2. > > *(1 mark definition + 2 marks list = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > > [!WARNING] > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. In **comparison questions**, students *only list points* without a proper table — KTU board deducts **1–2 marks** for poor structure. Always use a **two-column table**. > > 2. In **cache mapping questions**, students confuse the **index** with the **tag** — leading to wrong tag-size calculation. Re-derive: $\text{Tag} = \text{Address} - \log_2(\text{Sets}) - \log_2(\text{Block Size})$. > > 3. **Volatility** is a high-weight keyword in the marking scheme. If a question asks about RAM, *always* state "volatile" first; for ROM, state "non-volatile." > > 4. For **virtual memory** questions, students forget to mention the role of the **MMU** and **TLB** — these are *expected* key terms in any 7-mark answer. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **Q:** **(a)** Explain the **memory hierarchy** in a computer system. Discuss the trade-offs involved at each level. **[(7 marks)]** **(b)** Describe the different **types of ROM**. Compare PROM, EPROM, and EEPROM. **[(7 marks)]** #### Model Solution **(a) Memory Hierarchy & Trade-offs** The memory hierarchy is a **tiered organization** of storage devices arranged to minimize cost while maximizing performance. From top (fastest) to bottom (slowest): 1. **Registers** — Inside the CPU, nanosecond access, holds operands for current instruction. 2. **Cache (L1/L2/L3)** — SRAM-based, holds blocks of recently-used data. Hit ratios 80–95%. 3. **Main Memory (RAM)** — DRAM, holds running programs. Volatile. 4. **Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD)** — Non-volatile bulk storage. Slow but cheap. 5. **Tertiary Storage (Tape/Cloud)** — Archival; slowest, cheapest per bit. **Trade-offs at each level:** - *Speed vs Cost:* Higher speed ⇒ higher cost per bit. - *Speed vs Capacity:* Faster memories are smaller in capacity. - *Volatility:* Top tiers are volatile; lower tiers are non-volatile. - *Access Pattern:* Top tiers are random-access; bottom tiers are block or sequential. The CPU *only directly addresses* main memory and registers; lower tiers are managed by the **OS** and **MMU**. *(≈ 7 marks: 2 hierarchy + 2 trade-off + 2 elaboration + 1 diagram-suggestion)* **(b) Types of ROM** ROM (Read-Only Memory) is **non-volatile** memory used to permanently store firmware. | Type | Programming | Erasing | Re-programmable | Erasure Mode | |:-----|:------------|:--------|:----------------|:-------------| | **PROM** | Electrical (fuse burn) | N/A | ❌ No (one-time) | Not erasable | | **EPROM** | Electrical | UV light (~20 min) | ✅ Yes (limited) | Whole chip | | **EEPROM** | Electrical | Electrical | ✅ Yes (high cycles) | Byte-wise | **Key Comparisons:** - *PROM → EPROM:* Introduced *erasability* via UV exposure, allowing reuse of the chip. - *EPROM → EEPROM:* Replaced slow UV erasure with **in-circuit electrical erasure**, byte-by-byte — enabling field updates. - *EEPROM → Flash:* Flash memory evolved from EEPROM with **block-wise erasure**, making it 10×–100× faster to erase and write. > Modern smartphones and SSDs use **NAND Flash**, a derivative of EEPROM with stacked cells (SLC, MLC, TLC, QLC) to maximize density. *(≈ 7 marks: 2 PROM + 2 EPROM + 2 EEPROM + 1 evolution)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** **Q:** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **organization of a cache memory system** and discuss the three **cache mapping techniques**. **[(7 marks)]** **(b)** A system uses a cache with access time of **10 ns** and main memory access time of **100 ns**. The cache hit ratio is **0.85**. Calculate the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**. If the hit ratio is improved to **0.95**, what is the new AMAT and the speedup? **[(7 marks)]** #### Model Solution **(a) Cache Memory Organization & Mapping Techniques** **Organization:** - The cache sits *between* the CPU and main memory. - Each cache **line** holds one **block** (typically 16–64 bytes) of data, accompanied by a **tag** (identifying the originating main memory block) and a **valid/dirty bit**. **Three Cache Mapping Techniques:** 1. **Direct Mapping:** - Each MM block maps to exactly **one** cache line. - Formula: $i = B_{\text{mm}} \bmod L_c$ - *Pros:* Simple hardware (one comparator), fast. - *Cons:* High *conflict* misses; thrashing possible. 2. **Fully Associative Mapping:** - An MM block can occupy **any** cache line. - *Pros:* Lowest miss rate; flexible placement. - *Cons:* Expensive — requires $N$ parallel comparators for $N$ cache lines. 3. **Set-Associative Mapping (N-way):** - A *hybrid* of the above. Cache is divided into **S sets**; each set holds **N lines**. - Formula: $S_i = B_{\text{mm}} \bmod S$ - *Pros:* Compromise — moderate hardware cost, low conflict misses. - *Cons:* Slightly more complex than direct mapping. - *Industry standard:* Modern CPUs use **8-way** or **16-way** set-associative L1/L2 caches. *(7 marks: 2 organization + 2 direct + 1.5 associative + 1.5 set-associative)* **(b) AMAT Calculation — Numerical** **Given:** - $T_c = 10$ ns, $T_m = 100$ ns **Case 1: Hit ratio $H_1 = 0.85$** **Step 1 — Apply AMAT formula:** $$ \text{AMAT}_1 = T_c + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_m $$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$ \text{AMAT}_1 = 10 + (1 - 0.85)(100) = 10 + (0.15)(100) $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate:** $$ \text{AMAT}_1 = 10 + 15 = 25 \text{ ns} $$ **Case 2: Hit ratio $H_2 = 0.95$** **Step 1 — Substitute:** $$ \text{AMAT}_2 = 10 + (1 - 0.95)(100) = 10 + (0.05)(100) $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate:** $$ \text{AMAT}_2 = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 3 — Speedup computation:** $$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{\text{AMAT}_1}{\text{AMAT}_2} = \frac{25}{15} = 1.667 $$ > **Conclusion:** A 10-percentage-point improvement in hit ratio (85% → 95%) yields a **1.67× speedup**. This demonstrates the *highly non-linear* benefit of higher hit ratios. *(7 marks: 1 formula statement + 2 case 1 calc + 2 case 2 calc + 2 speedup & conclusion)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4)** **Memory Classification** - ☐ **Primary (Volatile):** SRAM (cache), DRAM (main). - ☐ **Primary (Non-Volatile):** MROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash. - ☐ **Secondary (Non-Volatile):** HDD (magnetic), SSD (NAND flash), Optical (CD/DVD/Blu-ray), Magnetic Tape. **Key Comparisons to Memorize** - ☐ **SRAM vs DRAM** — 6T vs 1T+1C; refresh; speed; use. - ☐ **Cache vs Main Memory** — SRAM vs DRAM; size; speed. - ☐ **Direct vs Associative vs Set-Associative Mapping** — flexibility vs hardware cost. - ☐ **Volatile vs Non-Volatile** — retain data after power loss? **Formulas (Must-Know for KTU)** - ☐ $\text{AMAT} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m$ - ☐ $H = \frac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits} + \text{Misses}}$ - ☐ $\text{Tag} = \text{Address bits} - \log_2(\text{Sets}) - \log_2(\text{Block size})$ - ☐ Direct map index: $i = B \bmod L$ - ☐ Set index: $S_i = B \bmod S$ **Engineering Concepts** - ☐ **Locality of reference** (temporal + spatial) — *justifies* cache hierarchy. - ☐ **Memory Wall** — performance gap between CPU and DRAM. - ☐ **MMU + TLB** — handle virtual-to-physical address translation. - ☐ **Page Fault** — most expensive event in virtual memory. - ☐ **Write policies** — Write-Through vs Write-Back; importance of *dirty bit*. **Common KTU Question Triggers** - ☐ "Differentiate SRAM and DRAM" (7 marks, expect a 6-row table). - ☐ "Explain cache mapping techniques with diagrams" (14 marks). - ☐ "Calculate AMAT for given $H$, $T_c$, $T_m$" (7 marks numerical). - ☐ "Compare primary and secondary memory" (7 marks). - ☐ "What is virtual memory? Explain the role of TLB." (7 marks). **Examiner's Pet Peeves — Avoid These!** - ❌ Writing "ROM is a read-only memory" *without* specifying **non-volatile**. - ❌ Confusing **Cache** with **Register** — registers are *inside* the CPU; cache is *between* CPU and RAM. - ❌ Saying SRAM needs refresh — it does **NOT**; DRAM does. - ❌ Using cache and RAM interchangeably — they are *different* hierarchy levels. - ❌ Forgetting units (ns, µs, ms) in numerical answers. > **Final Word for KTU 2024:** Memory concepts form the *bridge* between hardware (Module 1–3) and advanced architecture (Module 5: Pipelining, Multiprocessors). A solid grasp of AMAT and mapping techniques is **non-negotiable** for the board exam. ---

topics.title: Virtual Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT403 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Virtual Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Virtual Memory — Module 4 | PCEOT403

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> *Virtual Memory* is a **storage allocation scheme** in which **secondary memory (disk)** can be addressed as though it were a part of the **main memory (RAM)**. It decouples the **logical address space** (what the programmer sees, up to **2^N - 1** addresses for an N-bit logical address) from the **physical address space** (the actual RAM installed in the system), allowing programs larger than physical RAM to execute and enabling **multiprogramming** at a high degree.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Infinite Desk vs. The Real Desk"

Imagine you are a student writing a 1000-page research thesis at home, but your desk only fits **50 pages** comfortably.

| Real-World Object | Computer Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Your small physical desk | **Physical Memory (RAM)** | The workspace where you actively work |
| The bookshelf / cupboard | **Secondary Memory (Hard Disk / SSD)** | Stores the rest of the material |
| A bookmark system | **Page Table** | Tracks which material is on the desk vs. cupboard |
| The act of fetching a chapter from the cupboard | **Page Fault** + **Disk I/O** | Bringing the required pages onto the desk |
| The act of returning a chapter back | **Page Replacement (eviction)** | Swapping out old pages to make room |

When you need a page not on the desk, you **sweep** some old pages back to the cupboard (page replacement) and bring the new one in. To you, the student, it *feels* like you have an infinitely large desk — that **illusion is Virtual Memory**.

### Why Virtual Memory is Needed (KTU 2024 Syllabus Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Pillars of Virtual Memory (Board-Favourite Points):**
> 1. **Program Size > Physical RAM**: Programs larger than installed memory can still execute.
> 2. **Process Isolation**: Each process gets its own logical address space → safety & security.
> 3. **Higher Degree of Multiprogramming**: More processes can reside in memory simultaneously.
> 4. **Easier Programming**: Programmer uses contiguous logical addresses; no need to manually overlay code.
> 5. **Memory Sharing**: Shared libraries/code can be mapped into multiple processes efficiently.

### Core Terminology (Must Memorize)

- **Virtual Address / Logical Address**: Address generated by the CPU during program execution.
- **Physical Address**: Real address in main memory (RAM).
- **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**: Hardware device that translates virtual → physical at runtime.
- **Page**: Fixed-size block of virtual memory (typically **4 KB**, sometimes **8 KB / 16 KB**).
- **Frame**: Fixed-size block of physical memory, same size as a page.
- **Page Table**: Per-process mapping structure from virtual pages to physical frames.
- **Page Fault**: Exception raised when a referenced page is NOT in RAM (valid bit = 0).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 How Virtual Memory Works — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1: Address Generation**
The CPU generates a **logical (virtual) address** of N bits.

**Step 2: Address Decomposition**
The N-bit address is split into:
- **Page Number (p)** — upper bits → index into the page table
- **Page Offset (d)** — lower bits → location within the page/frame

$$\text{Logical Address} = \underbrace{p}_{\text{Page Number}} \quad \vert \quad \underbrace{d}_{\text{Offset within page}}$$

**Step 3: MMU Translation**
The MMU consults the page table using `p` as index, retrieves the corresponding **frame number (f)**.

**Step 4: Physical Address Construction**
The physical address is formed by concatenating `f` and `d`:

$$\text{Physical Address} = \underbrace{f}_{\text{Frame Number}} \quad \vert \quad \underbrace{d}_{\text{Offset}}$$

**Step 5: Page Fault Path**
If the **valid bit (Present bit) = 0**, the MMU traps to the OS, which:
1. Locates the page on disk.
2. Picks a victim frame (using a page-replacement algorithm).
3. If the victim frame is dirty, writes it back to disk.
4. Loads the required page into the freed frame.
5. Updates the page table.
6. Restarts the faulting instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why page size matters**: Larger pages (e.g., 16 KB) reduce the page-table size but increase **internal fragmentation** and the cost of a single page fault. Smaller pages reduce fragmentation but enlarge page tables and increase TLB pressure.

### 2.2 Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)

A TLB is a **small, fast, associative hardware cache** that stores recent page-table entries.

| TLB State | CPU Action | Time Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **TLB Hit** | Use frame number from TLB directly | Fast |
| **TLB Miss** | Access page table in RAM, then update TLB | Slow |

### 2.3 Page Replacement Algorithms (Theoretical Foundation)

1. **FIFO (First-In First-Out)**: Replace the page that has been in memory the longest. Suffers from **Belady's Anomaly** (more frames can cause MORE page faults).
2. **Optimal (OPT / Belady's Algorithm)**: Replace the page that will not be used for the longest time in future. Theoretical upper bound only — not implementable in practice.
3. **LRU (Least Recently Used)**: Replace the page that has not been used for the longest time. Implemented via **counters** or **stacks**.
4. **LFU (Least Frequently Used)**: Replace the page with the lowest reference count.
5. **MFU (Most Frequently Used)**: Replace the most frequently used page (based on the intuition that the page just brought in hasn't been used yet).

### 2.4 Thrashing & Working Set

- **Thrashing**: A condition where the system spends more time **swapping pages in/out** than executing instructions. Caused by over-multiprogramming.
- **Working Set $W(t, \Delta)$**: The set of pages referenced by a process in the time interval $(t - \Delta, t)$. Used to dynamically control the degree of multiprogramming.

### 2.5 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear almost every semester!**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **EAT (no page faults)** | $EAT = T_{TLB} + (2-h) \cdot T_{MEM}$ | $h$ = TLB hit ratio (0 to 1), $T_{TLB}$ = TLB access time, $T_{MEM}$ = Memory access time |
| **EAT (with page faults)** | $EAT = (1-p) \cdot T_{MEM} + p \cdot T_{PF}$ | $p$ = page fault rate (0 to 1), $T_{PF}$ = page fault service time |
| **Combined EAT (TLB + Page Fault)** | $EAT = T_{TLB} \cdot (1 + (1-h)) + (2-h) \cdot T_{MEM} \cdot (1-p) + (2-h) \cdot T_{PF} \cdot p$ | Most realistic model |
| **Page Table Size** | $\text{Entries} = 2^{P}$ where $P$ = page number bits | Bytes = Entries $\times$ Entry-size |
| **Physical Memory Size** | $\text{Frames} = 2^{F}$ where $F$ = frame number bits | Bytes = Frames $\times$ Page-size |
| **Page Fault Rate** | $p = \dfrac{\text{Number of Page Faults}}{\text{Total Memory References}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Hit Ratio** | $h = \dfrac{\text{TLB Hits}}{\text{Total TLB Lookups}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Effective Access Time (FIFO/LRU avg)** | $\displaystyle EAT = \frac{\sum (\text{Page Faults at each stage})}{\text{Total References}}$ | Used in replacement numericals |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are directly applied in **Operating System kernel design (Linux vm subsystem), Embedded RTOS (FreeRTOS memory mapping), Database Buffer Pool management, and Modern CPU MMU design (ARM Cortex, Intel x86 paging)**. Engineers tune page sizes and replacement policies to maximize throughput in cloud servers and mobile devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Access Time (EAT) with TLB

We consider the most general, KTU-favourite model where the system has a **TLB**, **page table in memory**, and a **possibility of page faults**.

#### Case A — EAT with TLB only (No Page Faults)

Let:
- $h$ = TLB hit ratio
- $(1 - h)$ = TLB miss ratio
- $T_{TLB}$ = Time to access TLB
- $T_{MEM}$ = Time to access main memory

**When TLB hits (probability $h$):**
- The CPU finds the frame number in TLB.
- It then performs ONE memory access to fetch the actual data.
- Total time taken = $T_{TLB} + T_{MEM}$

**When TLB misses (probability $(1 - h)$):**
- CPU consults the page table in RAM → 1 memory access.
- CPU fetches the actual data using the frame number → another memory access.
- Total time taken = $T_{TLB} + T_{MEM} + T_{MEM} = T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{MEM}$

**Total EAT** (probability-weighted average):

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_{MEM}) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{MEM}) \\
&= h \cdot T_{TLB} + h \cdot T_{MEM} + T_{TLB} + 2 T_{MEM} - h \cdot T_{TLB} - 2 h \cdot T_{MEM} \\
&= T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot T_{MEM}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard KTU board formula**.

#### Case B — EAT with Page Faults Only (No TLB)

Let $p$ = page fault rate, $T_{PF}$ = page fault service time.

$$
EAT = (1-p) \cdot T_{MEM} + p \cdot T_{PF}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

> A system has $T_{MEM} = 100 \text{ ns}$ and $T_{PF} = 30 \text{ ms}$. If the page fault rate is $p = 0.001$, find EAT. Comment on the result.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - 0.001) \cdot 100 \text{ ns} + 0.001 \cdot (30 \times 10^{6}) \text{ ns} \\
&= 0.999 \cdot 100 + 30{,}000 \\
&= 99.9 + 30{,}000 \\
&= 30{,}099.9 \text{ ns} \approx 30.1 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often forget to **convert $T_{PF}$ from ms to ns** (multiply by $10^6$) — this gives a wildly incorrect answer and full marks are lost. **Always use consistent units**.

#### Case C — Combined EAT (TLB + Page Faults) — Most Realistic

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT = &\ T_{TLB} \cdot (1 + (1-h)) + (2-h) \cdot T_{MEM} \cdot (1-p) \\
      &+ (2-h) \cdot T_{PF} \cdot p
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation:
- $T_{TLB} \cdot (1 + (1-h))$ → TLB is always accessed once; on miss, page table is consulted.
- $(2-h) \cdot T_{MEM} \cdot (1-p)$ → successful memory access time scaled by no-fault probability.
- $(2-h) \cdot T_{PF} \cdot p$ → on a page fault, the entire memory access time is replaced by $T_{PF}$.

### 3.2 Page Replacement Numerical — Worked LRU Example

> Consider the reference string: **7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1, 7, 0, 1** with **3 frames**. Compute total page faults using LRU.

**Solution Trace (Stack-based):**

| Step | Reference | Frame 1 | Frame 2 | Frame 3 | Fault? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 | 7 | - | - | ✅ Fault |
| 2 | 0 | 7 | 0 | - | ✅ Fault |
| 3 | 1 | 7 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Fault |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 7) |
| 5 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Hit |
| 6 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 3 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 1) |
| 7 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 3 | ❌ Hit |
| 8 | 4 | 4 | 0 | 3 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 2) |
| 9 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 3 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 0) |
| 10 | 3 | 4 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Hit |
| 11 | 0 | 4 | 2 | 0 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 3) |
| 12 | 3 | 4 | 2 | 3 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 0) |
| 13 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Hit |
| 14 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 3 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 2) |
| 15 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 3) |
| 16 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 4) |
| 17 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Hit |
| 18 | 7 | 0 | 1 | 7 | ✅ Fault (LRU = 2) |
| 19 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 7 | ❌ Hit |
| 20 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 7 | ❌ Hit |

**Total Page Faults = 12**

### 3.3 Optimal (OPT) Replacement — Same Reference String, 3 Frames

Following the rule: "Replace the page that won't be used for the longest time in future."

| Step | Reference | Frame 1 | Frame 2 | Frame 3 | Fault? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 | 7 | - | - | ✅ |
| 2 | 0 | 7 | 0 | - | ✅ |
| 3 | 1 | 7 | 0 | 1 | ✅ |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✅ (replace 7 — used farthest future) |
| 5–20 | (similar trace) | | | | |

**Total Page Faults = 9** (always ≤ LRU, this is the theoretical minimum).

### 3.4 Belady's Anomaly Demonstration (FIFO)

Reference string: **1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5**

| Frames | Page Faults (FIFO) |
|---|---|
| 3 | 9 |
| 4 | 10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Belady's Anomaly**: With **MORE frames**, FIFO gives **MORE page faults**. This is why LRU is preferred for stack-based algorithms. This is a **guaranteed KTU 2024 question**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Address Translation Flow (Virtual → Physical with TLB)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU Generates Virtual Address"] --> B["Split into Page Number p and Offset d"]
    B --> C{"TLB Lookup for page number p"}
    C -->|TLB Hit| D["Get frame number f directly from TLB"]
    C -->|TLB Miss| E["Access Page Table in RAM"]
    E --> F{"Valid Bit Check in Page Table"}
    F -->|Valid equals 1| G["Retrieve frame number f and update TLB"]
    F -->|Valid equals 0| H["PAGE FAULT - OS Trap"]
    H --> I["Disk I/O - Load required page into a free frame"]
    I --> J["Update Page Table entry and TLB"]
    J --> G
    G --> K["Form Physical Address as f concatenated with d"]
    D --> K
    K --> L["Send Physical Address to Memory Bus"]
    L --> M["Read or Write data at Physical Address"]
```

### 4.2 Virtual Memory Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_BOX["CPU"]
        CORE["Execution Core"]
        TLBU["TLB Hardware Cache"]
    end

    subgraph MMU_BOX["Memory Management Unit MMU"]
        PTREG["Page Table Base Register PTBR"]
        PTWALK["Page Table Walker"]
    end

    subgraph RAM_BOX["Main Memory RAM"]
        PT["Page Table Array"]
        FRAMES["Physical Frames Pages and Data"]
    end

    subgraph DISK_BOX["Secondary Storage Disk"]
        SWAP["Swap Space on Disk"]
    end

    CORE -->|"Virtual Address VA"| TLBU
    TLBU -->|"TLB Hit Frame No"| FRAMES
    TLBU -->|"TLB Miss"| PTWALK
    PTREG --> PTWALK
    PTWALK --> PT
    PT -->|"Page Fault Valid bit equals 0"| SWAP
    SWAP -->|"Page Loaded into Frame"| FRAMES
    FRAMES -->|"Physical Data Returned"| CORE
```

### 4.3 Page Fault Service Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1 - MMU detects Valid bit equals 0 and raises Page Fault Trap"] --> S2["Step 2 - OS saves process state and enters kernel mode"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 - OS checks if reference is valid or illegal"]
    S3 -->|Illegal| S4["Send Segmentation Fault signal to process"]
    S3 -->|Legal| S5["Step 4 - Find free frame using replacement algorithm"]
    S5 --> S6{"Step 5 - Is victim frame dirty"}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7["Schedule disk write of victim frame to swap space"]
    S6 -->|No| S8["Skip write back step"]
    S7 --> S9["Step 6 - Schedule disk read to bring required page into victim frame"]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10["Step 7 - Update Page Table entry with new frame number and Valid bit equals 1"]
    S10 --> S11["Step 8 - Restart the faulting instruction"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Diagram Matters for KTU 2024** — Examiners frequently draw a flowchart and ask students to *"Explain the steps in handling a page fault"*. Memorize these **8 steps** in order. This carries a guaranteed 7-mark question every cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Virtual Memory. List any two advantages of using virtual memory in modern operating systems.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Virtual Memory is a memory management technique that uses secondary storage (hard disk) as an extension of main memory (RAM). It allows execution of processes that may not be completely in main memory by mapping virtual addresses to physical addresses through a page table.
>
> **Advantages (1 mark for any two):**
> 1. Allows programs larger than physical RAM to execute.
> 2. Enables a high degree of multiprogramming — multiple processes can share memory.
> 3. Provides process isolation and memory protection.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? How does it improve the effective access time of a system?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** A TLB is a small, fully-associative hardware cache inside the CPU's Memory Management Unit that stores recent virtual-to-physical page translations. It acts as a high-speed lookup table for the most recently used page table entries.
>
> **EAT Improvement (1 mark):** By caching translations, the TLB eliminates the need to access the page table in RAM for every memory reference. If a TLB hit occurs (probability $h$), the effective access time reduces to $T_{TLB} + T_{MEM}$ instead of $T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{MEM}$ on a miss. Final formula: $EAT = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot T_{MEM}$.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE rules, you are required to answer **ONE full question** out of the two choices below. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of virtual memory using paging. Discuss how logical addresses are translated to physical addresses by the Memory Management Unit (MMU). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Virtual memory using paging divides both logical and physical memory into fixed-size blocks called **pages** (logical) and **frames** (physical), each typically 4 KB.

**Address Translation Steps (5 marks):**

1. The CPU generates a logical address containing a page number `p` and an offset `d`.
2. The MMU uses `p` as an index into the process's **page table**.
3. The page table entry contains the **frame number `f`** and a **valid/dirty bit**.
4. The MMU replaces `p` with `f` and concatenates it with `d` to form the physical address.
5. The CPU uses the physical address to access main memory.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Logical Address :  [ Page Number (p) | Offset (d) ]
                          |
                          v   (Page Table Lookup)
Physical Address:  [ Frame Number (f) | Offset (d) ]
```

If the valid bit is 0, a **page fault** occurs and the OS loads the page from disk.

---

**(b)** A system uses paging with a TLB. TLB access time = **20 ns**, memory access time = **100 ns**, and TLB hit ratio = **80%**. Calculate the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. What happens to EAT if the hit ratio drops to 60%? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_{TLB} = 20$ ns, $T_{MEM} = 100$ ns.

**Case 1 — Hit Ratio $h = 0.80$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot T_{MEM} \\
    &= 20 + (2 - 0.80) \cdot 100 \\
    &= 20 + 1.20 \cdot 100 \\
    &= 20 + 120 = 140 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2 — Hit Ratio $h = 0.60$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 20 + (2 - 0.60) \cdot 100 \\
    &= 20 + 1.40 \cdot 100 \\
    &= 20 + 140 = 160 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):** A drop in TLB hit ratio from 80% to 60% increases EAT by **20 ns (≈14% slower)**. This shows that TLB performance is critical to overall system speed — a key reason modern CPUs have multi-level TLBs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Model Paper - Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the **FIFO** and **LRU** page replacement algorithms. Using a reference string **7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1** with **3 frames**, calculate the number of page faults for each algorithm and compare. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**FIFO (3 marks):** Replaces the page that has been in memory the longest. Implemented using a simple queue.

**LRU (3 marks):** Replaces the page that has not been used for the longest time. Implemented using counters or a stack.

**Reference String:** 7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1 (length = 17)

**FIFO Trace (3 frames) — selected key steps:**

| Step | Ref | F1 | F2 | F3 | Fault? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 | 7 | - | - | ✅ |
| 2 | 0 | 7 | 0 | - | ✅ |
| 3 | 1 | 7 | 0 | 1 | ✅ |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✅ (evict 7) |
| 5 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 1 | ❌ |
| 6 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 1 | ✅ (evict 0) |
| 7 | 0 | 2 | 3 | 0 | ✅ (evict 1) |
| 8 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 0 | ✅ (evict 2) |
| 9 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 0 | ✅ (evict 3) |
| 10 | 3 | 4 | 2 | 3 | ✅ (evict 0) |
| 11 | 0 | 0 | 2 | 3 | ✅ (evict 4) |
| 12 | 3 | 0 | 2 | 3 | ❌ |
| 13 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 3 | ❌ |
| 14 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 3 | ✅ (evict 0) |
| 15 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | ❌ |
| 16 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | ✅ (evict 2) |
| 17 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 3 | ❌ |

**Total FIFO Page Faults = 12**

**LRU Trace (3 frames):** *(refer to Section 3.2 trace)*

**Total LRU Page Faults = 12** (same as FIFO for this particular string — note: in general LRU ≤ FIFO).

**Comparison (1 mark):** For this reference string, both algorithms produce identical results; however, in general LRU outperforms FIFO as it considers **recency** rather than just **arrival order**.

---

**(b)** What is **Thrashing**? Explain the **Working Set Model** and how it can be used to control thrashing in a multiprogrammed system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Thrashing (3 marks):**
Thrashing occurs when a system spends more time **swapping pages in and out of memory** than executing actual instructions. It happens when the total memory demand of all running processes **exceeds the physical memory**. Each process keeps faulting because its working set is too large, the OS swaps pages aggressively, and CPU utilization drops sharply. As more processes are added, thrashing worsens.

**Working Set Model (3 marks):**
The Working Set $W(t, \Delta)$ of a process at time $t$ is the set of pages referenced in the most recent $\Delta$ time units:

$$
W(t, \Delta) = \{ \text{pages } p \mid p \text{ was referenced in } (t - \Delta, t) \}
$$

The OS monitors each process's working set size $|W(t, \Delta)|$ and:

- If $\sum_i |W_i| > \text{Total RAM frames}$ → the system is about to thrash.
- The OS **suspends one or more processes**, reducing the degree of multiprogramming until $\sum_i |W_i| \leq \text{Total RAM}$.

**Prevention Strategy (1 mark):** Use the working set model as a feedback controller — suspend/resume processes dynamically to keep total working sets within physical memory limits.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Virtual Memory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in EAT Problems**: Mixing ms with ns (forgetting to convert $T_{PF}$ in ms to ns by multiplying by $10^6$). Always normalize units first.
> 2. **Wrong Formula Application**: Using $EAT = (1-p) T_{MEM} + p T_{PF}$ when a TLB is also present — use the combined formula instead.
> 3. **Page Replacement Trace Errors**: Off-by-one in tracing FIFO queues (forgetting to update the queue pointer after replacement). Use a clean table format.
> 4. **Confusing Belady's Anomaly**: Claiming "FIFO always gives fewer faults with more frames" — this is FALSE; counterexample required.
> 5. **Skipping the Valid Bit / Dirty Bit Discussion**: Examiners specifically want both bits explained in page-table entry questions.
> 6. **Ignoring I/O Cost**: Page fault service time ($T_{PF}$) typically includes **disk seek + rotational latency + transfer time** — students often compute only one component.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Checklist — Virtual Memory**
>
> ✅ Virtual Memory decouples **logical** (programmer-visible) from **physical** (hardware-real) address spaces.
>
> ✅ Standard page size = **4 KB** (but know 8 KB / 16 KB cases).
>
> ✅ **MMU** does the runtime translation; **Page Table** stores the mappings.
>
> ✅ **TLB** reduces EAT — formula is $EAT = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot T_{MEM}$.
>
> ✅ **Page Fault Service Time** $T_{PF}$ ≈ 1–10 ms (≈ $10^6$ to $10^7$ times slower than RAM access).
>
> ✅ **Belady's Anomaly** = FIFO gives MORE page faults with MORE frames (counterintuitive).
>
> ✅ **LRU never suffers Belady's Anomaly** — it is a stack algorithm.
>
> ✅ **Optimal Algorithm** gives the theoretical minimum faults (not implementable).
>
> ✅ **Thrashing** = CPU utilization crashes due to excessive swapping — fix by reducing multiprogramming degree.
>
> ✅ **Working Set** = pages referenced in last $\Delta$ time units; controls degree of multiprogramming.
>
> ✅ **Page Table Entry** typically contains: **Frame Number + Valid Bit + Dirty Bit + Protection Bits (Read/Write/Execute) + Reference Bit**.
>
> ✅ **Demand Paging** = pages are loaded only when a page fault occurs (lazy loading).
>
> ✅ **Pre-paging** = OS pre-loads anticipated pages to reduce initial page faults.
>
> ✅ **Internal Fragmentation** = unused space inside the last page of a process; **External Fragmentation** = not an issue in paging (it occurs in segmentation).
